q: Resolution Waterfall in Projections checks files in which order?
options: LIV → NAU → HIS → ARC
NAU → LIV → HIS → ARC
HIS first
ARC first
tip: Projections use the same Resolution Waterfall as SEE — they check NAU (Unauthorized) first (so pending drafts surface), then LIV (Live — current authorized), then HIS (History), then ARC (Archive). This ensures users always see the most relevant in-flight state when opening a record.

q: What does the Diff Engine do?
options: Compresses files
Highlights visual differences between draft vs live or two HIS versions
Translates languages
Audits SQL
tip: The Diff Engine in Projections highlights VISUAL DIFFERENCES between a draft and the live baseline — at AUTH time it shows the Authoriser exactly what changed. It can also compare two historical versions for audit purposes. This makes 4-Eyes review fast and accurate — the Authoriser doesn't have to compare 200 fields manually.

q: A simplified CUSTOMER~SIMPLE projection shows…
options: All 200+ fields
Only 5 fields for a teller
No fields
Random fields
tip: A CUSTOMER~SIMPLE Projection might show only 5 fields a teller needs — Name, Account, Balance, Phone, Notes — instead of the 200+ fields on the full CUSTOMER Table. This 'Targeted Subset View' approach reduces clutter, accelerates training, and prevents tellers from accidentally editing fields they shouldn't.

q: If a projection ID matches the table name exactly (e.g. ACCOUNT), it…
options: Acts as the Master View rendering all default fields
Fails to load
Is hidden
Acts as simulation
tip: If a Projection's ID exactly matches the Table name (e.g. ACCOUNT — no version suffix), it acts as the MASTER VIEW — rendering all default fields. Variants like ACCOUNT,CORP or ACCOUNT~TELLER are role-specific subsets. The Master View is the fallback when no role-specific Projection is invoked.

q: NOINPUT field property means…
options: Hidden
Read-only
Required
Encrypted
tip: NOINPUT is a Field Property Override in a Projection: it makes the field READ-ONLY in this specific Projection. The user can SEE the field but can't edit it. The underlying DDD definition is unchanged — other Projections on the same Table can still write to it.

q: NOCHANGE means…
options: Required
Immutable after creation
Hidden
Optional
tip: NOCHANGE makes a field IMMUTABLE AFTER CREATION. You can set it ONCE when creating the record; after that, no one can change it through this Projection. Useful for fields like Customer ID, Account Opening Date, or any 'set at birth' attribute that shouldn't drift over time.

q: HIDDEN means…
options: Used for background logic but removed from the UI
Encrypted
Skipped from DB
Visible only to managers
tip: HIDDEN means the field is used in BACKGROUND LOGIC (defaulting, validation, plugins) but is REMOVED from the user-visible UI. The user doesn't see it; the system still uses it. Useful for technical fields like internal flags that drive behavior without needing user input.

q: Field Property Overrides can do which of the following?
options: Make an optional field MANDATORY in a specific projection
Delete the field
Create the field globally
Lock the database
tip: Field Property Overrides in a Projection let you constrain fields per role/scenario: make an optional DDD field MANDATORY in this Projection; make it NOINPUT (read-only); make it NOCHANGE (immutable after creation); make it HIDDEN (invisible). One Table, many Projections — each with its own constraints.

q: Composite Layouts in projections support…
options: Only single-column forms
Collapsible Sections/Clusters and Associated Projections (Tabs)
Only text
No grouping
tip: Composite Layouts in Projections support modern UX: collapsible SECTIONS/CLUSTERS (group related fields together with a header), Associated Projections that split big tasks into TABS (e.g. General / Interest / Collateral on a mortgage form), and tooltips. This avoids overwhelming users with hundreds of flat fields.

q: What is the purpose of Associated Projections?
options: Backup
Split massive products (e.g. mortgages) into navigable logical Tabs
Hide fields
Send emails
tip: Associated Projections split a massive product (like a mortgage with 200 fields) into navigable logical TABS — General, Interest, Collateral, Documents, etc. Each tab is its own Associated Projection. Users move through them in sequence or as needed. Much easier than one giant scrolling form.

q: Suppress See Mode does what?
options: Hides non-participating attributes from the payload
Hides the whole projection
Suppresses validation
Disables See
tip: Suppress See Mode is a Projection setting that mathematically HIDES all non-participating or non-editable attributes from the payload — keeping the UI focused. So if a Projection is configured to view only certain fields, the irrelevant ones are stripped from the response entirely. Reduces clutter and load size.

q: Automated Defaulting can use which sources?
options: Fixed values, linked records, system vars (TODAY, USER, COMPANY)
Random
Customer email
User location
tip: Automated Defaulting in Projections pre-fills field values without user typing. Sources: FIXED values (e.g. country=PT), LINKED records (fetch a value from a related Table when an ID is entered), or SYSTEM VARIABLES like TODAY (current bank date), USER (current user ID), COMPANY (current company). Saves time and reduces errors.

q: What are Hot Fields?
options: Pre-rendered fields
ON.CHANGE fields that trigger validation/defaulting on tab-out
Hardware sensors
Audit fields
tip: Hot Fields trigger logic when the user changes a field and tabs out (the onBlur event). The associated plugin runs immediately — for validation (e.g. check ID against a registry) or auto-population (e.g. pull the customer's name when their ID is entered). Instant feedback before form submission.

q: Pre/Post hooks in projections are written in…
options: Assembly
Java/Groovy scripts
SQL only
HTML only
tip: Pre/Post hooks in Projections execute custom JAVA or GROOVY scripts — fragments of code attached to specific lifecycle events. Pre-hooks run BEFORE the operation commits (validation, external API calls); post-hooks run AFTER successful commit (notifications, cascading updates). The scripts are registered as Plugins.

q: The AUTH.REQUIRED flag does what?
options: Hides authorization
If YES, saves data as unauthorized draft (INAU), enforcing 4-eyes
Skips audit
Locks the system
tip: AUTH.REQUIRED is a Projection flag. When YES, INPUT writes the record to NAU as UNAU (Unauthorized) — requiring a second user's AUTH approval before it becomes LIVE. This is what implements the 4-Eyes Principle at the Projection level. Setting NO bypasses 4-Eyes (use sparingly for low-risk operations).

q: What does INAU mean?
options: Initial Audit
Unauthorized record indicator (Input Not Auth)
Inverted
Internal
tip: INAU is the visible MARKER that a LIV record has a pending UNAU change in NAU — visible in Enquiries and on Projections. So users see 'this record is live, but a draft amendment is awaiting authorization'. The Resolution Waterfall and Virtual Data Merging surface this flag automatically.

q: Projections forbid direct API writes — why?
options: Performance
To enforce business rules and the 4-eyes principle
Storage
User comfort
tip: Projections FORBID direct API writes to the database. All writes pass through a registered PROJECTION.DEF, ensuring rules (validation, plugins, AUTH.REQUIRED, field overrides) are enforced. This is a foundational architectural rule — bypassing it would defeat every control the system provides.

q: What is the practical example of CUSTOMER,CORP projection?
options: A version of the CUSTOMER projection for corporate clients
A new table
An external API
A holiday calendar
tip: CUSTOMER,CORP is a comma-suffix Projection (or 'version') of the CUSTOMER Table — typically a corporate-customer variant showing fields relevant to companies (TAX.ID, INCORPORATION.DATE, DIRECTORS, etc.) rather than individuals. Same underlying CUSTOMER Table; tailored Projection for corporate workflows.

q: Projections operate as a…
options: Reporting tool
Presentation & Logic Configuration Engine
File server
HR module
tip: Projections act as the system's Presentation & Logic CONFIGURATION ENGINE — controlling presentation (UI layout, field properties), validation hooks (pre/post programs, hot fields), and governance (AUTH.REQUIRED, audit triggers). They translate the underlying Table definition into role-aware, workflow-aware user-facing experiences.

q: What does Server-Driven UI prevent the need for?
options: Hard-coded frontend forms / client redeployment
Backups
Validations
Auth
tip: Server-Driven UI (SDUI) eliminates the need for HARD-CODED FRONTEND FORMS or client redeployment when forms change. The server defines the form (in a Projection); the client renders whatever schema is returned. Edit the Projection; users see the new form immediately on next request.

q: What does the Resolution Waterfall in Projections guarantee?
options: The most relevant active draft is shown
Random data
Always LIV
Only HIS
tip: The Resolution Waterfall in Projections guarantees users always see the MOST RELEVANT state: NAU drafts surface first (so users continue their in-flight work), then LIV (current authorized), then HIS (history), then ARC (archive). Without this priority, users might see stale LIV when they had a pending draft.

q: True or False: Projections can override DDD field rules.
options: True — e.g. making optional fields MANDATORY in a specific projection
False — DDD is immutable
Only for INT
Only for FIN
tip: Projections CAN override DDD field rules — that's the whole point of Field Property Overrides. The DDD might say a field is optional; the Projection can make it MANDATORY for THIS specific role/scenario. Or NOINPUT (read-only here). Or HIDDEN. The DDD is the underlying truth; Projections are the per-context restrictions.

q: Projections render forms via…
options: Hard-coded HTML
Dynamic JSON schemas returned by the backend
PDF only
Excel only
tip: Projections deliver dynamic JSON schemas describing the form — fields, labels, layout, validation rules. The frontend renders the schema. Admins editing the Projection (via config) instantly change what users see, without redeploying any client code. This is Server-Driven UI in action.

q: How are loan-origination flows organized in projections?
options: One huge form
Associated Projections as Tabs (General, Interest, Collateral)
Multiple windows
Random sequence
tip: A loan origination flow with 200+ fields shouldn't be one massive form. Use Associated Projections — split into Tabs like General / Interest / Repayment / Collateral / Documents. Each tab is its own Associated Projection. Users navigate intuitively; cognitive load is manageable.

q: Visual Diff between draft and baseline is used during…
options: Authorization and auditing
Login
Logout
Backups
tip: The Diff Engine (visual diffs between draft and baseline) is most useful during AUTH — the Authoriser sees exactly what fields the Initiator changed, accelerating review and accuracy. It's also used in audits — comparing two historical versions to understand evolution.

q: WYSIWYG drag-and-drop designer is used in…
options: Projections for composing layouts
Audit logs
Backup tools
Network setup
tip: WYSIWYG drag-and-drop designer is the tool admins use to compose Projection layouts — drag fields onto the canvas, group them into Sections, arrange Tabs (Associated Projections). No coding required. The output is a Projection configuration; the runtime applies SDUI.

q: What does Defaulting from TODAY do?
options: Sets a date field to the current bank date
Sets the user
Sets currency
Sets balance
tip: Defaulting from TODAY in a Projection pre-fills a date field with the current Bank Date (from the DATES Table). So new transactions get today's date automatically. Other defaulting variables: USER (the logged-in user's ID), COMPANY (the current company code).

q: Hot Fields trigger logic at which moment?
options: On page load
ON.CHANGE / onBlur — when a user tabs out
Only on submit
Never
tip: Hot Fields trigger on the onBlur event — the moment the user tabs/clicks out of the field. The associated plugin runs immediately, performing validation or auto-population. This is INSTANT feedback before the user submits the form.

q: Pre-hooks run…
options: Before the operation commits
After the operation commits
Always at midnight
Only on errors
tip: Pre-hooks fire BEFORE the operation commits (INPUT, AUTH, etc.). They can perform cross-field validations or synchronously call external APIs (like Credit Check, KYC). If a pre-hook rejects the operation, nothing is written. They're the gate keepers — synchronous, blocking, run-before-commit.

q: Post-hooks run…
options: Before commit
After successful commit (notifications, events, cascades)
On click
On login
tip: Post-hooks fire AFTER an operation completes successfully. They handle downstream side-effects — sending notifications, publishing events, cascading updates to other Tables. Post-hooks should be fire-and-forget where possible (don't block the user) and should only react to successful operations.

q: Setting AUTH.REQUIRED=NO would…
options: Bypass the 4-eyes principle (where allowed)
Crash the system
Lock the projection
Hide the form
tip: AUTH.REQUIRED=NO bypasses the 4-Eyes Principle — INPUT writes directly to LIV without needing a second user's AUTH. Use sparingly, for low-risk operations only. The audit fields INPUTTER/AUTHORISER will still record the user; but the SAME user can both create and commit. Risky for sensitive transactions.

q: What can a CUSTOMER~SIMPLE projection be used for?
options: A teller-focused subset view of CUSTOMER
Backups
A new table
A SQL query
tip: A teller-focused subset Projection (CUSTOMER~SIMPLE) shows only the fields tellers need — Name, Phone, Balance, Notes — instead of the full 200-field CUSTOMER Table. Faster to fill, less risk of incidental edits to unrelated fields, much better UX for high-volume desk work.

q: Targeted Subset Views avoid which problem?
options: Showing 200+ fields to a user who only needs 5
File system corruption
Auth bypass
Network latency
tip: Targeted Subset Views prevent overwhelming users with HUNDREDS of irrelevant fields. A teller doesn't need to see Tax ID, Incorporation Date, or Director List. A subset Projection (5-10 fields) keeps focus, reduces errors, and makes training easier.

q: What is the difference between MANDATORY and NOINPUT overrides?
options: MANDATORY forces input; NOINPUT makes read-only
They are the same
NOINPUT forces input
MANDATORY hides the field
tip: MANDATORY: the user MUST fill the field; otherwise the operation fails. NOINPUT: the user CANNOT edit the field — it's read-only. Both override the underlying DDD definition. They're opposites: MANDATORY forces input; NOINPUT prevents input.

q: How do projections enforce authorization workflows?
options: Via the AUTH.REQUIRED flag
Via PROGRAM registry
Via HIS file
Via SIM
tip: AUTH.REQUIRED is a Projection-level flag that controls whether the 4-Eyes Principle applies. AUTH.REQUIRED=YES forces records into NAU as UNAU pending second-user approval; =NO writes directly to LIV. The flag is set per Projection, allowing fine-grained control over which workflows require approval.

q: Why do projections support pre/post hooks?
options: For cross-field validation, external API calls, post-commit notifications
UI styling
Database backup
Holiday planning
tip: Pre/Post hooks let you inject custom business logic at lifecycle moments — cross-field validation that's too complex for DDD, synchronous external API calls (Credit Check, KYC), post-commit notifications (Kafka, email), cascading updates (when X happens, also update Y). Plugins are the engine; Projections are the gateway that invokes them.

q: True or False: A projection writes directly to the database bypassing the framework.
options: True
False — all writes go through the projection framework with rules enforced
Sometimes
Only at night
tip: A Projection NEVER writes directly to the database — all writes pass through the Projection framework, which enforces validation, plugin hooks, AUTH.REQUIRED, audit field maintenance, and field property overrides. This rule is architectural: no shortcuts, no exceptions, no direct SQL.

q: What kind of differences does the Diff Engine compare?
options: Draft vs live, or two historical versions
Two databases
Two networks
Two users
tip: The Diff Engine compares: (1) draft (NAU) vs live baseline (LIV) — most common, used during AUTH; or (2) two HISTORICAL versions (HIS;n vs HIS;m) — used during audits. Both produce a field-level visual diff showing what changed.

q: Why is SDUI advantageous for admins?
options: They can change UI by editing config — no client redeploy
They can edit hardware
They reduce CPU
They eliminate users
tip: Server-Driven UI is hugely advantageous for admins: they can change the UI by editing CONFIG (Projection definitions in the system), and the next request from any client sees the new form. No client redeploy. No app store updates. No 'please restart your browser'. Pure config-driven UX.

q: Field-level dropdowns from virtual tables are surfaced via…
options: Projections + DDD virtual-table linkage
Hardcoded HTML
User typing only
Backend logs
tip: Field-level dropdowns from VIRTUAL.TABLES happen via the Projection + DDD integration. DDD links a field to entries in VIRTUAL.TABLES (e.g. STATUS|ACTIVE, STATUS|INACTIVE); the Projection renders these as a dropdown auto-populated with those options. The system also validates input against the list. All from metadata.

q: A teller's onboarding form is best modeled with which projection style?
options: A targeted subset projection (e.g. CUSTOMER~SIMPLE)
Full Master View
SIM file
DEL file
tip: A teller's onboarding form is best a targeted subset Projection (e.g. CUSTOMER~SIMPLE) showing 5-10 essential fields. The full Master View would overwhelm with 200+ fields. A subset Projection keeps the workflow fast, error-free, and trainable in minutes rather than hours.

q: What is a Plugin in CBS?
options: A USB device plugged into branch terminals to extend their connectivity options
A programmable logic unit invoked by Screen for validations/calculations/business rules/integrations
A UI theme that customises the visual presentation of operator screens at branch terminals
A reporting tool that generates supplementary outputs alongside core banking reports
tip: Plugin = programmable logic unit (jBase or Java) invoked by Screen to perform complex validations, calculations, business rules, or external integrations.

jBase or Java
Python only

tip: Plugins are written in jBase or Java.

Only at startup
Only on hardware events
Random

options: Plugins allow extensibility without altering the core or DB schema
Vendor charges more
Plugins are mandatory
Vendor code is missing
tip: Plugins introduce custom logic without modifying core vendor code or the physical DB schema.

options: Complex validations, financial calcs, localized rules, synchronous external integrations
Only logging
Only printing
Backup

tip: Plugins attach to projections as Pre/Post programs (hooks).

After commit

q: Post-hooks fire…
options: After an operation completes successfully
Before commit

Only on login
tip: Post-hooks handle notifications, events, and cascading updates after a successful operation.

options: PROGRAM registry
FILE registry
USER table
HIS file

options: VALIDATION.PRGM

options: To guarantee plugin and field rules enforce before reaching the DB
To slow performance
To prevent backups
To avoid logs
tip: Guarantees rules (MANDATORY, NOINPUT) and plugin validations are enforced before any write.

q: Projections are described as the orchestrator; plugins are the…
options: Executor
Reader

Backup

q: Before authorizing a high-value international wire to a new beneficiary, the system must call an external sanctions-screening API and reject the transfer if the result is negative. Where does this logic belong?
options: A pre-hook plugin synchronously calling the API before commit
A post-hook plugin firing after commit
A manual phone call
Daily batch reconciliation
tip: Pre-hook plugins run BEFORE the operation commits. A pre-hook can synchronously call an external sanctions API and reject the transfer if the response is negative — preventing bad data from ever reaching NAU/LIV. Post-hooks fire after commit, which would be too late to block the transaction.

q: A post-hook plugin might…
options: Send notifications, publish events, cascade updates
Run validation
Block input
Delete HIS
tip: Post-hooks handle downstream tasks like notifications, events, and cascades.

q: Hot Fields validating an ID against a Government Registry uses…
options: Plugin logic triggered on onBlur
Email
HIS
SIM
tip: Hot Field plugin can verify an ID against an external Government Registry instantly.

q: True or False: Plugins can replace core vendor code.
options: True
False — they extend without modifying core vendor code
Only on weekends
Only with admin override
tip: Plugins extend functionality without modifying the core vendor code.

options: Projections (gateway) + Plugins (executor)
Tables + Audit
HIS + DEL
Customers + Users
tip: Projections gateway when/where logic runs; plugins execute the logic.

options: Form open, data entry/submission, authorization
Only at login

A backup
A plugin

Hardcoded API
Email engine

User-editable
Random
Always disabled
tip: Fixed Selection is a hardcoded background filter automatically applied to limit the dataset.

Hardcoded
SIM-only

options: LK
EQ
NE
RG

Merging two databases
Combining tables physically
Encrypting files
tip: Enquiries auto-merge LIV/NAU/HIS so users see the most relevant data and unauthorized rows are flagged.

q: In Enquiries, an unauthorized pending change is flagged as…

DEAD

tip: Enquiry flags pending NAU rows as INAU (Unauthorised).

tip: Stages: Define Capability → Validate Capability → Enable Capability.

q: What does COMBINE.ACTIONS=YES do?
options: Instantly catalogs the product after successful validation, skipping the secondary prompt
Deletes the product
Locks the product
Sends email
tip: COMBINE.ACTIONS=YES auto-executes the cataloging step immediately after validation.

q: How are products version-controlled?
options: Composite key of PRODUCT.ID + EFFECTIVE.DATE
Random UUID
Filename
User ID
tip: Products and conditions are versioned by PRODUCT.ID + EFFECTIVE.DATE — supports future-dated changes.

q: Operations Officer Mei begins amending CONTRACT XB-4471 and is interrupted by a meeting before submitting. While she's away, her colleague Hassan tries to amend the same Contract. What happens, and why?
options: Hassan is allowed to edit — last write wins
System returns 'Record Locked' to Hassan because Mei's draft occupies NAU
Both edits merge automatically
The record is wiped
tip: NAU (Unauthorized) is a SINGLETON file — strictly one in-flight draft per record ID. As soon as Mei pressed Input/Hold, her draft occupies NAU for XB-4471. Hassan's attempt finds the slot taken and the system returns 'Record Locked' — protecting Mei's pending work from being overwritten. This is the kernel-level edit-locking that prevents concurrent-modification races.

q: A teller's PC crashes mid-input on a deposit slip after she pressed 'Hold'. She logs back in. What does she expect, and where was the data?
options: Data is gone — HOLD doesn't persist
The draft is in NAU with status HOLD, ready to resume
The draft was auto-promoted to LIV
Recovered from a printer queue
tip: HOLD intentionally saves an unvalidated draft into NAU with status HOLD. It survives crashes because it's persisted. On next login, the projection's Resolution Waterfall finds the draft in NAU first and surfaces it for resumption.

q: A junior officer enters a new customer record. The branch manager reviews and wants to make it live. Which standard operation does the manager use?
options: INPUT
HOLD
AUTH
REVERSE
tip: The 4-Eyes Principle requires a second user to approve. AUTH moves the UNAU record from NAU to LIV (and archives any prior LIV to HIS). INPUT was the junior officer's action — AUTH is the manager's job.

q: An FX dealer accidentally routes a £215,000 outgoing payment to the wrong correspondent bank yesterday. The payment is now in LIV (authorized and posted to the GL). What's the correct way to undo it?
options: DELETE on the LIV record
REVERSE on the LIV record — creates an RNAU pending second-user AUTH
Edit the LIV record directly via SQL
Manually re-enter a refund
tip: DELETE only works on NAU drafts (records not yet LIVE). Because this payment is already LIV (and posted), the correct operation is REVERSE — which creates an RNAU (Reversal Not Authorized) entry in NAU. A second user then AUTHs the reversal, which moves the record to HIS as REVE. The audit trail is preserved; the General Ledger is correctly reversed.

q: Regulators want evidence of a now-deleted customer draft. The draft was new (SEQ.NO=1) and canceled before authorization. Where does this evidence live?
options: It's gone — DELETE removes it
In DEL — forensic delete preserves it
In SIM
In ARC
tip: For brand-new (SEQ.NO=1) records, DELETE moves the draft from NAU to DEL specifically to preserve a forensic audit trail. This is the only purpose of the DEL file — letting auditors see data that was input but never became live.

q: A user deletes an AMENDMENT (SEQ.NO=3) draft from NAU before authorization. Where does the deleted data go, and why is this different from a SEQ.NO=1 deletion?
options: Moved to DEL — treated identically to a brand-new record deletion in every respect
Physically removed from NAU; nothing written to DEL — because the base record still exists in LIV/HIS
Moved to HIS as a new history version representing the cancelled amendment for audit
Moved to SIM since the cancelled amendment becomes a what-if scenario for future review
tip: For amendments (SEQ.NO > 1), the deletion is a 'cancel amendment' — the base record still safely exists in LIV and HIS, so no forensic trail is needed. The draft simply vanishes from NAU. DEL is only used when the entire record would otherwise be lost forever.

q: A bank wants to validate every customer's national ID against a government API the moment the user tabs out of the ID field — without waiting for form submit. Right mechanism?
options: A nightly batch job
A 'Hot Field' configured in the projection, linked to a plugin that calls the API on onBlur
A new physical table
Manual approval
tip: Hot Fields are configured directly in the projection and trigger plugin logic on the onBlur event. The plugin synchronously calls the external API. This gives instant feedback without modifying the core system or making the user wait for full submission.

q: An auditor demands that every new SME loan above €250K trigger a real-time call to an external credit-bureau API before the record can post to the General Ledger. Where do you implement this?
options: Inside the user's browser
A pre-hook plugin on the loan Projection, registered in VALIDATION.PRGM
A post-hook running after commit
Manual phone calls
tip: Pre-hook plugins fire BEFORE the operation commits. A synchronous credit-bureau call inside a pre-hook can reject the loan if the result is poor — preventing the record from ever reaching NAU/LIV. Post-hooks fire after commit, far too late to block. Browser-side checks are bypassable. The PRE-HOOK is the architecturally correct place.

q: The treasury team needs a real-time dashboard showing ALL trade-finance contracts over ¥75M, with each contract's customer name (which lives on CUSTOMER, not on the contract). Which framework should they use?
options: A Projection over the trade-finance Table
An Enquiry using Soft Links (Virtual Joins) to fetch customer name from CUSTOMER
A new physical join table with foreign keys
Manual Excel exports
tip: Projections are bound to ONE physical target Table and are for write operations. Enquiries traverse MULTIPLE Tables via Soft Links (Virtual Joins) — dynamically fetching related fields like customer name from CUSTOMER at display time. No physical foreign keys, no rigid schema. For multi-table read-only reporting, Enquiry is always the right tool.

q: A teller's UI shouldn't show all 200+ CUSTOMER fields — only 5 that matter for deposits. Cleanest solution?
options: Hide fields in CSS
Create a targeted subset projection like CUSTOMER~TELLER with just the 5 fields
Edit the physical CUSTOMER table
Buy a new product
tip: Targeted Subset Views are the recommended pattern: create a projection (e.g. CUSTOMER~TELLER) that exposes only the 5 relevant fields. The Master View (CUSTOMER) stays untouched for managers who need all fields. Same table — different lens per role.

q: A bank wants to add a custom local field MIDDLE.NAME to CUSTOMER. The vendor doesn't allow kernel changes. How is this possible?
options: Impossible without vendor
DDD's customization injection adds user-defined fields to TABLE.DEF — no kernel change
Stored in a separate flat file
Stored in a comment field
tip: DDD (Dynamic Data Dictionary) was designed exactly for this. Custom local fields are automatically injected into TABLE.DEF and instantly extend the business object. No DDL, no kernel recompilation, no vendor code change — that's the whole point of the metadata layer.

q: A bank wants a display-only 'FULL.ADDRESS' field that's just CITY + ', ' + COUNTRY — built from existing stored fields at view time, never persisted (so it's always in sync). What System Type is best?
options: D (Data — stored on disk)
I (I-Descriptor — computed at runtime, never persisted)
J (Join — fetched from a related table)
K
tip: I-Descriptor fields (Type I) are computed in memory at runtime — never written to disk. They're perfect for derived values like 'FULL.ADDRESS' that should always reflect current values of the underlying stored fields. Type D would store it redundantly (risk of drift); Type J fetches from a related Table (this is one record). Type I is the right answer for same-record derivations.

q: On the ACCOUNT screen, you want to display the customer's name (stored on CUSTOMER, not ACCOUNT). Most efficient System Type?
options: D — duplicate the data
I — compute it
J — dynamic join to CUSTOMER via the foreign key
Hardcode it
tip: J (Join) fields fetch data from a related table at display time via a foreign key. No data duplication, no stale copies, and no physical FK needed at the DB level.

q: A risk admin needs to change a field's validation from 'Numeric only' to 'Alphanumeric'. The change must apply immediately. Does the system need to restart?
options: Yes — full reboot
No — DDD's validation engine is hot-swappable; the update takes effect on the next input
Yes — only at month-end
Only the database needs to restart
tip: The Hot-Swappable Validation Engine binds Field.type to pre-compiled validation routines. Changing the field's type in the dictionary applies instantly — subsequent inputs use the new rule without any restart or recompilation.

q: The product team wants to launch a new 'Eco-Saver' deposit — identical to the existing 'Standard-Saver' but with a 0.25% rate bonus for green-certified customers. Fastest correct approach?
options: Re-create every component from scratch
Define a new PRODUCT with PARENT.PRODUCT = Standard-Saver and override only the interest condition
Edit Standard-Saver in place (risks affecting existing customers)
Manually adjust each contract
tip: PARENT.PRODUCT inheritance is built for this scenario. Set Eco-Saver's PARENT.PRODUCT = Standard-Saver and override ONLY the interest condition (the delta). All other attributes — fees, schedules, accounting — are inherited automatically. The bank avoids drift between similar products: Standard-Saver enhancements automatically benefit Eco-Saver too. Effective-dated versioning ensures existing Standard-Saver contracts aren't disrupted.

q: A bank wants a new fee structure to take effect on 2026-07-01 — configure now, activate automatically. Feature?
options: Manual cron job
EFFECTIVE.DATE versioning on PRODUCT — system auto-switches on the date
An IT ticket on July 1
The fee will activate manually
tip: Products are version-controlled by composite key PRODUCT.ID + EFFECTIVE.DATE. The bank can stage new conditions today with EFFECTIVE.DATE = 2026-07-01; the system automatically switches on that date — no weekend intervention required.

q: A developer accidentally configures a new product with two conflicting interest rules and no mandatory Accounting attribute. What protects customers from this misconfiguration?
options: Nothing — the product goes live immediately with whatever configuration the developer specified
The Validate Capability stage (PRODUCT.MANAGER) blocks products with conflicts or missing mandatories
Marketing team approval required before any product reaches origination APIs in the catalog
Manual review by every customer before they can be offered the new product at their branch
tip: The 3-Stage Lifecycle Engine (Define → Validate → Enable) includes an automated Quality Gate. PRODUCT.MANAGER recursively compiles the product, resolves inherited conditions, and refuses to publish to PRODUCT.CATALOG if conflicts exist or mandatory attributes are missing.

q: COUNTRY codes are the same across every country office of the bank. ACCOUNT balances must be strictly separated by branch. How is this efficiently modeled?
options: Both as CUS
COUNTRY as INT (globally shared), ACCOUNT as FIN (segregated per company/book)
Both as INT
Both as FIN
tip: Classification drives data segregation. INT = Installation: one shared copy across all companies (perfect for COUNTRY, LANGUAGE). FIN = Financial: strictly partitioned by lead company/book (essential for ACCOUNT, FUNDS.TRANSFER).

q: An auditor asks: 'Show me the CUSTOMER record exactly as it looked 6 months ago.' Which operation and file?
options: SEE on LIV
HISTORY.RESTORE / read the appropriate HIS suffix (e.g. ID;n)
DELETE from HIS
REVERSE the record
tip: HIS stores immutable historical versions, indexed by suffixes ID;1, ID;2, ID;3, etc. The auditor reads the right historical record, or uses HISTORY.RESTORE to reinstate it. Copy-on-Write at AUTH ensures every previous version is preserved.

q: A senior developer adds a tooltip to a field in the customer onboarding form. What needs to happen on end-user computers after the change?
options: A full app reinstall
A browser restart
Nothing — SDUI delivers the new schema dynamically on next request
Manual patch download
tip: Server-Driven UI (SDUI): the backend returns the form schema dynamically as JSON. Editing the projection updates the schema; clients receive it on the next request — no redeploy, no patching, no restart.

q: Two AUTH officers click 'Approve' on the same UNAU record at the same millisecond. What prevents data corruption?
options: Pure luck — whichever AUTH happens to commit first wins and the other one is silently dropped
NAU's singleton constraint and atomic AUTH operation — only one transition succeeds; the other gets 'record not found'
Both AUTH operations succeed silently and the record is committed twice with slightly different states
The system rolls back both AUTH operations and queues a manual review by the duty supervisor
tip: NAU is a singleton (1 record per ID). AUTH is atomic: it moves the NAU record to LIV and archives the prior LIV to HIS. Once the first AUTH completes, the NAU entry is gone — the second AUTH finds nothing to authorize and fails cleanly.

q: A risk officer needs stress-test scenarios for loan portfolios that don't impact real accounts or the GL. Which file space?
options: LIV
NAU
HIS
SIM
tip: SIM (Simulation) is dedicated to what-if data and is completely isolated from live and financial records. Anything written to SIM cannot trigger accounting entries — perfect for stress testing without risk.

q: A teller starts entering a USD 10,000 deposit but needs to verify something with the customer. She wants to save and resume later — WITHOUT triggering validation (the form isn't complete). Which operation?
options: INPUT
HOLD
VALIDATE
AUTH
tip: HOLD saves an unvalidated, incomplete draft into NAU with status HOLD. Unlike INPUT, it does NOT run validation rules, so partial data is fine. She can return later and use INPUT to validate and commit. HOLD = pause; INPUT = commit.

q: After a successful AUTH on an amendment to CUSTOMER C123, where can you find the version that existed BEFORE this amendment?
options: Permanently deleted
HIS file with the next sequential suffix (e.g. C123;n) — Copy-on-Write archived it during AUTH
Still in NAU
Stuck in LIV
tip: Copy-on-Write: when AUTH commits an amendment, the OLD LIV record is automatically moved to HIS with the next sequential suffix. This is how the audit trail builds up over time — every prior version remains accessible via HIS.

q: A bank needs a 'Channel-Preference' dropdown on the CUSTOMER projection with values like Mobile, Branch, Online, ATM. Should they create a new physical Table for these four options?
options: Yes — every reference list needs its own physical Table for query-performance reasons
No — store the four entries inside VIRTUAL.TABLES (e.g. CHANNEL.PREF|MOBILE) with the DDD auto-generating the dropdown
Hardcode the four options directly into the HTML/React code of the Customer screen
Store them as comma-separated free text in a customer-preferences notes field on the record
tip: Creating physical Tables for every tiny static list would bloat the schema. VIRTUAL.TABLES centralizes ALL such reference lists in one physical Table, distinguished by compound keys like CHANNEL.PREF|MOBILE, CHANNEL.PREF|BRANCH, etc. The DDD's link to virtual tables auto-generates the dropdown UI and validates inputs against the list — zero schema bloat, zero code.

q: A junior teller should VIEW account info but never modify it. Which projection-level configuration achieves this without changing the underlying DDD?
options: Delete the fields
Mark the fields as NOINPUT in the teller's projection (read-only in this view)
Hide them via CSS
Restrict the database
tip: NOINPUT makes a field read-only in that specific projection while leaving the DDD definition untouched. Other roles using a different projection can still write to the same fields. Field Property Overrides give per-role behavior from one underlying schema.

q: The CSO wants a dashboard with a pie chart of accounts by branch, plus a tree view of GL accounts. Framework?
options: Projections
Plugins
Enquiries — they render SVG-based charts and Tree Views
HIS browser
tip: Enquiries are designed for data presentation: grids, Tree Views (great for GLs), and SVG-based Bar/Pie/Line charts. Projections handle data ENTRY. Reporting and dashboards = Enquiries.

q: A loan officer focuses on the 'Debit Account' field, and you want a side panel showing that account's balance to appear automatically. Feature?
options: Manual button click
Context-Sensitive Enquiries — the frontend broadcasts focus, triggering an enquiry side panel
CSS hover effect
A nightly batch
tip: Context-Sensitive Enquiries listen for focus events and automatically launch relevant side panels with contextual data. This actively drives the workflow rather than waiting for explicit clicks.

q: From an Enquiry list of pending loan approvals, you want users to click a row and immediately open the approval projection pre-filled with that loan's data. Mechanism?
options: Email link
Hypermedia Drill-Down / Fast Path — passes the @ID into the target Projection
Manual ID re-entry
A new window opens blank
tip: Fast Path is the synergy between Enquiry and Projection: rows in an Enquiry grid are actionable. Clicking can launch a target Projection (e.g. LOAN|AUTH) with the @ID auto-passed, opening a pre-filled form.

q: A compliance team needs every successful loan disbursement to publish an event to a downstream Kafka topic for audit. Where to add this?
options: Pre-hook plugin (would block writes)
Post-hook plugin — runs after successful commit, ideal for events/notifications/cascades
Inside the DDD
On the user's PC
tip: Post-hooks fire after successful commit. They're right for downstream side-effects: publishing events, notifications, cascades. Pre-hooks would block waiting for the message to send.

q: A reporting engineer needs IF/THEN coloring in a grid column: red if balance < 0, green otherwise. Right tool?
options: A hardcoded JS file
The Enquiry Designer's visual Expression Builder
A new projection
SIM
tip: The Enquiry Designer includes a visual Expression Builder where admins write conditional formatting logic like IF AMOUNT > 1000 THEN 'RED' ELSE 'GREEN'. The WYSIWYG canvas plus Expression Builder lets non-developers build complex views.

q: A user changes the 'Principal' field on a loan form. The 'Monthly Payment' field below should recalculate immediately — before submit. Feature?
options: A nightly batch
A 'Hot Field' (ON.CHANGE) on Principal triggering recalculation
Manual refresh
CSS animation
tip: Hot Fields trigger on the onBlur/change event. The associated plugin recalculates and updates Monthly Payment instantly — without form submission.

q: During quarter-end the system must reject new transactions on weekends. What table controls this, and which other table does it consult?
options: DATES table, consulting HOLIDAY
CUSTOMER, consulting USER
ACCOUNT, consulting CATEGORY
CURRENCY, consulting BRANCH
tip: DATES controls the Bank Date and works with HOLIDAY to determine if a given day is a working day. If today is a holiday/weekend per HOLIDAY, DATES enforces the right behavior — including blocking certain transactions and skipping interest accrual.

q: A wire transfer in Korean Won (zero decimals) must be rejected when 0.5 KRW is entered, while the same field accepts 49.95 in USD. Where is this decimal rule configured?
options: In the Projection
In the CURRENCY Table — defines ISO codes and decimal places per currency
In a Plugin
Hardcoded in HTML
tip: CURRENCY defines per-currency decimal rules: KRW = 0 decimals, USD = 2 decimals, BHD/KWD = 3 decimals. Any amount Field validated against CURRENCY automatically respects the right rule — no need to hardcode 'reject decimals for KRW' in code. Change the rule in CURRENCY (highly unusual — these come from ISO 4217 standards), and the new behavior applies system-wide instantly.

q: A junior officer creates a new ACCOUNT record. The system reports 'Cannot execute — program not registered'. Likely cause?
options: The customer record is missing from the CUSTOMER application and must be created first
The PROGRAM Registry doesn't have an active record for the executable; system blocks unauthorized code
A network issue between the branch terminal and the central CBS instance is preventing execution
The data centre disk is full and the system is rejecting writes until space is freed
tip: The PROGRAM Registry strictly blocks any program lacking an active registration. This is a core security control — only authorized code runs. Fix: register the program rather than bypass the registry.

q: A user gets 'Record not found' when opening CUSTOMER C999. What does this mean about the record's existence?
options: It exists somewhere
Record exists in none of NAU, LIV, HIS, or ARC (entire Resolution Waterfall returned empty)
It exists only in LIV
It exists only in SIM
tip: SEE uses the Resolution Waterfall: NAU first, then LIV, HIS, ARC. 'Record not found' is returned only when ALL spaces are empty for that ID. So the record genuinely doesn't exist anywhere in the lifecycle.

q: The bank's COUNTRY table is updated (configured as 0-Auth INT). What happens to the previous LIV record?
options: It's lost forever
Written to HIS as a previous version while the new input goes directly to LIV
Buffered in NAU
Sent to DEL
tip: Under 0-Auth, the system bypasses NAU entirely. The OLD LIV record is written to HIS and the new input goes straight to LIV. Audit trail preserved without 4-eyes.

q: A teller can both create AND authorize the same transaction. Is this allowed by the 4-Eyes Principle?
options: Yes — fewer steps is better
No — 4-Eyes requires a SECOND, different user to AUTH; the original inputter cannot authorize their own record
Yes — at year-end only
Yes — for INT tables only
tip: The 4-Eyes Principle (enforced via AUTH.REQUIRED with the record in NAU as UNAU) explicitly requires a different second user to approve. The whole point is fraud and error prevention through separation of duties.

q: A construction-loan Arrangement has a Drawdown Schedule of 36 individual drawdown events (date, amount, status) inside each contract. Should each drawdown be a separate child-Table row?
options: Yes — one row per drawdown in a dedicated DRAWDOWN child table linked by foreign key
No — DDD's Non-1NF and Field Associations natively store the repeating drawdown block inside the single Arrangement Record
Store the entire drawdown schedule as JSON text inside a single TEXT field on the Arrangement
Don't store individual drawdowns — track only the cumulative drawdown total at any point in time
tip: DDD Non-1NF lets one Record hold repeating blocks natively. The 36 drawdowns live inside the parent Arrangement Record as multi-value Fields kept index-aligned via Field Association markers (<, -, >). No child Table, no joins, atomic updates. Adding a new drawdown at index 5 automatically shifts all associated fields (date[i], amount[i], status[i]) consistently.

q: A field on a projection is marked NOCHANGE. A user creates a new record and fills it in. Later, they try to edit the same field. What happens?
options: The edit succeeds
Blocked — NOCHANGE means immutable after CREATION; you can set it once but not modify later
Random behavior
The field disappears
tip: NOCHANGE = set at creation, frozen thereafter. Useful for fields like 'Customer ID' or 'Account Opening Date' that should be permanent. Different from NOINPUT (always read-only) — NOCHANGE allows the initial write.

q: A user notices a row in a customer Enquiry list is highlighted as 'INAU'. What does that tell them?
options: The record is deleted
An unauthorized modification draft is pending in NAU on top of the LIV record — second-user approval required
The record has never existed
The customer was rejected
tip: Virtual Data Merging in Enquiries combines LIV + NAU + HIS. When a LIV record has a pending NAU draft, the row is flagged INAU to alert the viewer that a pending change exists.

q: A regulator wants assurance that no part of the system can bypass the 4-Eyes Principle. Which architectural feature gives this guarantee?
options: A trust-the-developers policy mandating code review for any direct database write attempts
Strict Gateway Enforcement: all writes must pass through a registered PROJECTION.DEF, which enforces AUTH.REQUIRED
Network-level firewall rules blocking write operations from non-approved IP addresses inside the cluster
Manual review by compliance staff of every transaction before it is allowed to commit to the database
tip: Direct DB writes are forbidden. Every mutation goes through a registered PROJECTION.DEF, which enforces AUTH.REQUIRED, plugin validations, and field properties. This architectural rule makes the 4-Eyes Principle non-bypassable by code.

q: A bank's marketing team launches a Summer-Promotion deposit product that must auto-deactivate on August 31. Best feature to use?
options: Manually delete the product on August 31
EFFECTIVE.DATE versioning — schedule a new (deactivation) version effective Sep 1; system switches automatically
Send a calendar reminder
Manual code change at year-end
tip: Products are versioned by composite key PRODUCT.ID + EFFECTIVE.DATE. Stage the Summer-Promotion version with effective date today, and a deactivation/replacement version effective Sep 1. The system switches automatically on Sep 1 — no weekend ops, no calendar reminders, no risk of forgetting. This is the same pattern used for scheduled fee changes, rate changes, and regulatory transitions.

q: The product manager wants product validation to flow directly into catalog publication without an extra approval click — but only when validation succeeds. Which flag?
options: AUTH.REQUIRED
COMBINE.ACTIONS = YES
HIDDEN
REVE
tip: COMBINE.ACTIONS = YES tells the 3-Stage Lifecycle Engine to auto-execute the cataloging (Enable Capability) step immediately after successful validation — no extra prompt.

q: A teller tries to HOLD a draft for ACCOUNT 5001, but the system blocks the action. Most likely cause?
options: NAU already contains an UNAU record for ACCOUNT 5001; NAU singleton constraint blocks writing HOLD on top
No specific reason — random transient failures occasionally affect HOLD attempts and a retry usually succeeds
The bank's data centre disk is full and the system is temporarily blocking new draft writes
The user's permission to write HOLD records has expired and needs to be renewed by an administrator
tip: NAU is a singleton (1 record per ID). If a UNAU draft already exists (awaiting authorization), saving a HOLD on the same ID would corrupt the pending committed record. The system blocks the HOLD to protect it.

q: A senior analyst wants to expose a runtime filter (e.g. 'AMOUNT > 5000') to end-users in a report. Selection type?
options: Fixed Selection — hardcoded
Dynamic Selection — exposed to user at runtime
Mandatory Selection — must be indexed
Validation Selection
tip: Fixed Selection is hardcoded (e.g. SECTOR = 1001 always). Dynamic Selection exposes a filter to users at runtime. Use Dynamic for user-facing filters; Fixed for invariants.

q: A new field added to a customer form via DDD must NOT impact existing production records on disk. What DDD characteristic ensures this?
options: A manual DBA migration applied during a scheduled maintenance window to add the field to existing rows
Logical-to-physical abstraction: new fields are positions in TABLE.DEF; existing physical records are unchanged
A full database reindex on the table being modified to ensure consistent access patterns post-change
Re-creating the table from scratch with the new schema and copying existing data into the new structure
tip: DDD decouples logical schema from physical storage. Adding a new field is a metadata change. Existing physical records remain untouched; the new field simply has no value until written. No migration, no downtime.

q: A consultant builds an Enquiry that scans 30 million transactions without using indexed fields as filters. What's likely to happen, and why?
options: Lightning-fast results because the unindexed scan triggers the in-memory query optimiser to take over
System enforces Mandatory Selection rules on Indexed Fields and refuses or warns — to prevent performance-killing full-table scans
The query returns random sample data instead of the full result set as a performance-protection measure
The query causes the entire bank to lose service until the operations team manually intervenes
tip: Performance Protection is built in: Mandatory Selection rules enforce filtering on Indexed Fields only. This prevents naive full-table scans on huge datasets. The consultant must redesign the query.

q: A consultant edits a projection's pre-hook plugin to add a new validation. Tellers across all branches need the change immediately. What do they need to do?
options: Reinstall the app on every teller terminal across the bank's branch network before close of business
Nothing — the new plugin is registered in PROGRAM and the projection is loaded on next request via SDUI
Restart their PC to clear the cache and pick up the new plugin code from the central server
Wait for the monthly patch cycle when the change will be pushed to all terminals together
tip: Combining SDUI (projections delivered dynamically) and the PROGRAM registry (plugins loaded on demand) means changes propagate instantly. Tellers' next form request returns the new schema with the updated plugin.

q: A teller authorizes a transaction created by herself from a different terminal earlier. Acceptable under 4-Eyes?
options: Yes — different terminals count
No — 4-Eyes requires a different USER ID, not a different terminal. INPUTTER and AUTHORISER audit fields must differ
Yes — if it's a small amount
Only with admin override
tip: 4-Eyes is enforced by USER ID, not by device. INPUTTER and AUTHORISER are captured in the audit block and must be different user IDs. Using two terminals doesn't satisfy the principle.

q: A loan officer wants to compare a draft amendment against the live version side-by-side, with field-level differences highlighted. Feature?
options: Manual line-by-line read
The Diff Engine in projections — highlights field-level changes between draft and baseline
SQL query
A printed report
tip: The Diff Engine is part of the projection framework. During authorization or review, it visually highlights exactly which fields changed between the unauthorized draft (NAU) and the live baseline (LIV). It can also diff between two HIS versions.

q: A fraud officer wants to enrich every customer onboarding submission by calling an external KYC API before commit. Where to add this?
options: Email after the fact
A pre-hook plugin on the CUSTOMER input projection — synchronous external call before commit
A nightly cron job
Manual phone call
tip: Pre-hook plugins are designed for synchronous external integrations (KYC, sanctions check, credit bureau). The hook can reject the operation if KYC fails — preventing bad data from reaching NAU.

q: After a SUCCESSFUL reverse-authorization (AUTH on an RNAU), where is the record ultimately stored?
options: Back in NAU
In HIS with status REVE — reversal is a terminal state
Back in LIV
Deleted permanently
tip: REVERSE moves LIV → NAU as RNAU. The second user's AUTH then moves the record from NAU to HIS with status REVE. The cancellation is now permanent and auditable. LIV no longer holds an active version.

q: A field needs to be visible to backend logic and used in defaulting, but NEVER shown to end users. Property?
options: NOINPUT
NOCHANGE
HIDDEN — used in background logic, removed from the UI
MANDATORY
tip: HIDDEN tells the projection to use the field in background logic (defaulting, validation, plugins) but strip it from the UI payload. Different from NOINPUT (visible but read-only) and NOCHANGE (visible, editable once).

q: A bank wants a single underlying CUSTOMER table but DIFFERENT forms for corporates vs individuals. Best pattern?
options: Two physical CUSTOMER tables
One CUSTOMER table with TWO projections — e.g. CUSTOMER,CORP and CUSTOMER,IND
Two separate databases
Hide fields with CSS
tip: Projections decouple UI from the underlying table. One CUSTOMER table can have a CORP projection and an IND projection — same physical data, different lenses, no duplication.

q: An admin wants to test a complex enquiry against live data WITHOUT compiling or saving the configuration. What helps?
options: Trust and pray
The Live Preview feature in the Enquiry Designer — runs unsaved config against live data
SIM file
Manual SQL
tip: Live Preview executes the unsaved configuration against live data and returns the first page of results immediately. This shortens the design cycle — admins iterate visually without compiling or deploying.

q: An officer wants the accounting team to receive a notification every time a high-value funds-transfer is authorized. Where?
options: Pre-hook (would block the commit)
Post-hook on the FUNDS.TRANSFER AUTH projection — fires after commit
Inside SIM
Manual phone calls
tip: Post-hooks run after a successful AUTH commit. They're the right place for notifications, event publication, and downstream cascades that should happen ONLY when the operation truly succeeded.

q: An auto-loan product would most likely sit under which PRODUCT.CLASS in the product hierarchy?
options: ACCOUNT
LENDING
DEPOSIT
CURRENCY
tip: PRODUCT.CLASS is the highest tier of the product hierarchy. LENDING is the class for loans (auto-loans, mortgages, personal loans, business loans). ACCOUNT class covers current/savings/checking products. DEPOSIT class covers fixed-term and notice deposits. Each class defines the set of permitted base Components (e.g. LENDING products MUST have an Interest Component and a Schedule Component; ACCOUNT products may not need a Schedule).

q: The PRODUCT.CATALOG is described as read-only and serving origination APIs. Why is the PRODUCT.BUILDER environment important?
options: A purely marketing-driven distinction with no operational consequence in production environments
Zero-Downtime Maintenance: staging changes in PRODUCT.BUILDER leaves the live PRODUCT.CATALOG intact, so customer journeys keep working during edits
To hide products under development from competitors who might monitor the bank's API responses
Just a draft folder for in-progress products with no architectural significance to the lifecycle
tip: Live products serve customer journeys 24/7. Editing them in place would risk inconsistency. PRODUCT.BUILDER sandboxes changes; the read-only PRODUCT.CATALOG keeps serving until the new version is officially published. Strict Data Isolation = Zero Downtime.

q: The bank wants to enhance the 'Charges' Product Attribute (e.g. add a new fee type) so it improves ALL existing products that use it. What architectural property makes this possible?
options: Each product needs a separate update applied individually by operations staff at change time
Modular Building-Block Assembly: enhancements to a shared Product Attribute instantly propagate to every product that uses it — no code duplication
Manual update per product by operations staff during scheduled product-maintenance windows
Hardcoded charges in each product definition that must be edited individually for every change
tip: Product Attributes (Interest, Charges, Schedules, Limits, Events) are reusable building blocks. Because functionality is encapsulated in the attribute, enhancing 'Charges' propagates to every product that uses it — a single change, system-wide benefit.

q: In Temenos terminology, what is the analogue of a CBS 'Table'?
options: Application
Version
Routine
Property
tip: A 'Table' in CBS is the logical business entity that holds records for one type of banking object (like CUSTOMER, ACCOUNT, or LIMIT) — it defines the data model, business rules, access controls, and lifecycle. In Temenos, the exact same concept is called an 'Application'. The vocabularies differ but the concept is identical. Knowing these mappings is essential when reading Temenos documentation or working in teams that use both systems.

q: In Temenos, what corresponds to a CBS 'Projection'?
options: Application
Version
Routine
Property
tip: A 'Projection' in CBS is the configuration that controls how data is displayed, entered, validated, and processed for a Table — fields shown, mandatory rules, default values, pre/post programs, authorization requirements. Temenos calls this exact same construct a 'Version'. Both let admins define multiple per-role views over the same underlying Table without duplicating data.

q: A CBS 'Plugin' is called what in Temenos?
options: Application
Version
Routine
Property
tip: A 'Plugin' in CBS is a programmable logic unit (written in jBase or Java) invoked at defined lifecycle hooks — for example, to validate input, call an external API, or run business calculations. Temenos calls this same construct a 'Routine'. Both attach to lifecycle moments like pre-input, post-authorize, or onBlur of a 'Hot Field'.

q: In Temenos, a 'Product Group' corresponds to what in CBS?
options: Product Class
Product Type
Product Attribute
Contract
tip: CBS organizes products in a three-tier hierarchy: Product Class (top — e.g. ACCOUNT, LENDING), Product Type (middle — e.g. PERSONAL.LOAN, MORTGAGE), and Product (the saleable item). Temenos calls the middle tier a 'Product Group'. Both group similar products together and define which Product Attributes (interest, charges, schedules) they share.

q: In Temenos, the 'Property class' is the analogue of which CBS term?
options: Product Class
Product Type
Product Attribute Class
Component
tip: A Product Attribute Class in CBS is a reusable set of characteristics/parameters/behaviors that can be applied across products — it standardizes how a feature (like an interest pattern) is defined and inherited. Temenos calls this a 'Property class'. The naming overlap is confusing: Temenos's lowercase 'Property' is the Attribute itself; 'Property class' is the Attribute Class.

q: Temenos calls it 'Product Line'. What's the CBS term?
options: Product Class
Product Type
Product Attribute
Product Builder
tip: CBS 'Product Class' is the highest tier of the product hierarchy (e.g. ACCOUNT, LENDING, DEPOSIT). It defines the absolute set of permissible base components and whether each is mandatory or optional. Temenos uses the term 'Product Line' for this same top tier. Below it sit Product Types and the individual saleable Products.

q: What is the CBS equivalent of Temenos 'ARRANGEMENT'?
options: Product
Contract
Component
Activity
tip: In CBS, a 'Contract' is the individual customer-specific agreement created from a Product blueprint (e.g. one specific 20-year mortgage for one specific borrower). It inherits properties from the Product but can override negotiated parameters like rate, margin, dates, or limits. Temenos calls this same instance an 'ARRANGEMENT'. The term ARRANGEMENT is also used by Backbase, which integrates with both systems.

q: Temenos 'PROPERTY' (Product Builder component) maps to which CBS term?
options: Product
Contract
Component
Activity
tip: In CBS, a 'Component' is a structural element of a product or contract — examples include Interest, Charge, Schedule, Limit, Payment, and Insurance. Each Component controls one specific aspect of contract behavior; a complete product is built by assembling Components. Temenos calls these same structural elements 'PROPERTY' (uppercase, in the Product Builder context).

q: In Temenos, the Dynamic Data Dictionary is implemented as which application?
options: TABLE.DEF
FIELD.DEF
STANDARD.SELECTION
PROGRAM
tip: The Dynamic Data Dictionary (DDD) is the metadata layer that defines the field structure, types, and validation rules for every Table — it lets banks change schemas without recompiling code. In Temenos, the DDD is implemented as the application 'STANDARD.SELECTION'. The two CBS records that underpin DDD are TABLE.DEF (the table's field map) and FIELD.DEF (per-field rules).

q: A 'Full Screen' projection in CBS corresponds to which Temenos concept?
options: VERSION,ONLINE
COMMA-VERSION
FULL.VIEW
OPEN.SCREEN
tip: A 'Full Screen' in CBS is a special projection identified by a comma (,) right after the Table name — e.g. CUSTOMER, — and it requires ZERO authorization levels, meaning the record is committed immediately upon input with no second-user approval. Temenos calls this same pattern a 'COMMA-VERSION'. It's used for low-risk maintenance where the 4-Eyes principle isn't required.

q: Temenos 'Property' (lowercase, attached to products) maps to which CBS term?
options: Component
Product Attribute
Plugin
Activity
tip: A 'Product Attribute' in CBS is a standardized, reusable business component you attach to a product — examples include Interest, Charges, and Term Amount. Temenos's lowercase 'Property' refers to this same idea. (Confusingly, uppercase 'PROPERTY' in Temenos's Product Builder is what CBS calls a 'Component'. Same source word, two different layers.)

q: What CBS term maps to Temenos 'Activity'?
options: Component
Product Attribute
Contract Operation
Override
tip: A 'Contract Operation' in CBS is the system mechanism that executes state changes and processing actions on a Contract — for example, applying a repayment, restructuring a loan, or accruing interest. Temenos calls this same mechanism an 'Activity'. Every change to a Contract must go through a defined Contract Operation/Activity rather than direct field editing — this guarantees audit trails and rule enforcement.

q: Temenos 'Activity class' maps to which CBS term?
options: Override Class
Contract Operation Class
Product Class
Product Type
tip: A 'Contract Operation Class' in CBS is a grouping/classification of similar Contract Operations within the contract processing engine — useful for permissions and management. Temenos calls this an 'Activity class'. Don't confuse it with OVERRIDE.CLASS (a security class for who can approve a soft-rule exception).

q: Temenos 'Design' (first lifecycle stage) maps to which CBS lifecycle stage?
options: DefineCapability
ValidateCapability
EnableCapability
Catalogued
tip: Products in CBS pass through a three-stage lifecycle: DefineCapability (draft/configure in a sandbox), ValidateCapability (compile and check rules), EnableCapability (publish to the live catalog). The first stage — DefineCapability — is called 'Design' in Temenos. This is where you create the draft product without affecting any live Contracts.

q: Temenos 'Proof' lifecycle stage corresponds to which CBS stage?
options: DefineCapability
ValidateCapability
EnableCapability
Catalogued
tip: The middle stage of the product lifecycle in CBS is 'ValidateCapability', where the system compiles the product definition, resolves all inherited parent conditions, and verifies that mandatory attributes and configuration rules are satisfied. Temenos calls this stage 'Proof'. The system blocks any product that fails this stage from reaching customers.

q: Temenos 'Publish' corresponds to which CBS lifecycle stage?
options: DefineCapability
ValidateCapability
EnableCapability
Validated
tip: EnableCapability is the final lifecycle stage in CBS where a validated product is activated by generating its runtime representation and publishing it to PRODUCT.CATALOG — making it available for origination APIs and live contracts. Temenos calls this stage 'Publish'. With COMBINE.ACTIONS=YES, EnableCapability runs automatically right after a successful validation.

q: The CBS status 'Validated' is called what in Temenos?
options: Designed
Proofed
Published
Catalogued
tip: 'Validated' is a product STATUS in CBS — it means the product passed all system validation checks (inheritance resolution, mandatory attribute verification, condition consistency) but has not yet been published to the runtime catalog. Temenos uses 'Proofed' for the same status.

q: The CBS status 'Catalogued' corresponds to what in Temenos?
options: Designed
Proofed
Published
Validated
tip: 'Catalogued' is the CBS product STATUS meaning the validated product has been successfully published to PRODUCT.CATALOG and is available for live contract origination. Temenos calls this status 'Published'. PRODUCT.CATALOG is the read-only runtime registry of usable products; PRODUCT.BUILDER is the sandbox you stage changes in.

q: Temenos 'Product Designer' corresponds to which CBS environment?
options: Product Catalog
Product Builder
Product Class
Product Factory
tip: PRODUCT.BUILDER is the sandbox configuration environment in CBS where administrators create and configure product drafts — defining attributes, inheritance, currencies, and conditions before submitting for validation. Temenos calls this same sandbox 'Product Designer'. Crucially, edits here don't affect the live PRODUCT.CATALOG, so existing Contracts keep running while you build the new product.

q: Which CBS component sits closest to Temenos 'AA' (Arrangement Architecture)?
options: Plugin
Product Factory
Enquiry
Override
tip: CBS's 'Product Factory' (sometimes called Product Architecture) is the component-based framework that defines products as compositions of reusable Components (Interest, Fees, Schedules, Limits, Events) and creates per-customer Contracts from them. Temenos calls this same architecture 'AA' (Arrangement Architecture) — sometimes also referred to as Product Builder/Designer. It's the engine that lets banks rapidly assemble new products without writing code.

q: A user moving from Temenos to CBS sees a 'VERSION' — what should she now call it?
options: Application
Projection
Routine
Property
tip: Temenos 'VERSION' = CBS 'Projection'. Both are role-specific configurations layered over a base Table/Application — controlling which fields appear, which are mandatory, default values, pre/post hooks, and whether authorization is required. So if a Temenos colleague refers to a 'CUSTOMER,CORP VERSION', the CBS equivalent terminology is the 'CUSTOMER,CORP Projection'.

q: A CBS architect explains 'Activities' to a Temenos colleague — what term should she use?
options: Properties
Activities (same term in both)
Versions
Routines
tip: The term 'Activity' is shared across both vocabularies — it refers to a defined business action performed on a Contract (e.g. accrue interest, apply payment, restructure). The mechanism that executes them is called 'Contract Operation' in CBS and 'Activity' (system-side) in Temenos — so the user-facing concept of 'an Activity' translates one-to-one.

q: In a project with both CBS and Temenos screens, the team must edit a 'Routine'. Which CBS object?
options: Table
Projection
Plugin
Enquiry
tip: Temenos 'Routine' = CBS 'Plugin'. Both are programmable logic units (jBase or Java) invoked from Projections/Versions to perform complex validations, financial calculations, or external integrations. The trigger points are the same too: pre-input hooks, post-authorize hooks, and onBlur Hot Field events.

q: Mapping the highest product hierarchy tier: CBS 'Product Class' = Temenos…
options: Product Group
Product Line
Property
Application
tip: The top tier of the product hierarchy is called 'Product Class' in CBS and 'Product Line' in Temenos. It identifies broad categories like ACCOUNT, LENDING, or DEPOSIT and defines which base Components are mandatory or optional. Beneath this sits Product Type (Temenos: Product Group) and then the individual Product (Temenos: Product).

q: The COMMA-VERSION pattern in Temenos signals zero-authorization. What's the implication in CBS Full Screen?
options: Two users must approve
The record becomes authorized immediately upon input — no 4-Eyes
HOLD is required
The record goes to SIM
tip: A 'Full Screen' projection in CBS (identified by a comma after the Table name, like CUSTOMER,) — the equivalent of a Temenos COMMA-VERSION — bypasses the standard 4-Eyes authorization workflow. As soon as the user submits, the record is authorized and becomes LIVE. This is reserved for low-risk operations where requiring a second approver would add friction without security benefit.

q: What is a Record in CBS?
options: A backup file
A single instance of a Table representing one specific business object
A reporting tool
A user account
tip: A Table in CBS holds many Records — each Record is a single instance representing one specific business object. For example, the CUSTOMER Table holds many Records: 'CUSTOMER 123456' is one Record (one specific client), 'CUSTOMER 123457' is another. Think of a Table as the schema/container and a Record as one filled-in row. Each Record contains values for all the Fields defined by its Table.

q: How is a Record uniquely identified?
options: By color
By a primary key (ID) — often a client number, account number, deal ID, or alternate key like IBAN
By creation timestamp only
By the field count
tip: Every Record has a primary key — its unique ID — that lets the system fetch exactly that Record. The ID is typically a domain-meaningful value: a customer number, account number, deal ID, or contract ID. ACCOUNT Records can also be retrieved via an alternate key like IBAN. The primary key is what the standard operations (SEE, INPUT, AUTH, etc.) work against.

q: What is a Field in CBS?
options: A whole Record
A data element within a Record in a Table (like a column in a database)
A user role
A plugin
tip: A Field is one data element inside a Record — analogous to a column in a relational database table. Examples on a CUSTOMER Record include Customer Name, Date of Birth, Nationality; on an ACCOUNT Record they include Currency, Working Balance, Category. Each Field has metadata (type, validation rules, mandatory/optional status) defined in the Dynamic Data Dictionary (DDD).

q: Which of the following is NOT a property a Field can have?
options: Mandatory or optional
Single-value or multi-value
System (vendor-defined) or locally customized
Cryptographically signed by default
tip: Fields can be: mandatory or optional (you must fill them in or not), single-value or multi-value (one value or a list), validated against dictionaries (the DDD checks types and constraints), and either system-defined (shipped by the vendor) or locally customized (added by the bank via DDD injection). Cryptographic signing is not a standard built-in Field property.

q: What is a Multi-Value Field?
options: A field shared across multiple records
A field that can hold multiple values within the same record
A field with multiple owners
A field stored in multiple databases
tip: A Multi-Value Field holds an ordered list of values inside a single Record — instead of needing a separate child table to hold the list. For example, a customer's PHONE Field can hold three phone numbers as multi-values on the same CUSTOMER Record. This is enabled by the DDD's Non-1NF (Non-First-Normal-Form) support, which lets one Record natively store repeating data.

q: Why use a Multi-Value Field instead of a child table?
options: Faster network
Native storage of repeating data inside one record — co-located, fewer joins, simpler model
Lower cost
Better encryption
tip: When repeating data (phone numbers, counterparties, repayment installments) is small and tightly tied to its parent Record, a Multi-Value Field stores it all inside that one Record. Benefits: no separate child table to manage, no joins to assemble the data, and the DDD automatically keeps related multi-value Fields aligned via Field Associations (the <, -, > markers). This is one of CBS's signature design choices.

q: Canonical examples of Multi-Value Field use:
options: Multiple phone numbers or addresses for a customer; multiple counterparties for a limit
Currency exchange rates
Holiday calendars
Login passwords
tip: The glossary highlights two textbook examples: (1) a customer with multiple phone numbers/addresses — all stored as repeating values inside the CUSTOMER Record's PHONE/ADDRESS Field; (2) a credit LIMIT Record with multiple counterparties — all stored inside one LIMIT Record's COUNTERPARTY Field. In both cases, the alternative (separate rows in a child table) would be more complex without adding value.

q: Are Fields always vendor-defined?
options: Yes — always
No — they can be system (vendor-defined) OR locally customized
Yes — for FIN classification
Only in INT tables
tip: Two kinds of Fields coexist: SYSTEM Fields (vendor-shipped, part of the core product) and LOCAL Fields (banks add their own via DDD customization injection). The DDD automatically injects custom local Fields into TABLE.DEF, instantly extending the Table without touching vendor code. This is what makes CBS adaptable to country-specific or bank-specific requirements without recompilation.

q: A Record identifier like 'CUSTOMER A78342' represents which conceptual layer of the data model?
options: One specific client of the bank
All clients of the bank in aggregate
A reporting view
A backup snapshot
tip: Each Record represents ONE specific business object. CUSTOMER A78342 is one specific person/corporate the bank holds master data for. The CUSTOMER Table itself is the SCHEMA (the container); each Record is a row holding values for all the Fields defined by the Table. Many Records per Table; one specific instance per Record.

q: What is the role of dictionaries in validating Fields?
options: They have no role — Field validation is performed entirely by per-projection custom code
Fields can be validated against dictionaries (DDD) which enforce data types, format, and constraints
They only translate language codes between regions but don't participate in validation
They only count fields per record for capacity-planning purposes during EOD reporting
tip: The DDD (Dynamic Data Dictionary) is the metadata layer that holds rules for every Field — its data type, format, length, allowed values, and any validation routines (like validateDate or validateAmount). Because the DDD is metadata, not code, an admin can update a rule (e.g. change a field from Numeric to Alphanumeric) and the change applies immediately without recompiling — this is called hot-swappable validation.

q: Examples of Fields mentioned in the glossary:
options: Customer Name, Date of Birth, Account Currency, Transfer Amount
Username, password, IP
Color, size, weight
Branch ID only
tip: Field examples cited in the glossary: Customer Name and Date of Birth (CUSTOMER Record), Account Currency (ACCOUNT Record), Transfer Amount (FUNDS.TRANSFER Record). Each Field has properties defined in the DDD: mandatory or optional, single or multi-value, validated against dictionaries, and either vendor-defined or locally customized.

q: Does a Record contain values for every Field defined in its Table?
options: Yes — every Field has a slot (possibly empty/null if optional)
No — only some Fields exist per Record
Only in INT Tables
Only after AUTH
tip: A Record contains a value position for every Field defined by its Table — optional Fields may be empty/null, but the slot exists. This is what makes Records uniform: any code reading a CUSTOMER Record knows exactly which Fields to expect based on the Table's definition in the DDD.

q: A customer has three phone numbers. The cleanest model?
options: Three separate customer records
A multi-value PHONE Field inside one customer record
Three child tables
Hardcoded constants
tip: Use a single multi-value PHONE Field inside ONE CUSTOMER Record. This is exactly what Non-1NF and multi-value Fields are designed for: keep the data co-located, no joins, automatic index alignment via the DDD's Field Association markers. Creating three separate CUSTOMER Records would imply three customers — totally wrong.

q: A credit limit with multiple counterparties is best modeled via…
options: One Record per counterparty
A multi-value COUNTERPARTY Field in one LIMIT Record
A linked list outside the system
Manual notes
tip: Per the glossary, a credit LIMIT with multiple counterparties uses a multi-value COUNTERPARTY Field inside one LIMIT Record. All counterparties live together on the limit they apply to. The DDD ensures related multi-value Fields (e.g. counterparty + counterparty's exposure) stay index-aligned via Field Association blocks.

q: An IBAN can serve as an alternate key for which kind of Record?
options: A customer
A program
An ACCOUNT Record
A holiday
tip: While Records are usually identified by their primary key (customer number, account number, etc.), some Records support alternate keys — IBAN is the canonical example for ACCOUNT Records. So you can SEE an ACCOUNT either by its account number or by its IBAN. The Resolution Waterfall (NAU → LIV → HIS → ARC) still applies — alternate keys just give another way in.

q: Per the glossary, an Enquiry is best described as…
options: A write operation
A read-only view or report over one or more Tables
A backup
A plugin
tip: An Enquiry is a READ-ONLY configuration that defines: which Tables to query, which Fields to filter on, what sort order, and what to display. It's how you build reports and list views without writing code. Enquiries can also merge data dynamically across Tables (Soft Links) and combine the LIV, NAU, and HIS files into a single virtual view (Virtual Data Merging). They cannot mutate data.

q: A Report (per the glossary) is…
options: The same as an Enquiry
