Reduces complexity and prevents 'spaghetti logic' across many apps each enforcing their own version of rules
Programming-language constraints in the older codebase forced kernel-level placement of governance rules
Hardware limitations in the bank's existing infrastructure required the centralisation pattern
tip: Kernel-level governance = ONE place implements segregation, 4-Eyes, edit locking, audit. Applications just use these primitives. Scattered governance (different rules in different apps) causes drift, gaps, and 'whose-version-is-right?' chaos. Centralization simplifies upgrades, audits, and security reviews — the kernel is the trusted layer.

q: Compare legacy 'fragmented brokers' (Axon + RabbitMQ) vs target unified event streaming. What's eliminated?
options: Customer data records inadvertently duplicated across the two messaging brokers' queues
The steep learning curve of Axon's CQRS/Event-Sourcing programming model + the dual-broker complexity
The need to process banking transactions across two different message-bus protocols simultaneously
User account synchronisation issues between Active Directory and the legacy broker setup
tip: Axon imposes a rigid CQRS/Event Sourcing programming style — developers must learn it before contributing. Plus RabbitMQ handled standard messaging. Two brokers = two operational stacks = two failure modes. Kafka alone handles everything (events, webhooks, async messaging) with a simpler model — fewer moving parts, lower cognitive load.

q: An 'External Event Publisher' (webhooks) in the target architecture is built on top of which broker?
options: The bank's existing email server forwarded into a webhook-style notification pipeline
Apache Kafka
An FTP-based file-drop integration pattern reused from the legacy reporting workflow
A custom carrier-pigeon-style relay system maintained internally by operations engineers
tip: Kafka's persistence and replayability make it ideal for webhooks: even if an external partner's endpoint is down, events sit in Kafka and are delivered when they recover. Replay enables re-sending if a partner needs to recover from their outage. High throughput handles thousands of partners. Compare with RabbitMQ's transient delivery (lost if not consumed).

q: Configurable validation framework (target arch) means validations are…
options: Hard-coded in Java with annotations marking which validations should run on which fields
Defined and executed dynamically based on metadata — admins can change rules without code changes
Generated randomly per request to make penetration testing more challenging for security teams
Performed manually by operators reviewing each transaction during business-hour processing
tip: Instead of compiling validation logic into code, the framework reads validation rules from metadata (e.g. 'this field must be a positive amount in [USD, EUR, GBP]'). Admins update rules; the framework picks them up at runtime. Release cycles shrink from months to minutes for rule changes — critical for regulatory compliance.

q: Virtual Tables (in the target DDD) reduce the need for…
options: Disk space allocated for storing physical tables holding rarely-changed reference data
Physical Tables holding small static dictionaries (Risk Levels, Statuses, Country Codes for one form)
Database server instances dedicated to serving reference-data queries with high availability
Backup operations needed to protect physical reference tables during weekly maintenance
tip: Without virtual tables, every small reference list ('Risk Level: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW') becomes its own physical Table — clutter, schema bloat, migration overhead. Virtual Tables centralize all such lists in ONE physical table (EB.LOOKUP), distinguished by compound keys. The DDD reports 90% reduction in physical schema for simple reference data.

q: Per the target architecture, what's the benefit of 'Effective Dating & Versioning' on products?
options: Randomly assigned effective dates to each product to prevent gaming by sophisticated customers
Each product record is effective-dated — supports historical tracking AND scheduling future product launches/condition updates
Random pricing fluctuations within configurable bounds to test customer price sensitivity
Customer confusion deliberately introduced to encourage them to call the support team for clarification
tip: Effective dating means each product version has start/end dates. The bank can schedule a fee structure change to take effect on a future date (e.g. 2027-01-01) — when that date arrives, the system automatically switches versions. Historical reports always use the version that was effective at the report date. No weekend deployments to flip versions.

q: The 5 primary triggers for Activities (in target architecture) include…
options: Tarot cards drawn by the head of product to determine which activities to schedule for the day
User (manual), Transaction (external apps), Scheduled (COB), OFS (external systems), Secondary (cascade from primary)
Only manual user input is supported — all other origin paths are deprecated in the current architecture
Only batch-scheduled execution is supported — interactive activity triggers are not architecturally allowed
tip: Activities can originate from: USER (a manager manually edits), TRANSACTION (an external app submits a payment that drives an activity), SCHEDULED (the COB engine triggers daily accruals), OFS (Open Financial Service — external system integrations), or SECONDARY (one Activity automatically chains to another). All five paths converge through the same Activity Orchestrator.

q: Why is 'separating system date from business date' important for COB?
options: Cosmetic — the date-display widget shows a more pleasing colour when business date differs from system date
Lets the COB engine process the day's batch without users seeing the date change until business reopens
It deliberately slows transaction processing during COB to give batch jobs higher CPU priority
Separation is a regulatory holdover from older banking laws that have since been repealed
tip: System date = the OS clock; business date = the 'banking day' the system considers current. If COB takes 4 hours overnight, you don't want users seeing the business date 'flicker' mid-batch. Decoupling means the business date advances only when COB completes successfully. Plus 24/7 operation: batches can run while branches in other time zones operate.

q: Determinism (in 'centralized transaction orchestration') means…
options: The result depends on environmental weather conditions like temperature and humidity at the data centre
Same inputs always produce the same outputs in the same order — making behavior predictable and debuggable
The order of operations is randomised at runtime to prevent timing-based side-channel attacks
Manual operator override is required between each orchestrated step for safety in critical paths
tip: Deterministic orchestration: given the same starting state and the same operation, the result is identical every time, in a predictable sequence. Compare distributed sagas: events bouncing across microservices may arrive in different orders, causing race conditions and non-deterministic outcomes. Determinism makes debugging tractable — you can reproduce any production issue locally.

q: Per the target architecture, the LOCAL.REF mechanism (Schema-less Extension) primarily serves which need?
options: A gaming-related extension allowing the bank to embed casino-style applications inside CBS
Adding bank-specific or region-specific fields (e.g. a unique national tax ID format) without changing core code
Customer dating preferences tracked to help relationship managers schedule sales conversations effectively
An email delivery extension routing notifications through the bank's preferred SMTP gateway provider
tip: Different countries have different fields: a German bank may need a unique tax identifier (Steueridentifikationsnummer); a Singapore bank needs an FIN ID; a Japanese bank needs a different ID format. LOCAL.REF lets the local team add these fields via metadata without modifying the vendor's core code — preserving the upgrade path.

q: Compared to a generic distributed event-driven approach, what kind of debugging advantage does the centralized Activity Orchestrator provide?
options: No advantage — both approaches produce equivalent debugging experiences in practice
Simpler debugging — the full transaction lives in one place; no need to trace events across multiple services and brokers
The orchestrator is implemented with fewer lines of code than the equivalent saga-based approach
Random distribution of state across services helps debugging by parallelising the investigation effort
tip: In a saga-based system, debugging a failed transaction means tracing the event chain across services, brokers, and timing windows — often impossible to reproduce. A centralized Orchestrator runs the whole transaction in one process with a clear sequence. Failures have a single stack trace, a single timeline, and are 100% reproducible. Operations team's life is dramatically easier.

q: Which Table holds system-wide immutable rules like floating-point precision, cache settings, and password requirements?
options: DATES — controls the system's notion of today and works with HOLIDAY for business-day rules
SPF (System Parameter File)
USER — manages staff identity, role assignments, and per-user preferences for the operations app
CUSTOMER — stores party master data for clients but doesn't hold system-wide configuration rules
tip: SPF = System Parameter File. It holds GLOBAL system parameters that govern installation-wide behavior — precision rules, cache settings, password complexity requirements, default session timeouts. Editing SPF changes the bank's 'operational constitution'. The COMPANY Table holds per-company config; SPF is for installation-wide.

q: A bank running multiple Companies in one installation typically has…
options: A single Lead Company with no Branch companies, since branches are not implemented in MVP scope
One Lead Company and many Branch companies
Many Lead Companies each operating independently with no shared parameters or customer lists
Only end customers and no logical company structure since customers are the only first-class entity
tip: Standard topology: ONE Lead Company per installation + many Branch companies underneath. The Lead Company is the primary autonomous banking entity; Branches share its tables and operate centrally. The COMPANY Table holds this topology. Initial deployments often start with just the Lead Company and add Branches as the bank scales.

q: The PGM.FILE (Program File) and FILE.CONTROL Tables together implement…
options: A custom-built reporting engine the team developed specifically for this CBS deployment
The Application (Table) Registry — central code governance and data table classification
The customer KYC verification engine integrating with external sanctions screening providers
Random storage allocation tooling determining which tables land on which physical disks
tip: PGM.FILE = Program Registry (registers all executable programs and enforces execution types). FILE.CONTROL = File Registry (registers logical data tables with their classifications: FIN, INT, CUS, plus links to physical storage including $HIS and $NAU files). Together they form the kernel-level Application Registry — the system's 'service directory'.

q: What does the EB.LOOKUP Table provide that complements STANDARD.SELECTION?
options: A login authentication module providing OAuth-based access to operator screens
Virtual table support — central storage of dynamic lookup lists (dropdowns) defined via metadata
Field-level encryption services for protecting sensitive customer data at rest
Backup orchestration tooling coordinating snapshot creation across distributed database nodes
tip: STANDARD.SELECTION holds the DDD field-level metadata (types, validation routines). EB.LOOKUP holds VIRTUAL TABLES — the dropdown values, reference lists, and dynamic dictionaries. Together they let admins define both 'what kind of field this is' (STANDARD.SELECTION) and 'what values it can take' (EB.LOOKUP) entirely via metadata.

q: What does the CURR.NO (Current Number) audit Field record?
options: The customer's preferred phone number registered for SMS notifications about transactions
The current sequential version number of the record (e.g. 1 = original, 2 = first amendment, etc.)
The currency code used for the most recent amount field in the audit trail for that record
The number of users currently viewing the record concurrently in their session UI
tip: CURR.NO is the version counter for a record. CURR.NO=1 means it's the original input; CURR.NO=3 means there have been 2 amendments. Combined with INPUTTER, AUTHORISER, DATE.TIME, the audit block provides the complete forensic timeline of every action on a record from creation onward — meeting SOX and Basel requirements without custom code.

q: A 'LOCAL.REF.TABLE' record extends a core application (e.g. CUSTOMER) by…
options: Replacing the core CUSTOMER application entirely with the bank's local custom implementation
Injecting new bank-specific fields into the LOCAL.REF segment of the application; STANDARD.SELECTION automatically rebuilds to make them queryable
Encrypting the existing CUSTOMER application records using a bank-specific encryption key
Backing up the existing CUSTOMER application records before any vendor-driven upgrades
tip: LOCAL.REF.TABLE is the mechanism to add custom fields without modifying the core. Define the new fields (e.g. PREFERRED.LANGUAGE, REGIONAL.TAX.ID) in LOCAL.REF.TABLE; the system injects them into LOCAL.REF on CUSTOMER records; STANDARD.SELECTION auto-rebuilds so the fields are immediately queryable via enquiries. Upgrade-safe — vendor upgrades preserve custom fields.

q: The SUB.ASSOC.CODE attribute is used to…
options: Subscription codes managing which external service subscriptions the bank uses for KYC
Group custom fields into associated multi-value sets (e.g. building a custom 'Address Block' with several related fields)
Random sub-codes assigned to associations for debugging purposes during development cycles
Subscriber-count metadata tracking how many users have opted into specific data fields
tip: Custom multi-value blocks (e.g. multiple addresses each with street, city, postal code) need their member fields kept index-aligned. SUB.ASSOC.CODE designates fields as members of the same association — so adding a new index shifts all members consistently. Same principle as core associated multi-value fields, but for bank-defined extensions.

q: Why does the system DECOUPLE the business date from the server clock?
options: Cosmetic — synchronisation with the bank's wall clock for executive office displays
Ensures financial integrity regardless of the physical clock — the COB batch is the only entity that advances the business date
Slows transaction processing during off-business hours by introducing artificial clock delays
Reduces the need for backup operations since business state changes are tied to clock advancement
tip: If the server clock advances, the business date shouldn't automatically — interest, schedules, and reports all key off the business date. The DATES Table (storing TODAY, LAST.WORKING.DAY) is updated ONLY by Close of Business (COB). This means batch processing controls when 'a new day starts' in banking terms. Branches in different time zones see the same business date globally.

q: The system supports a 3-tier holiday calendar structure. Which is NOT one of the three tiers?
options: OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY (country legal holidays for interest calc and value-date rules)
BRANCH.HOLIDAY (branch physical opening — when staff are physically at counters)
BATCH.HOLIDAY (system processing days — when COB runs)
CUSTOMER.HOLIDAY (per-customer personal days off when their account stops accruing interest)
tip: The 3 tiers are: OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY (country legal holidays — for interest calc rules), BRANCH.HOLIDAY (when the physical branch is open — can differ from official, e.g. a branch open Saturday morning), and BATCH.HOLIDAY (when COB runs — usually 365/year). 'Customer Holiday' isn't a thing — customers don't dictate the bank's calendar.

q: Branch opening on a Saturday WITHOUT advancing the financial business date is supported via…
options: A manual operator override applied at the branch each Saturday morning by the supervisor
The hierarchical calendar — BRANCH.HOLIDAY differs from OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY/BATCH.HOLIDAY
A physical change to the branch's network address routing during weekend openings
A bribery of the regulators to allow weekend opening contrary to standard business-day rules
tip: If your branch in São Paulo wants to open Saturday morning to serve customers, you set BRANCH.HOLIDAY to NOT mark Saturday as a holiday — but OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY still marks it as non-business. So customers can transact, but interest accrual, value dating, and ledger close treat it as a non-business day. Best of both worlds.

q: CURRENT.DAY status can be NORMAL, RESTRICTED, or CLOSED. What does RESTRICTED indicate?
options: A specific user's session is restricted from accessing certain account types during the day
The branch is open even though it's officially a holiday or weekend — accept transactions but with adjusted financial treatment
Only a specific physical area of the bank is open such as the ATM lobby during off-hours
Only system administrators may log in for emergency maintenance during the restricted period
tip: NORMAL = regular business day. CLOSED = no operations at all. RESTRICTED = an unusual day where the branch operates but the official calendar marks it as non-business. Useful for special events, regulatory exceptional days, or branch-specific Saturday openings. The status influences how transactions value-date themselves.

q: EB.TIME.ZONES handles…
options: The customer's preferred local time zone for SMS notification delivery scheduling
Multi-region processing — adjusting audit timestamps based on each Company's location relative to the server
A UI clock display showing the time in each branch's local zone on operator dashboards
Random time-zone offsets applied to audit data to prevent timezone-based fraud patterns
tip: A bank with branches in Singapore, London, and New York needs audit timestamps that make sense to each local team. EB.TIME.ZONES lets the system normalize timestamps based on each Company's location. The Sydney branch sees their actions in Sydney time; HQ sees consolidated logs in HQ time. Same underlying data, locale-aware presentation.

q: Date Conventions for handling scheduled dates that fall on holidays include Forward, Backward, and Modified Following. What does 'Modified Following' mean?
options: A random rule applied per transaction to obscure scheduling patterns from external observers
Move to the next business day, but if that crosses a month-end, move BACKWARD to the previous business day instead
Skip the scheduled payment entirely if it falls on any non-business day
Equivalent to the Forward convention — the two terms refer to the same date-shifting behaviour
tip: Modified Following is a common banking convention: if a scheduled date (e.g. month-end interest accrual) lands on a holiday, MOVE FORWARD to the next business day — UNLESS that moves into the next month, in which case move BACKWARD. Ensures payments stay in the original month. Used widely in FX, derivatives, and structured products.

q: In the LANGUAGE Table, Language 1 is reserved for…
options: The customer's primary spoken language as captured during onboarding for service routing
English
Programming languages used for kernel development like Java and jBase
Russian, reflecting the historical origin of the underlying T24 framework's codebase
tip: LANGUAGE 1 = English (by convention, system-wide reserved). Languages 2, 3, ... are bank-defined per region (French, Mandarin, Spanish, Arabic, etc.). The reservation means all base error messages, dictionary terms, and system documentation have a defined Language 1 fallback. Bank-specific languages extend the base.

q: DYNAMIC.TEXT handles application messages containing variable data (like 'Account & has insufficient funds'). What's the role of the '&' placeholder?
options: A magic symbol decorating the message to indicate it should not be translated to other languages
Replaced by actual values (e.g. the specific Account ID) at runtime when the message is translated and displayed
Pure decoration with no functional meaning in the message-formatting pipeline
An error indicator signalling that the message template is malformed and needs operator review
tip: DYNAMIC.TEXT messages have placeholders (often '&') that the system substitutes at runtime — so the user sees 'Account 12345 has insufficient funds' (with the actual account ID). The placeholder mechanism works across all configured languages: the English template 'Account & has insufficient funds' has counterparts in French, German, etc., each with the placeholder. The runtime substitutes the actual value.

q: If a Japanese branch needs Japanese characters validated correctly, which mechanism enables this?
options: Hard-coded Latin-only validation in the kernel, rejecting any non-Latin character input
ASCII.VAL.TABLE / ASCII.VALUES — define valid characters per language, supporting multi-byte characters like Japanese, Chinese
Reject any input containing non-Latin characters with an automatic error message to the user
Require manual review by a Japanese-speaking operator for each transaction containing non-Latin text
tip: ASCII.VAL.TABLE (despite the name) supports multi-byte and Unicode characters per language. Japanese (with hiragana, katakana, kanji), Chinese, Arabic, and Hebrew all need different valid-character sets. The validation engine reads ASCII.VAL.TABLE per LANGUAGE and rejects characters outside the allowed set — preventing data corruption or display issues.

q: The CURRENCY framework supports up to 99 distinct currency MARKETS. Why?
options: A randomly chosen ceiling figure with no specific architectural rationale behind 99
Different exchange rates per channel — e.g. Cash/Notes (market 10) vs Interbank (market 1) for the same currency pair
Only foreign-exchange traders ever need more than one market per currency in production deployments
Random pricing fluctuations across markets help obscure pricing patterns from competitor banks
tip: Same currency, different rates depending on context: an Interbank market (market 1) has tight spreads for institutional deals; Cash/Notes (market 10) has wider spreads to cover handling costs; Wire transfer market might be different again. 99 markets gives enough headroom for any bank's product/channel mix. Driven by CURRENCY.MARKET configuration.

q: CURRENCY.PARAM enforces…
options: Random structural constraints applied per currency to test edge cases during QA
IMMUTABLE structural standards (Numeric Code, Decimals) across a multi-company environment — same code, same decimals, every Company
Currency-conversion rate validation logic running during EOD batch processing each evening
User password complexity rules tied to the currencies a user is authorised to transact in
tip: Even in a multi-company installation where different Companies might use different exchange rates (CURRENCY.MARKET), the STRUCTURE of a currency must be uniform — Numeric Code (ISO 4217), decimal places. CURRENCY.PARAM enforces this immutably so reconciliation across Companies is reliable. JPY always has 0 decimals everywhere; USD always has 2.

q: QUOTATION.CODE is used for low-value currencies. Example use case?
options: Random pricing applied to obscure pricing patterns across currencies during competitive analysis
Quoting rates per 100 or 1000 units (e.g. for JPY/KRW), making the rate display human-readable
Hiding the exchange rate from end customers to prevent rate arbitrage during their transactions
Boosting displayed prices artificially to increase apparent margins on low-value currencies
tip: Some currencies have very low per-unit value (Japanese Yen, Korean Won, Vietnamese Dong). Quoting 'USD/JPY = 0.0067' is awkward; 'USD/100 JPY = 0.67' or 'USD per 1000 KRW = 0.85' is easier to read. QUOTATION.CODE tells the system to quote in units of 100 or 1000 for these currencies. Calculations always use the underlying rate; display uses the friendly quotation.

q: USER.SMS.GROUP enables…
options: Sending bulk SMS notifications to customers about account changes or marketing campaigns
Role-based provisioning — define 'Job Types' (Teller, Manager, etc.) with bundled permissions; assign users to groups instead of editing each user
User group chat functionality allowing operations staff to discuss transactions in real time
Random grouping of users for A/B testing different UI presentations during pilot rollouts
tip: USER.SMS.GROUP (SMS = Security Management System, not text messages) is the role-based permissions engine. Define a TELLER group with INPUT rights on certain Tables; users in that group inherit the rights. Updating the group updates all members. Bulk permission management — critical at scale. Users link to groups via '@GROUPNAME' in their profile.

q: The 4-Level RBAC matrix dimensions are…
options: Branch, Currency, Customer Sector, and Login Time — each modulating access at runtime
Company, Application, Function (Input/Authorise/Delete/Reverse/See), Field
Just the Company dimension — all other access decisions are inherited from the company-level permissions
Just the Field dimension — access is controlled at the data level only, with no application-level role concept
tip: 4-Level RBAC controls access along four axes: (1) Company — which branches/legal entities; (2) Application — which Tables/Versions; (3) Function — what verbs (INPUT, AUTHORISE, DELETE, REVERSE, SEE); (4) Field — data-level masking (e.g. hide salary from regular tellers). All four together = 'zero trust' permission model.

q: Temporal access logic in the USER Table allows the bank to enforce…
options: Random session-duration policies applied per user to test session-termination behaviour
Permitted system usage by time of day (START.TIME, END.TIME) and day of week (ALLOWED.DAYS), including holiday-specific rules
Customer waiting times in branch queues are tracked for staffing efficiency calculations
Login response speeds to detect brute-force authentication attempts on user accounts
tip: Temporal restrictions: a teller might be allowed access only Mon-Fri 8am-6pm; an external auditor only on specific days. START.TIME / END.TIME / ALLOWED.DAYS enforce this — login outside the window is rejected. Holiday-specific extensions allow exceptions (e.g. operations team can log in on a holiday for emergency support). Reduces attack surface during off-hours.

q: Data Access Control (DATA.ACCESS) is used to…
options: Encrypt the entire database with a single key managed by the bank's hardware security module
Restrict a user's ability to view/process specific records based on field-level values (e.g. preventing standard staff from seeing 'Staff Accounts')
Block all access to the data layer except through the API gateway with token-based authentication
Apply random restrictions to access patterns to test the system's response to denial attempts
tip: DATA.ACCESS is row-level + field-level security. Example: 'Staff Accounts' (the personal accounts of bank employees) are identified by a specific category range. Standard tellers should NOT see them (privacy + integrity). DATA.ACCESS lets you define this filter so the standard teller's enquiries never return Staff Accounts — even though the same Table holds them.

q: EB.ROLE manages…
options: A film role identifier used in marketing materials for promotional video productions
Specific roles or legal capacities (Signatory, Proxy, Guarantor) for staff and external parties involved in customer relationships
User password rotation policies enforced through the centralised IAM authentication layer
UI role definitions for layout customisation on the operator's dashboard during sessions
tip: EB.ROLE captures legal/business capacities a party can have in a relationship: SIGNATORY (can sign on behalf of), PROXY (legally represents), GUARANTOR (backs a credit). These roles are critical for compliance, contract enforceability, and AML. They sit alongside the standard CUSTOMER profile, attached via the RELATION table.

q: Password complexity (Minimum length, required uppercase/lowercase/numeric/special character counts) is defined where?
options: Each user picks their own password complexity preferences during onboarding sessions
Centrally in the SPF — system-wide rules everyone must respect
Hard-coded in the application source code with version-controlled updates per release
Random complexity rules applied per session to make brute-force attacks computationally infeasible
tip: SPF (System Parameter File) holds the password syntax rules — minimum 12 characters, at least 1 upper, 1 lower, 2 numeric, 1 special. The system enforces these centrally; no app can override. Changing the SPF rule applies to next password change for every user. Critical for meeting regulatory standards (e.g. NIST guidelines) consistently.

q: AUTOLOG.TIME.MINS enforces…
options: Random timeout values applied per session to obscure session-duration patterns from observers
Auto-logout for users holding RECORD LOCKS — ensuring locks don't persist forever if a user walks away
Backup interval timing for periodic snapshot creation across the production database fleet
UI refresh rate settings determining how often dashboard widgets pull fresh data from APIs
tip: If a user holds a $NAU draft (record lock) and walks away, the lock would block other users indefinitely. AUTOLOG.TIME.MINS automatically logs out users who've been inactive WHILE holding locks — releasing the locks for others. Distinct from TIME.OUT.MINUTES which is general inactivity logout. Prevents the 'forgotten draft' from blocking team productivity.

q: The PROTOCOL file captures…
options: A communication protocol document outlining how services should exchange messages
Security violations and unauthorized access attempts — even if individual user logging is off, violations are ALWAYS recorded here
Network packet captures stored for forensic analysis after security incidents
Customer interaction logs capturing every phone call, branch visit, and online session
tip: PROTOCOL is the security audit file — failed login attempts, unauthorized access tries, password resets, security setting changes. Critically, it's MANDATORY: even if individual user logging is disabled, security violations still land in PROTOCOL. The EOD report generator produces a regular PROTOCOL report for compliance and forensic readiness.

q: OVERRIDE.CLASS.DETAILS allows the bank to set…
options: Random thresholds applied across overrides to make override timing harder to predict
Specific value thresholds per override (e.g. amounts > 10,000 USD require manager-class approval; below that, supervisor-class is enough)
Customer biographical details linked to specific override decisions for audit purposes
User password complexity requirements tied to the override classes a user can approve
tip: Different overrides require different authority levels. OVERRIDE.CLASS.DETAILS encodes thresholds: 'unauthorised overdraft up to $5,000 = supervisor; up to $50,000 = branch manager; above $50,000 = regional director'. The override flow automatically routes to the right tier based on the amount — no human triage needed. Smarter exception handling.

q: DISPO.OFFICER is the…
options: A disposable single-use officer identity issued for short-term coverage roles
Designated officer to whom an override of a specific class/type is routed for review/approval
Random officer assignment across the workforce for override approval load-balancing
Customer service representatives handling override approvals for high-value transactions
tip: When an override is raised, it doesn't just go to 'somebody' — it goes to the DISPO.OFFICER assigned to that override class/type. Each officer has a defined competence area and hierarchy level. Combined with DISPO.ITEM (the override record itself), the system manages an exception WORKFLOW with defined responsibility — no overrides get lost in inboxes.

q: DISPO.ITEM tracks…
options: A disposable item record that gets purged after each EOD cleanup cycle to save storage
Individual override records — preventing final authorization of the master transaction until ALL associated DISPO.ITEMs are marked 'APPROVED'
Customer purchasing-pattern items tracked for marketing analytics in the operations layer
Random data items used during system testing to verify override workflow correctness
tip: A transaction might breach multiple soft rules and raise multiple overrides. Each override is a DISPO.ITEM in the queue. The master transaction CANNOT be authorized until every DISPO.ITEM is marked APPROVED by an authorized officer. This ensures exceptions aren't accidentally skipped — every override is explicitly handled.

q: DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER stores…
options: Department petty cash balances tracked separately from the main GL for small-expense management
The bank's organizational hierarchy — divisions, departments, account officers/relationship managers — linked to users and to accounting entries
Random data records used during testing of the hierarchy traversal performance
User profile photographs displayed in the operator UI alongside transaction approvals
tip: DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER is the org-chart Table. It defines business units, divisions, departments, and individual Account Officers/Relationship Managers, with reporting lines to parents. Every accounting entry can carry an ACCOUNT.OFFICER code, letting the bank consolidate financial activity by department for P&L attribution and accountability.

q: Why does the system maintain a MNEMONIC.CUSTOMER mapping?
options: For UI styling — making customer names appear in different visual styles based on configuration
Provides a human-readable alternate key for CUSTOMER records — users can search by mnemonic instead of long customer numbers
A random key for sharding customers across multiple database instances for performance
Customer slogans or branded taglines associated with corporate customers for marketing purposes
tip: Customer numbers are usually long, opaque sequences (e.g. CUSTOMER 178293461). Tellers don't memorize those. MNEMONIC.CUSTOMER gives each customer a short, memorable alternate key — like 'SMITH-J-LON-1' for John Smith in London. Search by mnemonic; the system resolves to the underlying customer number. Quality-of-life feature for high-volume teller work.

q: The RELATION Table manages…
options: Customer-dating application records tracking the bank's social events with VIP customers
Complex party relationships (Corporate Groups, Parent/Child, Signatory, Proxy, Guarantor) — automatically generating reverse-relationship records
User chat session records between operations staff and customer service representatives
Random key-value records used during data structure benchmarking and testing cycles
tip: Banking parties have many possible relationships: corporate parent/subsidiary, beneficial ownership, guarantor, signatory, joint account holder, power-of-attorney. RELATION captures these as bidirectional records — defining one direction automatically creates the reverse (parent → child also creates child → parent). Critical for 360-degree risk views and KYC.

q: The AML.CHECK field captures…
options: The bank's official AML policy text stored as a versioned document for staff reference
Persistent state of whether AML screening has been sent, accepted, or rejected for this customer
AML certificates issued by external compliance providers and stored as PDF attachments
Random sample of AML checks performed for benchmark testing of the AML provider integration
tip: AML.CHECK has values like SENT, YES (passed), NO (failed/blocked). It's the customer-level state of AML screening — alongside AML.RESULT (awaited/positive/negative). These persistent fields drive workflow: positive results may block account opening; awaited results trigger KYC re-checks. Combined with AUTO.NEXT.REVIEW.DATE, they automate KYC lifecycle management.

q: FATCA and MiFID are…
options: Programming languages developed for use in writing core banking applications and integrations
Regional regulatory compliance frameworks (FATCA = US tax; MiFID = EU investor protection); the system has fields for each
Backup file formats used by the financial reporting subsystem during nightly archive cycles
Currency codes used in cross-border payments for specific jurisdictions in CEMAC and UEMOA
tip: FATCA = Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (US law requiring foreign banks to report US persons' accounts to the IRS). MiFID = Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (EU regulation governing investment-services suitability). The system has dedicated fields (US TIN for FATCA, investor risk profile for MiFID) — config rather than custom development per regulation.

q: The AA.PRODUCT.MANAGER service enforces…
options: A visual UI styling pattern applied to product-management screens for visual consistency
The Product Factory's 3-stage lifecycle (Design → Proof → Publish) — including the Proof quality gate that blocks misconfigured products
Random validation checks applied during product publication to ensure quality at runtime
A vendor advertising framework allowing the bank to display product promotions in the operator UI
tip: AA.PRODUCT.MANAGER is the orchestrator of the product lifecycle. Design = sandbox configuration. Proof = compile-and-validate; checks inherited conditions resolve, mandatory attributes exist, conditions don't conflict. Publish = commits to PRODUCT.CATALOG for live origination. Failed Proof blocks publication — protecting customers from misconfigured products that could mis-price loans or skip required accounting.

q: ARR.LINK options include TRACKING, NON.TRACKING, and CUSTOMER.TRACKING. What does CUSTOMER.TRACKING enable?
options: Tracking customer geographical location through their mobile-device coordinates for risk scoring
Allows negotiation on certain attributes (per-customer rate, fee waiver) while the OTHER attributes still track product changes
Tracking customers physically in branch lobbies for queue-management and staffing decisions
Random tracking IDs applied to customer records during marketing campaign attribution analysis
tip: ARR.LINK governs how Arrangements (customer contracts) follow Product updates. TRACKING = inherits all product changes (mass updates apply). NON.TRACKING = fixed at creation, never changes. CUSTOMER.TRACKING = hybrid — negotiated attributes (e.g. a special VIP rate) stay fixed, but other attributes still follow product updates. Best of both worlds for negotiated customers.

q: The AA.ARR.PRODUCT.TRACKER service is responsible for…
options: User-activity tracking generating reports about which products operators view most frequently
AUTOMATICALLY updating Arrangement condition records when a Product is re-published — propagating changes to thousands of live contracts in one click
Random product updates applied during testing of arrangement-product synchronisation behaviour
Backup tracking ensuring product configurations are snapshot before each major version release
tip: Re-publishing a Product (e.g. changing a fee) should propagate to all live customer contracts using that Product. AA.ARR.PRODUCT.TRACKER scans Arrangements, identifies those linked TRACKING (or partially TRACKING) to the updated Product, and applies the changes. One product update = mass contract maintenance, no batch script writing needed.

q: EB.DUPLICATE.TYPE supports duplicate prevention by…
options: Random duplicate-detection rules applied unpredictably to test fraud-screening robustness
Letting the bank define field combinations (e.g. Debit Account + Amount + Value Date) to form a composite key — duplicates detected against this key
Just date-based detection — duplicates are flagged purely by transaction date and time matching
Manual review by operations staff for every transaction with no automated duplicate detection
tip: Duplicate prevention is critical — accidentally processing a $250,000 wire twice is catastrophic. EB.DUPLICATE.TYPE lets you define what counts as a duplicate per transaction type: a payment might dup-check on (Debit Acct + Amount + Value Date); a loan disbursement on (Customer + Amount + Date). Composite keys catch both technical (same message ID) and functional (same business intent) duplicates.

q: ARCHIVE / ARC.GENERIC handle…
options: Historical computer-game records stored alongside customer transactions for archive purposes
Migrating high-volume historical records (old STMT.ENTRY, closed transactions) from live files to read-only archive ($ARC suffixed tables) based on PURGE.DATE or RETENTION.PERIOD
User account backups copied to long-term storage for disaster recovery purposes only
Customer transaction history exported for analytics, leaving live tables fully untouched
tip: Over time, live tables accumulate millions of records and slow down. The ARCHIVE engine, driven by ARC.GENERIC.REQUEST, migrates old data to $ARC suffixed tables (e.g. STMT.ENTRY$ARC). The migration uses PURGE.DATE (absolute) or RETENTION.PERIOD (rolling window) rules. Archive Tables are read-only and live on cheaper storage — preserving compliance access while keeping live tables fast.

q: AC.BALANCE.TYPE differentiates multiple balance states on a single contract. Which is NOT one of the standard balance prefixes?
options: CUR (Current)
ACC (Accrued)
DUE (Arrears)
MAX (Maximum allowed)
tip: AC.BALANCE.TYPE uses standardized prefixes to track multiple states of a contract independently. CUR = the current principal, ACC = accrued (e.g. interest accrued but not yet capitalized), DUE = amounts past due / in arrears, AGE = aging buckets. MAX is not a standard balance prefix — it's just an upper limit, not a tracked balance. The dynamic appearance of these balances means a contract only carries balances it actually needs.

q: Which Table holds the live financial balances of every contract (decoupled from the static ACCOUNT record)?
options: The ACCOUNT table itself, alongside descriptive data such as account name and primary owner
EB.CONTRACT.BALANCES (ECB)
STMT.ENTRY — the same place where journal entries are stored for audit purposes
The DATES table, since balances are time-sensitive and stored alongside the system date
tip: EB.CONTRACT.BALANCES (ECB) is the high-performance engine holding live financial balances — Working, Cleared, Actual, plus all the AC.BALANCE.TYPE breakdowns. The ACCOUNT record holds only static descriptive data (account name, mnemonic, primary owner); financial state lives in ECB. This decoupling allows concurrent transaction processing without DB locking on the descriptive ACCOUNT record.

q: STMT.ENTRY, CATEG.ENTRY, and RE.CONSOL.SPEC.ENTRY are the three core posting files. What's the role of RE.CONSOL.SPEC.ENTRY?
options: Holds Profit & Loss entries only — duplicate functionality with CATEG.ENTRY for redundancy
Tracks CONTRACT-level updates and contingent (off-balance-sheet) items
Stores backup entries copied from STMT.ENTRY for disaster-recovery purposes
Stores random sample entries used for performance testing of the consolidation engine
tip: RE.CONSOL.SPEC.ENTRY is the third leg of the posting trinity. STMT.ENTRY = account-level movements (Asset/Liability), CATEG.ENTRY = P&L category movements, RE.CONSOL.SPEC.ENTRY = contract-level consolidation and contingent (off-balance-sheet) tracking like unutilized credit lines or memorandum assets. Together they cover synthetic (aggregate) and analytical (detailed) accounting views.

q: What's the role of AC.POSTING.DETAIL in the Soft Accounting chain?
options: Provides UI styling rules for how account-statement narratives are rendered to customers
Isolates formatting/narrative logic — mapping descriptions from Activity, Property, or Transaction independently of financial routing
Random data inserted into postings for testing purposes that gets filtered out at EOD
Encryption metadata applied to posting details for compliance with data-protection rules
tip: The Soft Accounting chain is AC.EVENT → AC.ALLOCATION.RULE → AC.POSTING.DETAIL → entries. AC.POSTING.DETAIL specifically handles the NARRATIVE side (account-statement descriptions, references) — separately from financial routing. So you can change how an entry is described in customer statements without touching where it lands in the GL.

q: The Working Balance on an account is defined as…
options: Sum of authorized credits and authorized debits only, ignoring any unposted pending items
Authorized entries + unauthorized debits (worst-case available funds)
The customer's average balance over the past year used for credit-score calculations
A randomly sampled balance figure used for stress testing of the calculation engine
tip: ECB tracks three distinct balance states: ACTUAL (the full ledger position), CLEARED (only authorized/cleared funds), WORKING (Cleared + unauthorized debits — the pessimistic 'what's truly available' view, used to prevent overdraws against pending debits). Tellers checking 'can the customer withdraw X?' use Working Balance.

q: Why does the system maintain ACCT.ACTIVITY (also called ACCT.BALANCE.ACTIVITY)?
options: Marketing analysis data used by relationship managers to identify cross-sell opportunities
A dated history of balances — essential for back-dated interest recalculations and audit
Random sample data used during performance benchmarking of the balance subsystem
Customer address history tracked for compliance with mailing-list update requirements
tip: ACCT.ACTIVITY snapshots the balance at every value date. If a back-dated transaction is posted (e.g. correcting an error from two weeks ago), the system uses ACCT.ACTIVITY to recompute interest accruals accurately from the corrected balance forward. Without ACCT.ACTIVITY, back-dating would be impossible without losing interest precision.

q: Activity Mapping bridges external transactions to internal AA Activities. Which Table holds the mapping?
options: AA.PRD.DES.ACTIVITY.MAPPING
DATES
USER
Random table
tip: AA.PRD.DES.ACTIVITY.MAPPING is the product-level config that ties external transaction codes (FT codes, Teller codes) to internal AA Activities. For instance, transaction code 850 (a particular disbursement code) might map to LENDING-DISBURSE-COMMITMENT activity. TRACKING.ONLY mode means mapping rules live at the product level and can't be overridden per individual contract.

q: An Eligibility Engine prevents a 16-year-old from holding an adult-only credit card. Which mechanism enforces this dynamically?
options: Manual screening
AA.PRD.DES.ELIGIBILITY rules evaluated against customer attributes (Age, Residence, Sector, Income) via EB.RULE.GATEWAY
Hardcoded if-else
Random rejection
tip: The Eligibility Engine uses rule sets (AA.PRD.DES.ELIGIBILITY) evaluated via EB.RULE.GATEWAY against EB.CONTEXT (customer + arrangement data). At product-selection time the catalog is filtered to show only products the customer qualifies for. Auto-transitions are supported too — e.g. a Minor account automatically Change-Products to a Standard account on the customer's 18th birthday.

q: A bank offers loans with Annuity, Linear, and Bullet repayment styles. These are all configured via…
options: A hard-coded calculation engine selected at install time based on the bank's product mix
AA.PRD.DES.PAYMENT.SCHEDULE — defines repayment rules including calculation type (Annuity / Linear / Actual)
Random selection from a predefined list of templates each time a new arrangement is created
Customer choice only — the calculation type is selected by the customer at contract signing
tip: The Schedule Engine configures repayment via AA.PRD.DES.PAYMENT.SCHEDULE. Annuity (constant total payment, varying principal/interest split), Linear (constant principal, varying interest), Actual (calculated each period — for bullet loans). The engine handles Date Conventions (Forward/Backward) when due dates fall on holidays, plus dynamic recalculation when interest rates change.

q: AA.SIMULATION.CAPTURE allows users to model 'what-if' scenarios. Where is simulation data stored to keep it isolated from live?
options: The main LIV (Live) files alongside production records, with a flag distinguishing simulation rows
$SIM suffixed files like AA.SIM.INTEREST, AA.SIM.TERM.AMOUNT
The customer's personal email account, sent as an attachment for review before commitment
Random storage on whichever node has spare capacity at the time the simulation runs
tip: Simulation Engine writes to parallel $SIM files (e.g. AA.SIM.INTEREST, AA.SIM.TERM.AMOUNT) — strictly isolated from live contract files. The three-stage workflow is Data Capture → Runner definition → Execution. If the customer likes the projected terms, the simulation can be promoted to a real LIV Arrangement in one click.

q: When a teller views an arrangement, ACTIVITY.PRESENTATION dynamically selects which Version (Projection) to show. What flag hides non-editable fields to save screen space?
options: HIDE_ALL
SUPPRESS.SEE.MODE
MIN_VIEW
ECO_DISPLAY
tip: SUPPRESS.SEE.MODE is a Projection flag that mathematically suppresses non-editable / non-participating fields from the payload — keeping the UI focused on only what the user can do. ACTIVITY.PRESENTATION orchestrates which Version to invoke based on the current Activity (e.g. CHANGE-INTEREST opens a different view than VIEW-BALANCES).

q: The Arrangement state machine progresses through several statuses. Which is NOT one of the standard arrangement states?
options: UNAUTH (initial draft state before any authorisation has been applied)
AUTH (authorised but not yet effective in business terms)
CURRENT (active and in force for operational purposes)
DELETED (an explicit lifecycle status indicating permanent removal of the arrangement)
tip: Standard Arrangement statuses include UNAUTH (pending authorization), AUTH (authorized), CURRENT (active and live), EXPIRED, MATURED, CLOSED. DELETED isn't a status — deletions are handled by lifecycle files (DEL space, REVE status in HIS). The state machine governs valid transitions: you can't go from CLOSED back to CURRENT, for instance.

q: When a fixed-term deposit reaches maturity, the system can renew it. Which strategy uses the CURRENT product's terms (instead of the original)?
options: ROLLOVER
RESET
CHANGE.PRODUCT
STOP
tip: Renewal strategies: ROLLOVER (extends with EXISTING terms — same rate, same period), RESET (uses CURRENT product terms — gets today's rate and conditions), CHANGE.PRODUCT (switches to a different product entirely). RESET is useful when interest rates have moved significantly and you want the renewal to reflect today's market — without manually editing each contract.

q: A bank wants up to 9 different account statement cycles per customer (e.g. monthly for customer, daily for internal reconciliation, weekly for audit). What Table supports this?
options: ACCOUNT.STATEMENT supporting up to 9 statement cycles simultaneously per account
Single-cycle hard-coded statements — multiple statement cycles per account are not supported in the architecture
A separate statement-generation engine invoked manually by operators when the bank wants extra cycles
An external email-based statement system that triggers monthly for retail and quarterly for corporate
tip: ACCOUNT.STATEMENT supports up to NINE independent statement cycles per account — frequencies from daily to yearly. Each cycle has its own STMT.GEN.CONDITION rule controlling what's included. So a corporate treasury customer might get daily statements for cash management, monthly for accounting, and quarterly for tax reporting — all simultaneously, all automated.

q: AC.PRINT.MASK is used to…
options: A print-server configuration setting controlling which printers can produce statements
Suppress internal/wash entries (e.g. CUR-to-DUE movements) from customer-facing statements while keeping them in the GL
Random selection of entries to mask for privacy testing during pre-production validation
Customer photographs masked on statement headers to comply with privacy regulations
tip: AC.PRINT.MASK (with the MASK flag in AC.ALLOCATION.RULE) hides confusing internal accounting movements from customer statements. For instance, an internal transfer from Current Principal balance to Arrears balance is meaningful accounting but bewildering to customers. Mask it on the statement; keep it in the GL. Cleaner customer experience without compromising audit.

q: ACCOUNT.CLOSURE simulates final settlement before committing. Which Table holds the pre-closure simulation?
options: AC.PRE.CLOSURE.DETAILS
A simulation file randomly selected from available SIM files at the time of closure
STMT.ENTRY records flagged with a pre-closure indicator until the closure is finalised
The DATES table where pre-closure target dates are temporarily stored for audit purposes
tip: AC.PRE.CLOSURE.DETAILS holds the dry-run simulation — final interest recalculation, charge accruals, balance zeroing logic, any blocking overrides. The branch officer can review before initiating REQUEST.CLOSURE. Reserved Posting Restriction codes 90-99 prevent unauthorized debits/credits once closure starts. Closed records move to $HIS and are registered in ACCOUNT.CLOSED.

q: POSTING.RESTRICT enables blocking of an account by reason — e.g. 'Account Frozen' or 'Deceased'. What still happens despite the restriction?
options: Nothing — the account is fully and immediately frozen against all operations including system-internal ones
Interest and charges can still accrue or park in suspense — accrual integrity is preserved
All customer-initiated transactions process normally with only operator-initiated transactions blocked
The account is automatically closed after the restriction has been in place for thirty days
tip: POSTING.RESTRICT blocks customer-facing Debits, Credits, or both — but the system still ACCRUES interest and charges (parking them in suspense if needed). Why? Otherwise, freezing an account would silently stop interest accrual, leading to incorrect balances when the freeze is lifted. The restriction is for outward movements, not internal accounting integrity.

q: AC.LOCKED.EVENTS lets the bank lock specific amounts for defined periods. Use case?
options: Random transaction restrictions applied unpredictably during fraud-testing scenarios
Reserving funds for guaranteed obligations (e.g. a $25,000 letter-of-credit reserve held for 60 days)
Locking out users from logging in to the system after multiple failed authentication attempts
Encryption keys stored on the account record to encrypt sensitive customer information
tip: AC.LOCKED.EVENTS maintains a chronological 'ladder' of locked amounts inside an account. Each lock has an amount, a period (or indefinite), and a reason. The Working Balance subtracts the locks — so the customer can't withdraw funds that are reserved for a pending letter-of-credit, escrow obligation, or court-ordered hold. When the lock expires, funds become available automatically.

q: IBAN (International Bank Account Number) is auto-generated for new accounts. Which validation algorithm does the system use?
options: MD5 hashing applied across the country code, bank ID, branch ID, and account number string
ISO/IEC 7064 MOD97-10 check-digit algorithm
SHA-256 with truncation to produce a 2-character check-digit appended to the IBAN string
Manual calculation by operations staff during onboarding with a verification step at the end
tip: IBANs use the ISO/IEC 7064 MOD97-10 standard for check-digit calculation. The system reads IN.IBAN.STRUCTURE for the country's BBAN format (Bank ID + Branch ID + Account Number positions), computes the check digits with MOD97-10, and stores the IBAN in ALT.ACCT.ID under type T24.IBAN. Auto-generated IBANs are IMMUTABLE — users can't edit them, preserving payment-network integrity.

q: Which ACCOUNT.CLASS designation is for accounts holding funds the bank has placed with another bank abroad?
options: NOSTRO
VOSTRO
SUSPENSE
BANK
tip: ACCOUNT.CLASS distinguishes internal account types. NOSTRO = 'our' account held by another bank (the bank's foreign currency cash sitting at correspondent banks). VOSTRO = 'your' account — foreign bank's funds held with US. SUSPENSE = temporary parking for unallocated entries. BANK = identifies counterparty as a bank for risk grouping. Categories 10000-19999 are typically reserved for these internal account types.

q: The Mismatch tool RE.STAT.MISMATCH does what?
options: Random checks
Identifies CRF (Central Reporting File) keys where the sum of underlying contract balances doesn't equal the total in the key — high-level integrity check
Encrypts data
Sends email
tip: RE.STAT.MISMATCH continuously monitors whether the Virtual GL agrees with the underlying sub-ledger. If the sum of individual contract balances under a CRF key doesn't match what the key reports, that's a mismatch — investigated via the more granular RE.STAT.BAL.REC tool. Together they trap any out-of-balance condition before it compounds across reporting cycles.

q: Line 9999 in CRF reporting is reserved for…
options: The last item
Catch-All — captures balances NOT specifically mapped to other report lines, ensuring no balance is omitted
Errors only
Random
tip: Reports define line definitions mapping CRF keys to display lines. Line 9999 is the 'Catch-All' — any balance whose key doesn't match a defined line still appears here. This guarantees the report total ties to the underlying GL, even when a new product creates keys the report writer hadn't anticipated. Critical for balance sheet integrity.

q: CRF (Central Reporting File) is composed of two main tables. They are…
options: LIV (Live) and NAU (Unauthorized) — the lifecycle files that hold current and pending state
CAL (Asset/Liability) and CPL (Profit/Loss)
STMT.ENTRY and CATEG.ENTRY — the two posting tables that feed into the consolidation engine
Random sample tables aggregating subsets of the full ledger for analytical processing
tip: CRF has two sides: CONSOLIDATE.ASST.LIAB (CAL) holds Asset/Liability key balances; CONSOLIDATE.PRFT.LOSS (CPL) holds P&L key balances. Together they form the Virtual GL — the consolidated, key-based ledger that supports balance sheet and P&L reporting. STMT.ENTRY/CATEG.ENTRY feed into them via Consolidation.

q: When customer static data changes (e.g. a customer moves from Sector 2001 to Sector 2002), what happens during COB?
options: Nothing — the change does not propagate and historical balances remain attached to the old sector
Automatic re-classification — affected balances are regrouped under the new keys during COB; no manual journals needed
The customer is locked out of the system until the sector change has been reviewed by compliance
All of the customer's accounts close automatically as a result of the sector change taking effect
tip: The Consolidation Engine handles dynamic re-classification. If a customer's Sector changes, contracts that key off Sector get re-keyed during COB. Balances are correctly grouped under the new keys without any manual reclassification journals. This is why the Virtual GL stays accurate even as underlying static data evolves.

q: Revaluation methods include Spot, Rebate, Straight Line, Interest, and Interest Hedged. Which is the simplest mark-to-market method?
options: Spot — uses current exchange rate against the original (book) rate
Straight Line — amortise gain/loss over time with equal increments per accounting period
Interest Hedged — applies to interest-bearing positions with explicit hedge accounting treatment
A randomised method selected per position to avoid systematic bias in revaluation outcomes
tip: REVALUATION.PARAMETER drives multi-method revaluation of FX positions. SPOT method = simply mark to current market rate. STRAIGHT LINE = amortize the FX gain/loss over time. INTEREST / INTEREST HEDGED = applied to interest-rate-sensitive instruments. REBATE = adjust for fees/discounts. Each is appropriate for different instrument types and accounting standards.

q: ADJ posting vs I/O posting in revaluation — what's the difference?
options: No difference — the two terms refer to the same posting style with different naming conventions
ADJ posts only the DIFFERENTIAL amount; I/O reverses yesterday's revaluation and posts today's TOTAL — same net effect, different ledger noise
ADJ is illegal in CEMAC banking regulations whereas I/O is the only permitted revaluation style
I/O is significantly faster than ADJ at high transaction volumes due to its bulk-update characteristics
tip: ADJ (Adjustment) = post only the change vs yesterday (cleaner GL). I/O (Input/Output) = reverse yesterday's full revaluation, then post today's full revaluation (more journal volume but each day stands alone). Choice depends on audit / regulatory preference. Same end balance; very different audit-trail experience.

q: Charge Amortization (e.g. spreading a £900 upfront origination fee over 36 months at £25/month) is configured via which field setting?
options: UPFRONT — recognises the entire fee on the day it is collected without any spreading
ACCRUE.AMORT = AMORT — triggers spreading logic instead of upfront recognition
BATCH — defers recognition to a nightly batch that posts the fee at end-of-day
MANUAL — requires operators to manually post each instalment of the amortisation
tip: Setting ACCRUE.AMORT to AMORT in the charge property tells the engine to spread (amortize) the fee over the contract term — instead of recognizing all £900 on day one. AA.ACCRUAL.FREQUENCY controls 'dripping' cadence. Compliant with IFRS matching principle: income recognized in periods earned, not when cash arrives. Smoother P&L.

q: Drill-down from synthetic (consolidated GL line) to analytical (individual contract record) is supported via…
options: Random walk navigation between report tables, choosing the next drill-down at random for exploration
RE.STAT.BAL.REC reconciliation tool, navigating from a CAL key down through ECB to specific contract records
Manual search across multiple tables using SQL queries crafted by analysts on the fly
Email queries sent to the accounting team who manually walk the data and reply with results
tip: The drill-down 'golden thread' goes Report Line → CAL/CPL key → ECB record → Contract record. RE.STAT.BAL.REC handles the line-by-line reconciliation. Auditors can start at a balance sheet line and follow it all the way down to the specific loans/deposits that drive it — without manual spreadsheet matching.

q: The TRADE-DATED vs VALUE-DATED accounting mode is controlled by which Table?
options: DATES — controls the bank's system date and integrates with HOLIDAY for date-shifting rules
ACCOUNT.PARAMETER
STMT.ENTRY — each entry carries a flag indicating which dating mode applied at posting time
USER — each user's profile carries a default dating mode for transactions they authorise
tip: ACCOUNT.PARAMETER is the global accounting-system control. It switches the engine between Trade-Dated mode (entries booked on transaction date — entries hit GL immediately) and Value-Dated mode (entries treated as CONTINGENT until value date, then converted to actual). The choice affects regulatory reporting and back-dated transaction handling.

q: AA.ARRANGEMENT.ACTIVITY is the central engine for all updates to an Arrangement. Why must changes go through Activities?
options: Display purposes only — Activities are essentially UI metadata for grouping operations on screens
Ensures process integrity, automatic downstream impacts (accounting, limits, notices), and immutable audit trail — direct DB edits would break all of this
Slows transaction processing artificially to give batch jobs higher priority during business hours
A random architectural constraint chosen by the original Temenos design team without a strong reason
tip: If users could free-edit an Arrangement's components, business rules would be silently violated, downstream accounting wouldn't fire, and audit trails would be ambiguous. Forcing every change through an Activity (LENDING-DISBURSE, CHANGE-INTEREST, REPAY, etc.) ensures consistency. Activities have validation, side-effects, and audit baked in. Direct DB edits are forbidden.

q: PAYMENT.ORDER acts as which kind of component in the architecture?
options: A final settlement ledger holding payment instructions before they reach the clearing house
Common integration vehicle that DECOUPLES payment INITIATION from EXECUTION — routes to internal FT or external payment hubs
A customer-information table dedicated specifically to capturing payee details for outgoing payments
An archive layer that retains payment instructions for the regulatory retention period
tip: PAYMENT.ORDER captures the request (ordering party, beneficiary, IBAN/BIC, amount, value date) without immediately executing it. It then routes the order — internal Funds Transfer for own-bank movements, or external Payment Hub/ESB for cross-bank. Source applications stay simple; PAYMENT.ORDER handles formatting, validation, and routing complexity.

q: An AML hook intercepts a payment and the engine pauses to await an external screening response. What status indicates this wait state?
options: Status 200 (OK) — confirming the payment was accepted and is queued for processing in the next batch
Status 649 (asynchronous wait for external response)
Status 500 (Internal Server Error) — the screening engine cannot continue and the operator must retry
Status 404 (Not Found) — the beneficiary is not on any watchlist and the payment may proceed
tip: Status 649 is the dedicated wait-state for asynchronous hooks. The payment engine pauses while an external AML/sanctions engine (OFAC, SDN watchlists) screens the transaction. When the external response arrives (Pass/Fail), the engine resumes — either completing the payment or rejecting it. This prevents bottlenecks: the engine isn't blocked synchronously.

q: PROTECTION.LIMIT enforces what kind of cap?
options: A file-size limit applied to individual payment instruction files before they reach the gateway
Cumulative daily fraud-prevention limits at Customer and External User levels (cap on potentially-fraudulent outflows)
A database row-count limit enforced on the PAYMENT.ORDER table to prevent runaway growth
A randomised quota applied per transaction to obscure spending patterns from external observers
tip: PROTECTION.LIMIT is a fraud guardrail — total daily amounts that can be moved through external channels. If a fraudster gets credentials, this caps their damage. Set at both Customer level (the account holder) and External User level (an internet banking session). Combined with BEN.RISK.RATE (per-beneficiary risk profile) it creates a layered defense against fraudulent disbursements.

q: BEN.RISK.RATE allows the system to…
options: Calculate the beneficiary's credit score using internal scoring data plus external credit bureau lookups
Apply transaction logic per beneficiary risk profile — e.g. tighter limits or enhanced due diligence for high-risk targets
Reveal a customer's internal credit information to other banks during clearing for verification
Apply random risk ratings during testing of the fraud-screening engine in pre-production environments
tip: BEN.RISK.RATE (Beneficiary Risk Rate) lets the bank score each beneficiary and apply differentiated controls. A long-trusted vendor might have no extra friction; a never-seen-before payee might trigger enhanced due diligence, lower PROTECTION.LIMIT, or call-back verification. It's a risk-based approach rather than one-size-fits-all controls.

q: Funds Transfer (FT) handles internal/P&L transfers natively in CBS. What gets generated automatically upon AUTH?
options: A confirmation email sent to the customer summarising the transfer details for their records
STMT.ENTRY for account movements and CATEG.ENTRY for P&L impacts — automated accounting
Random data records inserted for benchmark testing of the transfer processing pipeline
Nothing automatic — operations staff manually post the corresponding entries during EOD review
tip: FT (Funds Transfer) is the engine for internal/own-bank transfers. When the transfer is authorized, the system automatically raises STMT.ENTRY (debit source, credit destination) and CATEG.ENTRY for any fee/commission income hitting P&L. No manual journal entries — transaction-driven accounting in action.

q: CHEQUE.STATUS tracks the lifecycle of a chequebook. Which value indicates the chequebook is awaiting printing?
options: RECEIVED — the chequebook has been printed and is in the bank's possession
SENT FOR PRINTING
ISSUED — the chequebook has been handed to the customer at the branch counter
DESTROYED — the chequebook has been shredded due to spoilage or compliance reasons
tip: CHEQUE.STATUS values include 'Request Received' (customer asked for a book), 'Sent for Printing' (in production), 'Issued' (handed over to customer), etc. The progression is tracked so the bank can answer 'where's my chequebook?' instantly. STOCK.ENTRY and STOCK.REGISTER track the raw blank inventory.

q: PAYMENT.STOP captures stop-payment instructions. Up to how many reasons can be configured?
options: 10 — a single digit limit because reasons are stored in a single character field
99 — a two-digit limit reflecting the field's two-character size for reason codes
Unlimited — reasons are stored in a text field with no enforced upper bound
1 — only a single default reason is supported because most stops are functionally equivalent
tip: PAYMENT.STOP supports up to 99 stop-payment reasons (e.g. Lost, Stolen, Disputed, Duplicate, Funds-insufficient). Reasons can target specific cheque numbers, ranges, or amounts. Real-time validation during clearing rejects any cheque matching a stop instruction — fraud and loss mitigation in the cheque lifecycle.

q: CARD.ISSUE / CARD.TYPE separate the…
options: Card colours and visual branding parameters configured per product type for marketing reasons
PRODUCT definition (CARD.TYPE — defines rules) from the INSTANCE (CARD.ISSUE — a specific card for a specific customer)
Random card-design selection from a fixed library of templates available per product line
Internal data about the card-printing supplier from the BFF instance to the order processing layer
tip: CARD.TYPE = the product (e.g. 'VISA Platinum' with rules: expiry frequency, renewal rules, billing offset). CARD.ISSUE = a specific card issued to a specific customer, linked to one or more accounts. The same Product/Contract pattern as elsewhere: one CARD.TYPE serves many CARD.ISSUE instances. CARD.STATUS tracks state (Issue / Re-issue / Scrap / Cancelled).

q: A single card can be linked to MULTIPLE customer accounts. Which account is designated as the 'primary'?
options: A randomly chosen account selected from the customer's linked accounts at transaction time
A designated primary account for fee debits and drawings
All linked accounts equally — fees split proportionally across them based on balance share
The most recently opened account — the newest account becomes the primary by default
tip: Multi-account linkage lets one card act as both Current and Savings, etc. One account is the PRIMARY — that's where fees debit and where 'default draw' happens. The customer can switch between linked accounts at the ATM/POS as needed. Decoupling cards from accounts gives flexibility without account proliferation.

q: DD.DDI is the central record for Direct Debit Instructions. Outward vs Inward mandates — what's the difference?
options: Both directions are identical and the term 'outward/inward' is used interchangeably in practice
OUTWARD = bank's CUSTOMER authorizes a third party to debit (e.g. utility bill); INWARD = ANOTHER BANK is authorized to collect from our customer
Outward DDIs process faster than Inward ones due to lower validation requirements at clearing
Inward DDIs are illegal in most jurisdictions due to fraud concerns and are not supported
tip: Direct Debits operate in both directions. OUTWARD: your customer signs a mandate authorizing a utility company to collect monthly. INWARD: another bank is authorized to collect from your customer's account. DD.DDI tracks both, including clearing references, status (NEW/ADVISED/ACTIVE), and bank sort codes. Failed DDs route into a retry framework.

q: A Standing Order (STO) is used for…
options: One-time payments scheduled for a specific future date with no recurring component
Recurring fixed or variable transfers (rent, subscriptions, sweeps)
Random one-time transfers used as part of A/B testing for customer engagement campaigns
Manual entries posted by the operator on behalf of customers who lack online banking access
tip: STANDING.ORDER orchestrates periodic transfers — fixed (rent, mortgage payment) or variable (utility bills). Supports 'Credit' STOs that periodically claim fixed amounts from external systems. Failed STOs (insufficient funds) go into a retry framework for resubmission later. 'Set and forget' convenience for customers, automated liquidity management.

q: The limit hierarchy supports up to how many levels?
options: 3 — Global, Product, Customer — the minimal hierarchy supporting risk-policy enforcement
5 — Global, Country, Product, Sub-product, Customer — a typical 5-tier credit governance model
Unlimited — limits can nest arbitrarily deep with no enforced architectural ceiling
tip: LIMIT.REFERENCE and LIMIT support up to 12 hierarchical levels — Global → Product → Sub-product → ... Each child limit is inherently constrained by its parent. So you could have 'Total Country Exposure' as Level 1, then Level 2 by Industry, Level 3 by Customer, Level 4 by Product Class. Real-time aggregation enforces all levels simultaneously.

q: Revolving vs Non-Revolving limits differ in…
options: The visual colour of the limit type on operator screens for at-a-glance recognition
Revolving restores availability upon repayment (like a credit card); Non-Revolving is a one-time cap (utilization permanently reduces available)
The currency the limit is denominated in — Revolving is always local currency; Non-Revolving is always foreign
Randomly assigned semantics at limit-creation time for testing the limit-management engine's resilience
tip: REVOLVING limit (e.g. credit card limit, line of credit): when you use £500 and then pay £300 back, £300 of capacity is restored. NON-REVOLVING (e.g. term loan): the £500 you used reduces your remaining capacity permanently. Choice depends on product type. Defined via LIMIT.PARAMETER configuration.

q: FX.OR.TIME.BAND set to 'TB' enables…
options: Random sizing logic applied to limits when no specific size has been negotiated with the customer
Tenor-based tiering — a single limit record with different caps for different transaction maturities
Cancellation flow allowing limits to be wound down without affecting in-flight transactions
Time-travel logic letting back-dated transactions appear in the limit utilisation history
tip: Time Banding (FX.OR.TIME.BAND = 'TB') lets one limit have descending exposure caps based on transaction tenor: e.g. up to $1M for short-dated, $500K for medium-dated, $100K for long-dated. Encodes the principle that longer maturity = higher risk = stricter cap, all in one consolidated limit record.

q: Liability Groups vs Limit Sharing Groups — both share credit lines, but…
options: Identical mechanisms — the two terms describe the same concept with different naming conventions
Liability Group = linked members share a Group Master credit; Limit Sharing Group = multiple parties draw against one shared facility
Liability Group is used exclusively for car-loan facilities; Limit Sharing Group for all other lending products
Random groupings assigned during testing to verify the system handles multi-party limit sharing correctly
tip: Both manage shared credit across multiple entities (common for corporate groups). Liability Group ties subsidiaries to a Group Master's credit. Limit Sharing Group lets several distinct customers all draw against one shared facility. Together they handle the complex web of corporate borrowing relationships in modern banking.

q: When a transaction exceeds the customer's available limit, the system generates…
options: No notification — the transaction is silently rejected with no override or appeal mechanism
'Unauthorized Overdraft' override message — requires authorized approver to allow it
A customer call routed automatically to the customer-service team for verbal confirmation
A random response chosen between accepting, rejecting, or queuing the transaction
tip: Real-Time Control intercepts every transaction. If the requested amount exceeds the Working Balance + available limit, an 'Unauthorized Overdraft' override is raised. The transaction sits unauthorized until a properly classed officer (OVERRIDE.CLASS) approves. Limit utilization is updated online so the 360-degree view of credit availability is always real-time.

q: STOP.TXN logic in document management blocks…
options: Random transactions selected unpredictably for additional document review during testing cycles
Transaction authorization if required documents are missing or expired
All transactions across all customers and products are blocked for documentation reasons
Customer login attempts when the customer's KYC documents have not yet been uploaded
tip: Document Management uses STOP.TXN to enforce KYC compliance. If a customer's mandatory documents (ID, proof of address, FATCA self-cert, etc.) are marked Missing or Expired in CUST.DOCUMENT / TRANS.DOCUMENT, the system blocks transaction authorization until they're refreshed. Document expiry monitoring happens during COB so issues are flagged proactively.

q: DOCUMENT.CLASS vs DOCUMENT.TYPE — what's the relationship?
options: Identical concepts — used interchangeably depending on the bank's preferred terminology
DOCUMENT.CLASS groups document types into logical buckets (e.g. 'Account Opening Forms'); DOCUMENT.TYPE defines specific instruments within a class
DOCUMENT.CLASS is encrypted at rest while DOCUMENT.TYPE is stored in plain text for fast lookups
Random label distinction without operational consequence in the document-management framework
tip: DOCUMENT.CLASS is the high-level grouping ('Account Opening Forms', 'KYC Refresh', 'Loan Documentation'). DOCUMENT.TYPE is the specific document within the class ('Passport scan', 'Utility bill', 'Salary slip'). DOC.GEN.CONDITION rules say which documents are mandatory per customer profile or product.

q: IM.DOCUMENT.IMAGE stores digital assets like signatures and ID scans. What enables retrieval at high speed?
options: Random file-naming conventions applied at upload time for storage-layer optimisation
IM.IMAGE.TYPE — defines logical storage paths and metadata grouping (KYC, Signatures, etc.)
Brute-force scanning through the storage directory at retrieval time to find matching images
Manual indexing performed by operations staff after each batch of customer-document uploads
tip: IM.DOCUMENT.IMAGE is the digital evidence repository. IM.IMAGE.TYPE categorizes assets (KYC, Signatures, Passport) and defines logical storage paths. IMAGE.APPLICATION maps each digital file to specific records (Customer, Account). When a teller pulls up CUSTOMER 12345, the relevant signature image displays instantly — no paper file retrieval needed.

q: PV.LOAN.CLASSIFICATION supports automated risk categorization. Which state indicates highest risk?
