options: Standard — the default classification applied to all loans at origination by the system
Loss (highest rank — full provisioning required)
Watchlist — early-warning classification applied to loans showing initial deterioration signals
Substandard — applied to loans where collateral coverage has dropped below required thresholds
tip: Asset Classification states (in rank order, lowest risk to highest): Standard → Watchlist → Substandard → Doubtful → Loss. Higher ranks = higher provisioning percentage required. Loss = full write-off. EB.RULE.GATEWAY automatically transitions assets based on Days Past Due (DPD), customer Sector, and other risk criteria. Multi-level option uses 'worst' classification across all customer contracts.

q: IFRS 9 provisioning uses PD (Probability of Default), LGD (Loss Given Default), and LIP (Loss Identification Period). These are inputs to…
options: A randomly seeded statistical ensemble used during stress testing of provisioning calculations
Statistical Expected Credit Loss models, opposed to simpler Standard Percentage-based provisioning
Marketing analyses used by relationship managers to identify customers at risk of attrition
User password complexity rules applied to staff with provisioning approval responsibilities
tip: IFRS 9 requires Expected Credit Loss (ECL) calculations using PD × LGD × LIP times exposure. Compare with the legacy Standard Percentage method (e.g. 'Loss class = 100%, Doubtful = 50%'). The system supports BOTH methods via PV.CALCULATION. ECL is more accurate but data-intensive; Standard% is simpler. Banks often use both for parallel multi-GAAP reporting.

q: TELLER.ID 9999 conventionally identifies what?
options: A specific senior teller designated as the head of cash operations for that branch
The Vault — central cash store (persistent, no daily open/close)
The branch manager's till ID used for override authorisations during cash discrepancies
A randomly assigned numeric identifier for testing till-management workflows
tip: By convention, Till IDs are numbered low; the Vault is given a special ID like 9999. The Vault doesn't go through daily OPEN/CLOSED cycles like Tills — it persists. Tellers transfer cash between the Vault and Tills as operational needs change. STOCK.ENTRY brings new blank instruments into the Vault.

q: TELLER.DENOMINATION tracks…
options: Random sample denomination data used during performance testing of the cash-balancing engine
Per-currency unit definitions (specific notes, coins, multiples/fractions of a unit)
Customer address details captured during cash deposits at the teller counter
User password rotation rules applied to staff who handle cash at the teller windows
tip: For physical cash, the system tracks EXACT denominations. TELLER.DENOMINATION defines which notes/coins exist for each currency (e.g. for EUR: 500/200/100/50/20/10/5 notes; 2/1/0.50/0.20/0.10/0.05/0.02/0.01 coins). DENOM.TYPE classifies assets — Cash, Travelers Cheques, Gift Cheques. TT.STOCK.CONTROL holds real-time counts per Till/Vault.

q: During Till closure, the physical balancing engine reconciles…
options: Random transaction outcomes during stress testing of the till-closure workflow
Counted physical cash against the digital ledger — discrepancies adjusted via Overage/Shortage accounts with override approval
Customer satisfaction scores collected after each in-branch cash transaction is completed
Server uptime metrics correlated with cash-transaction volume during the closure period
tip: Till closure forces the teller to enter physical cash counts. The system compares with the digital expected balance. Any discrepancy must be explained: if short, debit Shortage account; if over, credit Overage account. The override requires approval, ensuring no cash discrepancy hides without explanation. Hardened cash accountability.

q: Why do teller transactions support entering NEGATIVE denomination counts?
options: A random feature toggle used during user-acceptance testing of the teller cash module
To record CHANGE returned to customers (e.g. customer pays £100 for a £73 transaction; teller enters £27 change as negative denominations leaving the Till)
To create fake transaction records for benchmark testing of the cash-balancing engine
Encryption metadata applied to negative entries for additional protection in the audit trail
tip: Real-world transactions involve change. If a customer hands £100 to settle a £73 bill, the teller records +£100 incoming + (-£27) outgoing (the change given back). Negative denominations reflect cash LEAVING the till. The system validates the net amount matches the transaction total. Models real-world cash handling precisely.

q: BASIC.INTEREST holds…
options: Random interest rates assigned per customer for testing the interest-calculation engine
Floating benchmarks (the bank's central interest reference rates)
Customer account balances aggregated across all accounts for portfolio-level reporting
User salary information for operations staff who manage the interest-product catalogue
tip: BASIC.INTEREST is the central reference table for floating interest rates — the bank's house benchmark. Contract interest rates can reference BASIC.INTEREST + a margin (e.g. 'BASIC + 1.5%'). When BASIC.INTEREST changes (e.g. due to central bank rate moves), all linked contracts automatically pick up the new rate — no manual recalculation across thousands of loans.

q: PERIODIC.INTEREST captures…
options: Random sample data used during performance testing of the rate-calculation pipeline
Periodic index rates (e.g. LIBOR, EURIBOR, SOFR) used as floating references for loans
Calendar configuration data integrated with the DATES table for business-day calculations
User profile preferences for which interest-product types they specialise in supporting
tip: PERIODIC.INTEREST holds market index rates — LIBOR, SOFR, EURIBOR, etc. — that products reference for floating-rate calculations. The system fetches the index value as of the rate-fix date. Combined with BASIC.INTEREST (internal bank rates) it covers both market-driven and bank-driven floating rate products.

q: LEVEL vs BAND tiered interest — what's the difference?
options: Identical mechanisms — Level and Band are interchangeable names for the same tiering logic
LEVEL = single rate applies to the WHOLE balance based on which threshold is hit; BAND = blended rate where each balance SLICE gets a different rate
LEVEL is reserved for credit balances; BAND only applies to debit balances on overdraft accounts
Random naming distinction applied during early-stage testing of the tiering implementation
tip: LEVEL tier: balance £100K → entire balance earns 2%; balance £200K → entire balance earns 3%. BAND tier: first £100K at 1%, next £100K at 2%, next £100K at 3% — blended effective rate. Banks use LEVEL for simpler 'reward bands'; BAND for tiered products like progressive savings or progressive overdraft. Configured per product.

q: Day Basis rules (360/360, 366/360, etc.) control…
options: Random number generation used for adding noise to interest calculations during stress testing
How the day count is computed for interest (numerator days / denominator days per year) — different conventions per market/product
Holiday-day adjustment rules consumed by the COB scheduler at the boundary of each business year
Server uptime statistics correlated with interest-accrual completeness during the daily batch
tip: Day Basis conventions: 360/360 (each month = 30 days, year = 360); 366/360 (actual days/360); 365/365 (actual/actual); ACT/360 etc. Different markets and products use different conventions. Money market often uses ACT/360; corporate loans might use 30/360. The Day Basis directly affects accrued interest amounts — critical for precise yield calculations.

q: Negative interest rates on credit balances are supported by…
options: A hard-coded floor at zero — negative rates are not supported in the architecture and would require code changes
Dynamic Spread Management — banks can configure negative rates in BASIC.INTEREST / PERIODIC.INTEREST
Manual entry by operations staff to override automated rate-application logic for negative scenarios
Negative rates are not supported anywhere in the system; rates must always be positive integers in basis points
tip: In modern banking (esp. post-2014 in EU), interest rates can go negative — banks pay to hold large deposits at central banks, and may pass this on to large depositors. The system handles negative rates natively in BASIC.INTEREST. Contract rate = BASIC + margin; if BASIC is -0.5% and margin is 0.2%, the effective rate is -0.3% (the depositor pays the bank).

q: GENERAL.CHARGE acts as…
options: A login authentication portal for staff who manage fee structures across the product catalogue
Central hub grouping disparate fee types (interest-related addons, ledger fees, transaction-based charges) into one tariff structure
Random data records inserted for testing the fee-calculation engine's behaviour under load
User data tracking which staff members have charged which fees for compliance audit purposes
tip: GENERAL.CHARGE consolidates the bank's fee structure. Instead of fees being scattered across many tables, they're grouped into one tariff that links to products via CONDITION.PRIORITY. Different customer segments (Staff, VIP, Standard) get different tariffs automatically. Centralization prevents 'rogue fees' and supports clean fee reporting/reconciliation.

q: FT.COMMISSION.TYPE supports calculation in which forms?
options: Only flat amounts — the framework does not support percentage-based or tiered fee calculations
Flat amounts, percentages, or tiered (Level/Band) structures with built-in minimum/maximum caps
Only percentage-based fees — flat fees are deprecated in the current architecture for simplicity
Randomly generated fee amounts within configurable bounds for testing fee-collection workflows
tip: FT.COMMISSION.TYPE handles fee calculation flexibility: FLAT (£10 fixed), PERCENTAGE (0.2% of amount), TIERED (different rates per balance/amount band). With built-in MINIMUM and MAXIMUM caps — e.g. 'percentage 0.5% with £5 minimum and £50 maximum'. Configurable enough to model virtually any real-world fee structure.

q: CONDITION.PRIORITY enables…
options: Random ordering of tariffs applied to accounts to test fee-calculation distribution patterns
Auto-application of specific tariffs to accounts based on customer attributes (Sector, Industry, Residence) — preferential pricing by segment
Customer rank assignment for VIP-status determination used by relationship managers
Random selection of tariffs from a predefined library each time a fee event occurs
tip: CONDITION.PRIORITY is the rules engine for applying preferential tariffs. Different customer attributes trigger different tariffs: VIPs get the VIP fee structure; Staff get the Staff one; standard customers get the standard one. No manual assignment — driven by attributes that are already maintained on the customer record.

q: The 5-Phase COB engine runs phases in which order?
options: Random execution ordering chosen at runtime to prevent gaming of the EOD job scheduling
Application (A) → System Wide (S) → Reporting (R) → Start of Day (D) → Online (O)
Online jobs run first to clear interactive workload before batch processing begins each evening
Reverse alphabetical order based on the technical job names defined in the COB scheduler configuration
tip: 5-Phase COB: A (Application — module-specific work like loan accruals), S (System Wide — global tasks like FX revaluation), R (Reporting — generate trial balance, statements), D (Start of Day — roll forward DATES table, prepare for next day), O (Online — restore online services). The strict order ensures dependent jobs always have the data they need from earlier phases.

q: The T24 Service Manager (tSM) and Service Agents (tSA) work together to…
options: Random task assignment from a queue of available batch jobs each EOD cycle
Multi-threaded batch execution — tSM launches and monitors multiple tSA agents that share the workload
Customer-service routing during business hours when batch processing is not active
Backup orchestration coordinating database snapshots across multiple data centres during EOD
tip: tSM (T24 Service Manager) orchestrates batch processing. tSAs (Service Agents) are the worker threads that actually run jobs. tSM distributes the workload across multiple tSAs so a single-threaded job becomes multi-threaded. AGENT.STATUS and COB.PROGRESS enquiries provide real-time visibility into how many agents are running, what percentage complete, etc.

q: Why does the COB engine support '24/7' online while batch is still running?
options: Marketing claim — the architecture does not actually support 24/7 ops while batch runs concurrently
Online transactions are accepted for the 'Next Day' while batch closes the current one — no full system downtime
Random sample transactions are accepted during COB to test online behaviour under reduced load
Impossible — banks always require complete downtime during COB and the claim is aspirational only
tip: Modern banks can't shut down for hours of batch. The COB engine decouples online activity from batch closing: tellers can keep taking transactions (booked to the 'Next Day') while batch closes the current day in parallel. EB.EOD.ERROR catches batch failures; critical errors block the date rollover. The customer never notices COB happening.

q: ACCT.ACCRUAL and GROUP.CAPITALISATION are batch processes that…
options: Random sampling of accounts to verify accrual calculations during regulatory audit cycles
Calculate daily interest accruals and execute scheduled interest capitalizations (debiting/crediting accounts on the configured frequency)
Customer onboarding workflows triggered nightly to process pending KYC reviews and approvals
User logout enforcement to clear stale sessions across the user base before the next business day
tip: During COB, ACCT.ACCRUAL computes the daily interest on every relevant account/contract (adding to ACC balance). GROUP.CAPITALISATION executes scheduled capitalization (moving ACC balance into actual posted entries — debiting customer's account for loan interest, crediting for deposit interest). Frequency is configurable: monthly, quarterly, etc.

q: The automated testing framework supports Model Bank. What is Model Bank?
options: A small bank used as a reference deployment for verifying that the testing framework works
Pre-built standardized test data injected into versions to avoid repetitive manual setup during testing cycles
A real-world bank's anonymised data used as a comparison baseline for performance benchmarks
Random sample data generated nightly to simulate realistic production-volume testing scenarios
tip: Model Bank is a vendor-shipped set of standardized test data + configurations. QA teams can run regression tests against it to validate that new releases don't break baseline functionality. Combined with the trace tooling (number of I/Os, subroutine calls, elapsed time), it lets QA validate both functional correctness AND performance impacts before production deployment.

q: EB.EOD.ERROR captures…
options: Random error messages generated during chaos testing of the COB scheduler's resilience
All COB job failures — central audit trail for troubleshooting; critical errors prevent rollover
Customer-facing errors raised during EOD that need to be cleared before next-day operations begin
Network errors only, captured separately from application-level errors which go elsewhere
tip: Every COB job's failures are logged in EB.EOD.ERROR. Routine warnings might allow COB to proceed; CRITICAL errors block the date rollover until resolved (preventing a corrupted close). The operations team reviews EB.EOD.ERROR each morning to identify and fix any issues. Without this central error trail, troubleshooting overnight failures would be impossible.

q: AASUSPENSE is described as an 'internal intermediate balance'. What advantage over a physical Suspense account?
options: Marketing claim — AASUSPENSE has no real performance advantage over a physical suspense account
Improves performance by handling temporary postings internally — avoids creating a high-volume physical suspense account
It is significantly more expensive to operate than a physical suspense account in production
Random naming chosen to differentiate the internal balance from external suspense accounts
tip: Some intra-day or intra-transaction postings need a temporary 'parking' account before final disposition. Physical suspense accounts would handle MILLIONS of these intra-day postings — bloating storage and slowing queries. AASUSPENSE handles them as INTERNAL intermediate balances within the engine, never persisting as discrete physical entries. Better performance, cleaner ledger.

q: RE.STAT.REP.LINE defines…
options: Random selection of report lines used during automated testing of the consolidation engine
Mapping between CRF/CRB report lines and the underlying CAL/CPL keys they aggregate
User-facing UI lines displayed on the reporting dashboard for visual identification
Random sample data injected into reports for benchmarking the rendering performance
tip: Financial statements are built from report lines (e.g. 'Total Cash and Cash Equivalents' on the balance sheet). RE.STAT.REP.LINE defines which CAL keys roll up into each report line. Combined with RE.STAT.REQUEST (the report itself) and RE.STAT.COLUMN.TYPE (column configuration), it lets admins design custom regulatory reports without writing code.

q: An 'undefined item' — a balance whose key matches no defined report line — goes to which line?
options: Random allocation across visible report lines, distributing undefined items evenly for balance
Line 9999 (Catch-All) — ensures total report still balances against underlying GL
Auto-deletion of the underlying entry to prevent it from showing on any report at all
Customer-facing email notification asking them to clarify the nature of the unmapped item
tip: Without a catch-all, missing line mappings would produce a report total that doesn't equal the GL — accounting equivalent of money disappearing. Line 9999 absorbs anything that wasn't explicitly mapped, so reports always tie. Reviewing Line 9999's contents tells you what new products introduced keys that need explicit mapping in next report iteration.

q: Eligibility failure can be configured to trigger an automated…
options: A complete system shutdown until compliance has reviewed the rejected eligibility result
Change Product activity — moving the arrangement to a default product variant (e.g. Minor → Standard at age 18)
Random product re-selection for the customer to choose another product from the catalogue
Customer dismissal — the customer's profile is automatically deactivated for the failed product
tip: When eligibility fails, instead of just rejecting the customer, the system can automatically initiate a Change Product activity. Classic example: a customer's Minor Account auto-transitions to a Standard Adult Account on their 18th birthday — same account number, new product behind it, no back-office workflow. Customer experience: seamless aging-up.

q: The Custom Logic Hooks property class is called…
options: RANDOM.API — a randomised endpoint used for chaos testing during pre-production verification
ACTIVITY.API — links user-defined Pre-routines, Post-routines, and Validation routines to specific arrangement events
AC.HOOK — an accounting-only hook framework distinct from the broader activity-extension mechanism
ROUTINE — a generic catch-all term that doesn't specifically identify the hook framework
tip: ACTIVITY.API is the property class for extending AA arrangements with custom logic. Pre-routines run before activity commits (validation, external API calls); Post-routines run after (notifications, cascades); Validation routines enforce custom rules. This is the AA-equivalent of Projection-level Plugin hooks — but tied to Activities rather than Projection events.

q: What does TRACKING.ONLY mean in Activity Mapping configuration?
options: A user-tracking module that logs which operators consult the activity-mapping configuration most often
Mapping rules live at the PRODUCT level and cannot be overridden at the individual ARRANGEMENT instance level — consistency enforced
Random rule generation applied during testing of the activity-mapping engine's resilience
Customer-tracking metadata that follows each customer through their interactions with mapped activities
tip: TRACKING.ONLY ensures mapping rules (e.g. 'transaction code 850 = LENDING-DISBURSE') are defined ONCE on the product and apply to all arrangements derived from it. You can't override per-customer (which would lead to inconsistent accounting per contract). The product is the authoritative source — change it there and all arrangements track the new rule.

q: AA.PRD.DES.PAYMENT.SCHEDULE supports calculation types Annuity, Linear, and Actual. Which is used for bullet loans?
options: Annuity — constant total payment with declining interest portion over the loan's life
Linear — constant principal payment with declining interest portion over the loan's life
Actual — calculated each period (suits bullet structures where principal pays only at maturity)
A random calculation type selected at origination from a pool of available templates
tip: Annuity = constant total payment (variable principal/interest split). Linear = constant principal payment (declining total). Actual = calculated each period based on actual balance and rate. Bullet loans (principal only at maturity) use Actual because there's no constant payment formula — interest accrues each period, principal repays at the end. Configured per product.

q: ACCOUNT.STATEMENT defaults its frequency on creation based on…
options: Random frequency assignment used during testing of the statement-generation engine's resilience
STMT.GEN.CONDITION rules engine — driven by customer attributes (Sector, Industry, Product)
Vendor whim — the CBS vendor selects default frequencies based on their own preferences
User-typed values entered by operations staff for each new account during onboarding workflow
tip: When an account is created, ACCOUNT.STATEMENT is automatically instantiated. STMT.GEN.CONDITION rules look at the customer's profile and decide the default frequency: e.g. Retail = monthly, Corporate = daily, Tax-sensitive = annual + monthly. Customers can request different cycles, but defaults reduce setup work — most accounts get the right cycles automatically.

q: CRB (Central Reporting Balance) reports differ from CRF reports in…
options: Identical reports under different names; the naming distinction is a Temenos legacy holdover
CRB drills down to underlying ACCOUNTS and CONTRACTS — granular analytical view; CRF is the higher-level CAL/CPL key view
CRB reports run monthly while CRF reports run daily during the EOD batch processing cycle
Random sampling of reports chosen at runtime for benchmark performance comparisons
tip: CRF (Central Reporting File) reports = key-level (CAL/CPL) — the synthetic view (totals by Consolidation Key dimensions). CRB (Central Reporting Balance) reports = the analytical breakdown showing which specific accounts/contracts comprise each key total. CRF for management summaries; CRB for auditor drill-down.

q: Currency split options in CRF/CRB reports include Local, Foreign, and All. What's the use case for 'Foreign' only?
options: Random selection of currencies for testing the FX revaluation engine under varied conditions
Identifying the bank's TOTAL exposure to non-local currencies — useful for FX risk and revaluation analysis
Customer-facing reports showing only foreign accounts to non-resident customers in their statements
Random data used during stress testing of the currency-handling logic in the reporting pipeline
tip: Reports can be filtered by currency type. 'Foreign' shows only non-local-currency positions — perfect for FX risk dashboards, revaluation impact analysis, and regulatory reporting on FX exposures. 'Local' shows only home-currency positions; 'All' aggregates everything. Section-wise currency splits enable detailed analysis without separate reports.

q: Bucket-level revaluation operates at which granularity?
options: Random per-record buckets used for performance benchmark testing of the revaluation engine
Each Asset Type within a Consolidation Key — adjusts buckets to current LCY equivalent
Per-customer buckets used for relationship-manager risk reporting at the customer level
Per-branch buckets reflecting local rate revaluations at the branch's chosen reference currency
tip: Revaluation isn't applied per-transaction (too granular) or per-currency (too coarse). It operates at the BUCKET level within the CRF: each Asset Type under each Consolidation Key gets revalued to current LCY equivalent. Position Balancing then ensures the non-contingent reporting base ties to the system-wide Position file for each currency — internal consistency check.

q: Unrealized P&L from revaluation can be posted to which categories?
options: P&L only — the only legal posting category for any unrealized revaluation gains or losses
Profit/Loss OR Equity (e.g. IAS 39 hedge accounting may book to OCI/Equity rather than P&L)
Random allocation between Profit/Loss and other categories during benchmark testing periods
Customer accounts directly — the gain/loss is allocated to customers' personal accounts as a fee
tip: International standards (IAS 39, IFRS 9) sometimes require unrealized P&L to be booked to EQUITY (other comprehensive income / OCI) rather than running through the P&L statement. For instance, hedged positions or available-for-sale securities may route there. The system supports both, configured via REVALUATION.PARAMETER. Critical for compliant multi-GAAP reporting.

q: Received-Not-Earned balances are managed in which category range?
options: Random internal categories chosen at runtime depending on the available accounting buckets
Internal categories 10000-19999 — bank's internal accounts (not customer-visible)
Customer-facing P&L categories 0-99 — exposed directly in customers' year-end statements
Random category 20000-29999, selected at runtime for testing fee-amortisation workflows
tip: When a fee is received upfront but earned over time, the unrecognized portion sits in an internal category in the 10000-19999 range. As periods pass, AA.ACCRUAL.FREQUENCY drips the earned portion into P&L. Customers don't see these internal categories; they're for the bank's own ledger management. EB.ACCRUAL.PARAM controls the drip cadence.

q: Why is daily mismatch monitoring crucial?
options: A cosmetic monitoring activity for compliance documentation that doesn't actually catch issues
Errors discovered immediately are tractable; errors compounding over weeks become unwinable — RE.STAT.MISMATCH traps them daily
UI display purposes — daily mismatch banners give operators something to glance at during their morning routine
Random sampling check applied during stress testing of the reconciliation engine's accuracy
tip: If the Virtual GL drifts from underlying contracts by £0.01 on Monday, it's trivial to find Monday's failing transaction. If undetected for a month, you'd have to investigate millions of transactions to find the culprit. Daily mismatch monitoring keeps the investigation tractable. RE.STAT.MISMATCH for high-level detection; RE.STAT.BAL.REC for granular drill-down.

q: CARD.STATUS values include 'Scrap' — what does it mean?
options: A poor risk rating assigned to the card based on the customer's recent transaction history
The physical card is destroyed (e.g. ATM ate it, mailed back damaged) — removed from active inventory
Cancellation initiated by the customer through online banking after the card has been compromised
A random status used during testing of the card-lifecycle engine's transition handling
tip: Card lifecycle includes physical events. SCRAP = physical card destroyed (ATM swallowed it, returned damaged, post office lost it). CANCELLED = card administratively closed (customer left bank, account closed). ISSUE = active in customer's hands. RE-ISSUE = replacement sent. Tracking these states lets the bank manage physical inventory + know which plastic is in the wild.

q: DD.PARAMETER controls…
options: Random configuration values inserted during stress testing of the Direct Debit engine
System-wide DD configuration — defaults for new mandates, retry rules, country-specific clearing settings
Customer salary information for direct-debit eligibility scoring against income thresholds
User passwords for staff authorised to manage Direct Debit mandates on customer behalf
tip: DD.PARAMETER is the central config for Direct Debit behavior. It defines defaults for new mandates (how many retries on failure, retry interval), clearing-house specific rules (SEPA vs UK BACS vs local schemes), and default messaging templates. Per-mandate specifics live in DD.DDI; PARAMETER governs the system-wide defaults.

q: If a STO fails because of insufficient funds, what happens?
options: The mandate is permanently cancelled and the customer must re-establish it from scratch
Captured in a retry recycler for configurable online or daily resubmission
The customer is fined a non-trivial amount for the failed direct-debit attempt during their next statement cycle
Random retry intervals applied to obscure direct-debit attempt patterns from external observers
tip: Failed STOs (most common reason: insufficient funds) aren't lost — they go into a retry framework. The bank can configure how aggressively to retry (e.g. hourly for 4 hours, then daily for 3 days). This is critical for customer experience: a temporary £20 shortfall shouldn't permanently break your rent payment STO. Retry until success or escalation.

q: LIMIT.DAILY.OS records…
options: Random sample limit utilisation snapshots used for testing the reporting engine's performance
Daily outstanding limit snapshots — supports historical limit-utilization reporting and trend analysis
User login frequency data correlated with limit-utilization decisions across the workforce
Operating system version metadata for the servers hosting the limit-management module
tip: LIMIT.DAILY.OS captures end-of-day outstanding utilization per limit. Useful for: trend analysis ('Is exposure growing?'), regulatory reporting (peak utilization), credit committee reviews ('what utilization did this customer hit last quarter?'). Without daily snapshots, only current utilization would be queryable; historical patterns would be invisible.

q: DOCUMENT.REQUIRED records…
options: Random sample required-document lists used during testing of the document-management workflow
Which documents are mandatory per customer/contract type — derived from DOC.GEN.CONDITION rules
User passwords for staff authorised to manage document requirements at the customer level
Customer photographs captured at onboarding for compliance with KYC photo requirements
tip: DOC.GEN.CONDITION rules say 'corporate customers in Sector 4500 (Pharma) require Trade License + Drug Manufacturing Permit + Authorised Signatories letter'. DOCUMENT.REQUIRED records realize these rules per specific customer/contract. STOP.TXN enforces them — blocks transactions if any required doc is missing or expired.

q: Daily batch automatically re-evaluates provisioning if…
options: Random sample re-evaluation applied during testing of the provisioning engine's robustness
Balances fluctuate OR asset classifications change — recalculates required provisions and posts adjustments
Provisions never get re-evaluated automatically; manual review is required for any provisioning change
Customer-requested re-evaluations triggered through the bank's online portal at any time
tip: Provisions aren't static. If an account moves from Standard to Watchlist classification, more provisioning is needed. If balances grow, absolute provision amount changes. Daily batch reads PV.PROFILE and recalculates per PV.CALCULATION rules — posting any required adjustment entries (debit P&L expense, credit provision liability). Keeps provisioning current automatically.

q: Marketing Exchange Profit/Loss is captured by the system when…
options: Always — every FX transaction captures profit/loss regardless of the relationship between offered and treasury rates
The OFFERED exchange rate differs from the TREASURY (mid) rate — bank profits/loses on the spread
Never — Marketing Exchange Profit/Loss is a deprecated concept no longer captured in modern systems
Random times — the system samples a subset of transactions and only captures profit/loss for those
tip: Banks make money on FX spreads at the teller. The teller might offer 1 USD = 0.83 EUR while the underlying treasury rate is 0.85. The system automatically books the spread (0.02 × amount) as Marketing Exchange Profit. Conversely, if marketing rate is more generous than treasury, the bank books a loss. This automation captures the FX spread revenue stream without manual tracking.

q: Daily, Average, or Min/Max value-dated balances are options for…
options: Random selection of balance methods to test the interest-calculation engine under varied conditions
The interest calculation basis — different products use different balance methods (e.g. credit cards use Average Daily Balance)
Reporting layouts that display balance information in different visual styles to operators
Customer-scoring inputs used by relationship managers to gauge a customer's account engagement
tip: Different products compute interest on different balance methods. Credit cards typically use Average Daily Balance (sum balances each day, divide by days in period). Savings accounts might use Daily (point-in-time). Some products use Minimum Balance during the period (to discourage end-of-period gaming). The Interest Engine supports all these methods configurably.

q: TRANSACTION.CHARGE captures fees tied to…
options: Customer login attempts — fees are charged per failed authentication to discourage brute-force attacks
Specific transactions (e.g. fee per wire transfer, per ATM withdrawal abroad, per cheque issued)
Random fees applied during testing of the fee-collection engine's resilience under unexpected loads
Account opening events captured as a single one-time fee for new customer onboarding workflows
tip: TRANSACTION.CHARGE configures per-transaction fees — a fee that triggers when a specific transaction type occurs. £25 per international wire, £2 per non-bank ATM, £0.50 per cheque. The fee is calculated and posted automatically as part of transaction processing — no manual fee entry, no leakage, no inconsistency between branches.

q: JOB.PROGRESS enquiry shows…
options: Random sample COB metrics shown to operators on their dashboards during off-business hours
Real-time completion percentage of individual COB jobs — operators can see progress mid-batch
User progress metrics showing how quickly operators clear their daily queue of pending overrides
Customer-engagement scores calculated nightly based on the prior day's transaction patterns
tip: JOB.PROGRESS is the operations team's window into running COB. It shows current job, % complete, estimated time remaining. AGENT.STATUS shows the health of each tSA worker. COB.PROGRESS shows the overall percentage. Together they let ops monitor a 4-hour overnight batch in real time — and predict when online services can resume the next day.

q: The automated testing framework's 'trace' feature records…
options: Random data captured during testing of the trace subsystem's accuracy and overhead
Number of I/Os, calls to subroutines, total elapsed time — performance metrics per test
User-click patterns captured during user-acceptance testing of new operator-facing features
Customer-response data captured during pre-production testing of new customer-facing screens
tip: Trace captures the technical performance of each test run: how many disk reads/writes the operation triggered, how many sub-routine calls it made, total elapsed CPU time. Developers analyze these to identify performance regressions. A change that doubles I/O count is suspicious even if functionally correct — it might blow up COB performance under production volume.

q: Posting Restriction codes 90-99 are reserved for…
options: Random restriction codes used during testing of the closure workflow's edge-case handling
Closure flags — prevent unauthorized debits/credits once an account closure is initiated
Customer-dispute resolution codes assigned when a closure is contested by the account holder
Random uses determined at runtime by the closure workflow's internal logic for testing purposes
tip: Codes 0-89 are general posting restrictions (Account Frozen, Deceased, etc.). 90-99 are RESERVED for the closure workflow. When ACCOUNT.CLOSURE starts, the system applies a 90-99 restriction; this is structurally distinct from operational restrictions and is final. Once 90-99 applies, the account is in unwind mode — only closure-related postings can occur.

q: AA.ACCOUNT.DETAILS holds…
options: Random AA-related fields aggregated for testing the account-detail subsystem under varied loads
Account-related fields specific to an AA arrangement — bridges generic AA structure to ACCOUNT-class behavior
Customer email contact information specific to the AA arrangement's primary owner
User session data for operators currently viewing the AA arrangement's detail screens
tip: AA arrangements can implement many product types (loans, deposits, accounts). When the arrangement IS an account, AA.ACCOUNT.DETAILS holds account-specific fields (e.g. statement frequency, overdraft features). It's the bridge between the generic AA framework and the specific ACCOUNT behaviors customers expect. Internal — most users never see it.

q: PV.PROFILE defines…
options: Random profile data used during testing of the provisioning engine's resilience under varied conditions
The provisioning calculation profile — which method (Standard%/IFRS9 statistical), which inputs, how to post entries
Customer profile data captured during onboarding for KYC compliance with regional regulations
User salary information for staff who manage provisioning calculations across the loan portfolio
tip: PV.PROFILE is the per-product-or-segment provisioning configuration. It specifies: which calculation method to use (simple % or statistical IFRS 9 with PD/LGD/LIP inputs), which categories to debit/credit, what frequency. Different products often need different profiles (mortgages vs unsecured personal loans have different risk dynamics). Centralizing in PV.PROFILE keeps provisioning consistent.

q: AC.PRE.CLOSURE.DETAILS is populated by…
options: A customer-service representative entering the pre-closure data manually during the closure call
Pre-closure simulation — recalculates final interest/charges, identifies any blocking overrides, all WITHOUT affecting live balances
Random data generation applied during testing of the closure workflow's edge-case handling
User-typed values entered by operations staff for each closure based on their judgement
tip: Before committing a closure, AC.PRE.CLOSURE.DETAILS runs a SIMULATION. It computes: final accrued interest to date, any unbilled charges, the exact payout amount, any blocking issues (overrides not yet approved, missing documents). The branch officer reviews this. If anything's wrong, fix it before initiating REQUEST.CLOSURE. No partial closures, no surprises.

q: Why is the CURR.NO audit field designed to monotonically increase?
options: Random number generation used to obscure version-tracking patterns from external observers
Version tracking — CURR.NO=1 is the original input, CURR.NO=2 is the first amendment, etc. Combined with HIS suffix gives chronological audit
Customer-count metadata for analytics about how many customers have been affected by each record
User score metrics derived from the number of records a user has authorised over time
tip: Every record's CURR.NO starts at 1 and increments with each authorized amendment. The HIS file's suffixes (;1, ;2, ;3) align with these. So 'show me CUSTOMER 123456 at CURR.NO=4' fetches the specific historical state. Combined with INPUTTER/AUTHORISER/DATE.TIME, it forms a complete forensic timeline — required for SOX, Basel, and similar standards.

q: The 'Stateful Data Mapping' in Composite Screens uses SELECT.FROM to…
options: Random selection of data sources used during testing of the composite-screen orchestration
Pass selection criteria (e.g. a Customer ID from a primary frame) to other tabs/frames automatically — preserves context across the workspace
Filter customers based on their KYC status to ensure compliance during screen interactions
Login authentication metadata propagated across frames to maintain the user's session state
tip: Composite Screens divide the browser into multiple frames. SELECT.FROM and Enquiry Mapping pass the CONTEXT (e.g. the selected Customer ID) from one frame to another — so when you click a customer in the search frame, the detail frame automatically shows that customer's accounts, the third frame shows their last 10 transactions, etc. No re-keying; everything stays in sync.

q: Role-Based Home Pages combine which elements?
options: Random combinations of UI elements assembled during testing of the home-page renderer
Banners, menus, real-time operational dashboards into a unified landing page tailored to a specific role (Teller, Supervisor)
Customer-account summaries for each account a customer holds with the bank as the entry point
Random sample data used during the rendering benchmark for the role-based dashboard subsystem
tip: When a Teller logs in, they see a Teller-specific home page: prominent buttons for cash transactions, quick-search for customers, real-time queue of pending overrides. A Supervisor sees a different home page — dashboards for branch performance, pending authorizations awaiting their approval. Role-Based Home Pages compose all this from EB.COMPOSITE.SCREEN + dashboards + menus.

q: The DE.OUTWARD routine and DE.PRINT pair handle…
options: Customer outreach campaigns triggered automatically based on transaction patterns and outreach rules
Outbound transaction advice formatting (DE.OUTWARD via DE.FORMAT.PRINT) + delivery to physical printer or system disk (DE.PRINT)
Random sample outputs generated during testing of the print-formatting engine under load
Customer-email scheduling for marketing campaigns coordinated through the operations dashboard
tip: When a transaction is authorized that requires customer notification (a deal slip, confirmation, advice), DE.OUTWARD formats the raw data into human-readable layout (per DE.FORMAT.PRINT). DE.PRINT then dispatches to physical printers OR a system disk hold queue (&HOLD&) for digital review. Banner pages use DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER data to route printouts to the correct internal department.

q: AUTO.NEXT.REVIEW.DATE on a CUSTOMER record automates…
options: Random review schedule chosen at runtime depending on workforce availability that day
KYC re-review scheduling — system-calculated next review date based on risk profile (higher risk = more frequent review)
Random tracking of customer interactions for analytics about customer engagement patterns
User score updates correlated with how diligently they conduct KYC reviews across the workforce
tip: KYC isn't a one-off — high-risk customers need re-review (e.g. annually); standard customers maybe every 3 years. AUTO.NEXT.REVIEW.DATE is computed by rules based on risk profile. The system flags upcoming reviews during COB; staff get alerts. MANUAL.NEXT.REVIEW.DATE lets staff override if a special situation requires earlier review.

q: CR.CONTACT.LOG provides…
options: Random data captured during testing of the customer-contact-log subsystem under varied conditions
Interaction history — every customer touchpoint logged for 360-degree relationship view (calls, meetings, complaints, sales attempts)
Random sample data inserted nightly for benchmark testing of the contact-log query performance
Login timestamp data tracking when each user accessed the customer-relationship management module
tip: CR.CONTACT.LOG is the CRM-like interaction history. Every customer touchpoint — phone call (logged by call center), branch visit, email, complaint, sales pitch — gets a CR.CONTACT.LOG entry. Combined with profile changes (tracked in $HIS), this gives the relationship manager a TRUE 360-degree view. Helps avoid embarrassing situations like calling a customer who complained yesterday.

q: MANDATORY.FIELD in a VERSION (Projection) does what compared to the DDD field's mandatory flag?
options: Identical behaviours — MANDATORY.FIELD and the DDD mandatory flag are interchangeable in the framework
Lets a Projection make an OPTIONAL DDD field MANDATORY for this specific UI — without changing the underlying DDD
Random selection of fields that get marked as mandatory during testing of the field-validation engine
Encrypts the field at rest as an additional security measure when marked as mandatory in a projection
tip: The DDD says 'this field is optional in general'. A specific Projection (e.g. CORPORATE-CUSTOMER-OPEN) may need it to be MANDATORY for THIS role/scenario. The MANDATORY.FIELD override in the Projection enforces this without modifying the underlying DDD. Other Projections on the same Table see the original optional status. Per-role rule customization.

q: REPGEN's 4-stage processing engine produces…
options: Random reports generated during testing of the report-engine's resilience and output quality
High-volume custom reports — Create → Source → Sort → Output stages compile and execute custom BASIC routines
Customer-facing reports delivered through the online-banking portal for retail customers
User reports tracking each operator's daily productivity for management-reporting purposes
tip: REPGEN is the BASIC-routine-based report engine. The four stages are: CREATE (compile the report definition), SOURCE (extract data from tables), SORT (order it as configured), OUTPUT (format and write to printer/disk). It's used for very high-volume reports that ENQUIRY isn't optimized for (millions of records). REPGEN.CREATE captures the definition.

q: Output retention (purging) is governed by which controls?
options: Random retention periods chosen at runtime based on disk-space availability at the data centre
HOLD.CONTROL with configurable retention periods — old printed reports purge automatically to maintain server performance
Customer-requested retention timelines for their personal statements through the online portal
User passwords with retention rules applied for compliance with password-rotation requirements
tip: Printed reports stack up. HOLD.CONTROL defines retention periods per report type — e.g. tellers' end-of-day reports kept 30 days; regulatory reports 7 years. After retention, old reports are purged from &HOLD& disk space. Keeps server storage manageable. Banks configure retention based on regulatory requirements + operational needs.

q: Banner pages on physical print output are generated automatically using metadata from…
options: Random sample user/department records selected for testing the banner-page renderer under load
USER and DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER records — ensuring prints reach the correct internal department
Customer photographs displayed on banner pages for visual identification by the printer attendant
Server log metadata for monitoring print-job completion across the bank's printer fleet
tip: In a big bank, the printer prints reports for dozens of departments. Each printout gets a BANNER PAGE on top showing destination — pulled from USER and DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER records. So when the printer attendant looks at the stack, they know exactly which department each report is for. Distribution becomes automatic. Saves operations time and prevents misdelivered confidential reports.

q: EB.GC.ACTIVE and EB.GC.PARAMETER are part of which framework?
options: A randomised pricing framework with no specific architectural role beyond test instrumentation
Validation engine — Active rules, parameters, constraints managed centrally; supports Errors (block) vs Overrides (warn-approve)
Customer-relationship management framework dedicated to staff who manage VIP customers
User-management framework for adding and removing staff accounts in the operations team
tip: The Generic Constraints (GC) framework is the central validation engine. EB.GC.CONSTRAINTS = constraint definitions; EB.GC.ACTIVE = which are currently active; EB.GC.PARAMETER = configuration knobs. Constraints can be Errors (hard block) or Overrides (soft warn; approved by OVERRIDE.CLASS). Supports rule cumulative or hierarchical evaluation (User > Application > Group priority).

q: Group Limits and Liability Groups serve…
options: Random risk-pool calculations used during testing of the credit-aggregation engine's accuracy
Aggregating credit exposure across complex corporate webs — a parent and its subsidiaries share consolidated credit lines
Customer-dating preferences tracked by relationship managers for VIP-customer scheduling purposes
Random groupings of customers used during A/B testing of marketing campaigns and outreach
tip: Corporate banking deals with complex group structures: a parent has 5 subsidiaries; collectively they want a £50M facility. Liability Group ties members to a Group Master. Limit Sharing Group lets multiple parties draw against ONE shared facility. Real-time enforcement ensures total drawn across the group never exceeds the consolidated limit, even though drawn by different legal entities.

q: Why does the Application Registry audit violations even when individual user logging is disabled?
options: Random sample logging applied during stress testing of the audit subsystem's performance
Security violations are TOO IMPORTANT to be missed — PROTOCOL file captures them unconditionally as a mandatory audit floor
A vendor whim chosen by the original Temenos design team without strong technical rationale
Performance overhead minimisation — PROTOCOL avoids logging during high-load periods to preserve throughput
tip: The PROTOCOL file enforces a security floor that overrides per-user logging settings. Even if a user has logging disabled (perhaps for privacy or operational reasons), VIOLATIONS — failed logins, unauthorized access attempts, password resets — ALWAYS land in PROTOCOL. The audit trail of security events is non-optional. EOD reporting from PROTOCOL provides regulators with the violation history.

q: CARD.CHARGE allows the bank to…
options: Random fees applied during testing of the card-charge engine's resilience under varied conditions
Automatically calculate and apply card-specific fees (issuance, annual, replacement) based on tariff configurations
Customer photographs displayed on cards to deter fraudulent reuse of lost or stolen cards
Login authentication fees charged to staff who manage the card-lifecycle workflow daily
tip: CARD.CHARGE handles fees specific to cards: card issuance fee (£25 for a new debit card), annual fee on credit cards, replacement fee for lost cards. Like all charge frameworks, it supports flat, percentage, or tiered structures. Linked to the primary account via CARD.ISSUE, so fees automatically debit the right account on the configured schedule.

q: DD.RETURN handles…
options: Random sample data inserted during testing of the direct-debit return-handling workflow
Direct Debit unpaid returns — when the receiving bank rejects the DD (e.g. account closed, NSF), the rejection comes back via DD.RETURN
Customer return-of-goods records for trade-finance contracts handled through the bank's STO module
User logout records correlated with the failed direct-debit attempts they were unable to approve
tip: Not every Direct Debit succeeds. The receiving bank may reject (account closed, no funds, mandate cancelled). DD.RETURN captures these rejections with reason codes. The originating bank then handles them: notify the customer, retry, escalate to manual collection, or write off. Without proper return handling, DD-based collections would be a mess.

q: BULK.STO supports…
options: Bulk customer creation workflow used during onboarding of large corporate accounts in the registry
High-volume standing-order initiation — for instance, a payroll system pushing 50,000 salary credits to employees on the same day
Random sample standing-order data generated for testing the bulk-processing engine's accuracy
User logout enforcement across the workforce during high-volume STO-creation maintenance windows
tip: BULK.STO is for bulk recurring transfers. Classic use: a corporation's payroll — 50,000 salary payments to be made same day. Instead of 50,000 individual STO records, BULK.STO can be configured with the recipient list, and the system processes them as a batch via FT.BULK.CREDIT routing. Far more efficient than individual processing.

q: STOP.TXN logic on documents is invoked at which point?
options: Random moments during business hours chosen for invoking the document-validation workflow
Transaction authorization time — if required documents missing or expired, AUTH is blocked
Customer login attempts when KYC documents have not yet been uploaded for verification
Random sample workflows checked nightly for compliance with document-management requirements
tip: STOP.TXN runs at the AUTHORIZATION moment of a financial transaction. The system checks: does this customer/contract have all required documents in valid status? If anything's Missing or Expired, AUTH is blocked. The user sees an error explaining which doc is needed. Prevents transactions from clearing into LIV while compliance is incomplete.

q: STOCK.REGISTER vs STOCK.ENTRY in cheque management — what's the difference?
options: Identical mechanisms — both terms refer to the same physical-inventory tracking record
STOCK.ENTRY = receiving new blank stationery into inventory; STOCK.REGISTER = maintaining the current inventory state
STOCK.ENTRY is internal-only while STOCK.REGISTER is exposed to external suppliers via APIs
Random ordering applied to entries and registers during testing of the stock-management engine
tip: STOCK.ENTRY records the INTAKE event ('1000 new blank cheque books received from supplier'). STOCK.REGISTER maintains the current state ('Vault holds 800 books; Till 1 has 50; Till 2 has 50; Till 3 has 100'). Together they form a complete inventory tracking system — auditable from supplier to specific Till. Compliance for high-value blank instruments.

q: DM.APPLICATION.INFO handles…
options: Random sample data inserted for benchmark testing of the document-management framework's accuracy
Document Management application-level integration metadata — links T24 applications to specific document requirements
Customer direct-message conversations captured between customers and operations staff for audit purposes
User message conversations between operations staff and senior management for HR communications
tip: DM.APPLICATION.INFO bridges document management to specific application records. It defines which applications require which document types, how documents map to records, and how lifecycle events (e.g. account closure) trigger document archive workflows. Without it, document management would be siloed; with it, documents tie back to specific contracts/customers automatically.

q: EB.RULE.GATEWAY is the underlying engine used by…
options: A single feature dedicated to one specific rule-evaluation context within the operations workflow
Eligibility checks, Asset Classification rules, and other rules-driven workflows — the system's central rules-evaluation framework
Random rule selection applied during testing of the rule-evaluation engine's resilience under load
Customer-service representatives' triage workflow for routing inbound customer-support queries
tip: EB.RULE.GATEWAY is the generic rules engine. Eligibility (can customer X buy product Y?) uses it. Asset Classification (is this loan Standard or Watchlist?) uses it. Many other rule-driven flows. By centralizing in EB.RULE.GATEWAY (with EB.CONTEXT supplying inputs), the system avoids dozens of one-off rule engines — one consistent, configurable framework for every rules-based decision.

q: IMAGE.APPLICATION maps digital images to specific records by…
options: Random storage location selection at upload time for performance distribution across the cluster
Storing the application name (e.g. CUSTOMER), the record ID, and the image ID — providing cross-reference for retrieval
Encrypting them with a different key per image for additional security beyond the storage-layer encryption
Random data records inserted for benchmark testing of the image-application linkage subsystem
tip: IMAGE.APPLICATION is the cross-reference Table. For each digital image (IM.DOCUMENT.IMAGE), it stores: which application (CUSTOMER, ACCOUNT, COLLATERAL), which record ID, and the image ID. So when a teller opens CUSTOMER 12345, the system queries IMAGE.APPLICATION for matching entries and displays the signature, passport scan, etc. — instant retrieval without complex search.

q: Why does the cheque/card lifecycle decouple Product (CARD.TYPE / CHEQUE.TYPE) from Instance (CARD.ISSUE / CHEQUE.ISSUE)?
options: Random design choice with no specific architectural rationale beyond historical convention
Allows banks to define product behavior ONCE (expiry rules, billing) and deploy many instances; same Product/Contract pattern as in AA
More complex than necessary for fun and learning opportunities for new junior developers joining the team
A vendor mandate imposed by Temenos on banks adopting their CBS framework as a contractual obligation
tip: The Product-vs-Instance split is consistent across the platform: CARD.TYPE defines behavior (VISA Platinum, 3-year expiry); CARD.ISSUE is an instance for a specific customer. CHEQUE.TYPE defines behavior; CHEQUE.ISSUE is the actual book. PRODUCT defines lending behavior; ARRANGEMENT is the customer loan. Same separation everywhere — change the product once, all instances benefit (or stay frozen if NON.TRACKING).

q: The team wants the new CBS to 'match Temenos robustness while being lighter'. Which technical choice would BEST embody both goals?
options: Copying Temenos's full module catalogue feature-for-feature so banks switching from Temenos see identical capability — Temenos is the proven benchmark and matching it ensures parity
Adopting Temenos's architectural principles (AA, virtual GL, registry) while selecting only the components the bank actually needs at its scale — keeping the structure proven but the surface lean
Replacing every Temenos pattern with custom inventions that better fit modern cloud-native conventions like CQRS and event sourcing — a fresh design unencumbered by legacy
Building a thin shim that delegates to Temenos via OFS calls for everything complex while owning only the lightweight presentation layer in-house
tip: Matching robustness = adopting the proven structural principles (Arrangement Architecture, virtual ledger, registries) — these are what give Temenos its enterprise-grade behaviour. 'Being lighter' = NOT carrying features the bank doesn't need yet (e.g. niche Forex products). Selective adoption keeps the system maintainable while the architecture remains industry-grade. Copy-everything bloats; reinvent-everything loses Temenos's hard-won lessons.

q: A bank stakeholder asks: 'Why do you separate the core models from the APIs?' Strongest justification?
options: APIs change frequently (new channels, new partners); core models must remain stable — coupling them forces constant model churn whenever a channel evolves
Most modern frameworks (React, Spring) optimise better when business models and exposed APIs share a single class definition, so separating them sacrifices runtime efficiency
Regulators in CEMAC and UEMOA explicitly mandate the model/API separation in their core-banking compliance frameworks, making it a hard external requirement
Separation primarily helps with frontend developer ergonomics — back-end teams gain nothing structurally and the split mainly exists for UX-team productivity
tip: Core models encode business invariants (a Customer, an Arrangement, a Balance) that shouldn't be invalidated by UI changes. APIs are the contract with channels and evolve constantly — new mobile features, new partner integrations, new regulatory endpoints. Coupling them means every channel change forces a model rewrite. Separating them lets each layer evolve at its natural pace and keeps the audit-critical core stable for regulators.

q: A 'customer-centric' CBS anchors accounting and products to the customer rather than to accounts. Which downstream benefit follows MOST directly?
options: Faster login times because customer identity records sit closer to the authentication path than account records did under account-centric designs
Easier 360° risk view — total exposure to one customer across their loans, accounts, and contracts can be computed without complex cross-table joins
Reduced storage cost because customer data is normalised once instead of being duplicated inside every account record the customer holds
Better UI consistency since every screen pivots around the same customer profile, eliminating the design problem of which entity 'owns' a given screen
tip: When the customer is the anchor, every contract/account/balance has a clean direct reference back to the same Party. Group risk, regulatory KYC, AML and credit-aggregation queries become single-hop walks instead of complex multi-Table joins. Banks that anchored on Account first historically struggled with 'which is the real customer profile?' across merged accounts — customer-centric design prevents that mess from day one.

q: Phase Zero scope was deliberately limited to core accounts, products, and basic payment-hub integration. What's the strongest argument for this scoping?
options: Deposits and loans are not strategically important to the bank's near-term revenue plan, so they're scheduled later without operational risk
Without a working accounts + posting + product spine, every later module (deposits, loans, cards) lacks a stable foundation to attach to
The bank's existing legacy system already handles deposits/loans adequately for the medium term, so Phase Zero focuses on the missing capabilities
Regulators require accounts and payments to launch before loans/deposits in any new core-banking deployment under CEMAC banking law
tip: Loans, deposits, cards, mortgages, treasury — all stand on top of: account balances, product configuration, and posting. Without that spine working end-to-end, building deposits next means simultaneously fighting deposit-domain complexity AND foundational ambiguity. Phase Zero exists to make the spine bulletproof; subsequent phases then add one product at a time on a known-good base. Slicing by 'layer of depth' is more honest than slicing by 'product breadth'.

q: The proposal emphasizes 'embedding data management features like auditability and the four-eyes principle by design'. What does 'by design' add over just 'enforcing them later'?
options: Auditability and 4-Eyes only need to be enforced for transactions above certain regulatory thresholds, so 'by design' efficiently scopes when checks run
By-design means audit and 4-Eyes are baked into the kernel (every record carries audit fields; NAU/LIV separation is structural) — no application can accidentally bypass them
'By design' is a marketing phrase chosen to please regulators reviewing the proposal — the technical implementation is identical to a bolt-on approach
It allows the bank to selectively disable audit on certain low-risk Tables during phase zero to accelerate delivery without compliance impact
tip: By-design means the controls live in the kernel/framework. INPUTTER/AUTHORISER are on EVERY record automatically; NAU singleton enforces the 4-Eyes flow regardless of which app initiates it. A later-bolted-on approach relies on every developer remembering to call the audit library — one forgotten call breaks the audit chain. By design = unbypassable; later = best-effort.

q: Why frame the CBS explicitly as a transactional / OLTP system rather than a hybrid OLTP/OLAP one?
options: OLAP queries are illegal in retail banking under most central-bank rules; classifying CBS as pure OLTP keeps it lawfully operable across all four target regions
OLTP and OLAP have conflicting optimisation requirements (row vs column store, latency vs throughput); trying to be both compromises both
OLAP support requires significantly more disk storage and the bank's infrastructure budget cannot accommodate the additional capacity needed
The CBS team lacks expertise in column-store databases like ClickHouse so the OLTP-only framing limits scope to what the team can deliver reliably
tip: OLTP = high-frequency, low-latency, row-level reads/writes (a teller processing a deposit). OLAP = ad-hoc analytical queries over millions of rows (group risk dashboards). Their storage layouts, indexing, and consistency models diverge. Modern designs use a transactional OLTP core (PostgreSQL) and stream changes into a separate analytical store. Calling the CBS 'OLTP' is an architectural commitment to keeping the core fast and consistent.

q: The project's preliminary roadmap explicitly divides CBS modules into phases. From a delivery-risk perspective, which phasing principle minimizes risk most?
options: All modules should be built in parallel by independent teams to maximise wall-clock delivery speed and avoid serial-dependency bottlenecks
Earlier phases deliver the foundation modules that other modules depend on (registries, ledger, AA spine) before product modules (cards, loans)
Modules should be phased by alphabetical order in the product catalogue so the team has a deterministic non-arguable sequencing rule
Most-customer-visible features should ship first so the bank can demo capability to its stakeholders and justify continued investment
tip: Dependency-respecting phasing means each phase delivers a stable layer the next phase builds on. Building cards (Phase 3) before the AA spine (Phase 1) creates rework when the spine arrives. Foundation-first phasing trades 'customer visibility now' for 'no rework later' — a critical bet on a multi-year project where rework cost compounds.

q: Why is regional analysis (CEMAC, UEMOA, South Sudan, Liberia) needed even though the CBS itself is region-agnostic?
options: Regional analysis is mostly an internal marketing exercise; the architecture is genuinely region-agnostic and the analysis just shapes the sales presentation
Each region's data retention, language, KYC/AML, and compliance rules differ — and these shape NFRs and Configurability requirements before phase zero ships
CEMAC and UEMOA explicitly require pre-deployment regional impact assessments under their banking regulator's approval framework, so it's a procurement gate
Different regions use different physical infrastructure providers, so regional analysis is essentially a vendor-selection exercise for hosting partnerships
tip: Region-specific rules drive non-functional decisions: 10-year retention in South Sudan = 400TB storage planning; CEMAC regulators dictate certain reports; UEMOA may require certain languages. If phase zero ships without supporting these as configurable, every region adds a code release. Regional analysis lets configurability requirements be captured in the framework, not bolted on later.

q: The team adopts 'Temenos best practices… while incorporating modern frameworks like Beyond'. What risk does this pragmatic blend manage?
options: Modern frameworks like Beyond ship faster than Temenos's release cycle, so blending them is purely a time-to-market optimisation for the project plan
Pure-Temenos clone = no modernisation benefit; pure-greenfield = repeating decade-old lessons the hard way; blending captures battle-tested patterns plus modern tooling
Temenos's licensing terms require any derivative work to incorporate at least one Temenos best practice, so the blend is a contractual obligation
Banks are accustomed to seeing Temenos terminology in core-banking projects and the blend mainly serves familiarity rather than technical merit
tip: Pure-Temenos clones inherit Temenos's legacy weight (jBase quirks, COBOL-era patterns in places). Pure-greenfield re-discovers issues like 'oh, accounting must be transaction-driven not coupled' that Temenos already solved. The pragmatic blend takes Temenos's STRUCTURAL wins (AA, virtual GL, registry) but rebuilds them on modern frameworks (PostgreSQL, Kafka, Kubernetes). Best of both worlds; minimises both risks.

q: Andrei Sech noted Temenos accounting uses 'hard accounting' for most models but 'soft accounting' for arrangement products. Why this split?
options: Hard accounting is the older Temenos style maintained for backward compatibility; soft accounting is the new direction and arrangement products are the testbed

Standard transactions (FT, Teller) have well-understood, stable postings (hard); arrangement products vary by product (loan vs lease vs syndicated) and benefit from configurable allocation rules (soft)
Soft accounting is operationally faster than hard accounting at high transaction volumes, so it's reserved for arrangement products which carry the heaviest volume
Hard accounting cannot represent the multi-leg postings that arrangement products require (e.g. accrual + capitalisation + writeoff), so soft is structurally necessary
tip: Stable, common transactions (cash deposit, wire transfer) have one canonical posting pattern — hard-code it once, get speed and certainty. Arrangement products differ wildly: a mortgage's accounting differs from a leasing accrual differs from an Islamic-finance contract. Soft Accounting (allocation rules per product) handles that variability without bloating the core. Split by 'where flexibility actually pays off'.

q: The team committed to delivering documents 24h before each working session. From a meeting-effectiveness perspective, the strongest reason is…
options: It's basic professional etiquette in banking projects across CEMAC and UEMOA, where deliverable-ahead rules are codified in regional procurement templates
Lets stakeholders pre-read and arrive with informed questions — turning the session from a presentation into a decision-making discussion
The 24h gap creates a documentation paper-trail useful for audit purposes, particularly when stakeholders later dispute when information was shared
Confluence's notification system batches updates at 24h intervals, so pre-sharing documents at that cadence aligns with the bank's tooling workflow
tip: Without pre-reads, sessions degrade into one-way presentations where the architect explains while the bank listens. With pre-reads, attendees arrive with specific questions and counter-proposals — turning the hour into decisions, not exposition. For a multi-stakeholder project like CBS, this difference compounds over many sessions: 5 minutes saved per attendee per session × 20 sessions × 10 attendees = 16 hours saved.

q: Why staff three teams (rather than one large team) for CBS delivery?
options: Three is the minimum number of teams that allows shift coverage for 24/7 on-call during the rollout phase, balancing labour cost against follow-the-sun support
Three teams enable parallel work on independent modules (kernel, product factory, payments) — but coordination overhead grows with team count, so 'three' balances parallelism against integration cost
Three teams are required by the bank's procurement framework which mandates dedicated team-per-domain staffing for any vendor delivery contract
Three teams happens to match the number of senior architects available, so the staffing reflects existing senior resources rather than optimal team structure
tip: One team = serial bottleneck; many teams = coordination chaos. Three teams aligned with major architectural seams (foundation, products, channels) let each work on largely independent surface area while sharing common interfaces. Brooks's Law: adding teams adds communication cost as n²; the 'right' team count maximises parallel value before coordination cost overtakes it.

q: The team plans to gradually off-board members from Payment Hub to CBS. What execution risk does 'gradually' (vs 'all at once') manage?
options: Bank policy in the target regions prohibits any team from being fully re-allocated within a single quarter, so gradual movement is the compliant approach
Off-boarding everyone at once means losing all current Payment Hub support; gradual moves preserve domain knowledge transfer and ongoing operations
The Payment Hub team has labour agreements requiring 90-day re-allocation notices, so gradual is the legally required pace of personnel movement
Salaries between the two projects differ enough that gradual movement smooths the budget impact on each project's quarterly burn rate
tip: Payment Hub is a running production system — its team holds tribal knowledge that's tacit and hard to document. Yanking everyone leaves operations brittle and CBS without context. Gradual transfer means: (a) some Payment Hub coverage remains, (b) leavers train their replacements, (c) the CBS team grows in waves it can absorb. Same logic applies to most rebuilds: brick-by-brick beats forklift.

q: The MVP definition includes 'multicurrency support from day one' even though it complicates Phase Zero. Why not defer multicurrency?
options: Customer demand for multicurrency was identified during pre-engagement workshops as the single highest-priority gap, so it was prioritised by customer voice
Adding multicurrency LATER means retrofitting every Table (Balance, GL key, Transaction Code) — far more disruptive than baking it in from the start
Multicurrency is a CEMAC regulatory requirement that goes into effect mid-2026, so the phase-zero timeline forces it in regardless of preference
Multicurrency is technically the easiest cross-cutting feature to add early, so prioritising it boosts team confidence with a quick foundational win
tip: Multicurrency isn't a feature — it's a pervasive concern affecting every monetary field, every posting, every report. Retrofitting it means changing thousands of code paths and migrating data. Building it in from day one costs ~20% extra in Phase Zero; bolting it on later costs ~10× that and risks data corruption. Pervasive concerns must be designed in early.

q: Joel Kamdem asked for documents 24h before each session. This is a quality control on which dimension of the delivery process?
options: Schedule control — the 24h rule prevents stakeholders from delaying decisions by claiming they need more time, locking the agenda into a planned cadence
Decision quality — informed stakeholders make better-aligned decisions than reactive ones; investing 24h of reading saves weeks of rework from misaligned decisions
Speed of approval — pre-reading dramatically accelerates the in-session approval votes, compressing two-hour decision meetings into thirty-minute affairs
Cost control — the rule reduces meeting-overrun costs because attendees having pre-read no longer need clarifications during the billable session time
tip: Quality control isn't only about deliverables — it's also about decision quality at gates. A session where a bank stakeholder makes an off-the-cuff decision often reverses a week later when they've thought about it. 24h of pre-read makes those decisions stickier. The 24h rule is essentially a process-level quality gate that prevents downstream rework.

q: Andrei Sech contrasted 'manual entries with explicit debit/credit accounts' (legacy) with 'entries automatically raised online' (modern). The deepest implication of this shift for operations staff is…
options: Staff can process more transactions per hour because they no longer need to navigate the accounting screens between business steps in the workflow
The TRANSACTION becomes the single source of truth — staff can no longer 'fix' the ledger separately from the business event that produced it
Operational staff salaries adjust to reflect the lower skill threshold needed when accounting expertise becomes embedded in the system rather than the role
Staff are reorganised into accounting-specialist vs business-specialist teams because the two workflows have now visibly diverged into distinct competencies
tip: When operators typed entries by hand, the business event and the accounting entry were independent artefacts — and could disagree. Transaction-driven means the entry IS derived from the event; you can't 'patch the books' without also reversing the underlying transaction. That tight coupling eliminates a common class of reconciliation pain, but takes away the operator's ability to 'just adjust' the GL. Discipline up, hidden fixes gone.

q: Modern accounting entries are 'richer' than legacy ones (transaction codes, P&L category, account officer, FX original equivalent). What's the architectural payoff of this richness?
options: Storage costs increase substantially since each transaction now carries more fields, so banks need bigger databases and more frequent archive cycles
Reports become a PROJECTION over richer data — new analytical slices (by officer, by category, by FX) require config, not new postings or new tables
Posting latency increases because every additional field requires validation and metadata lookup, slowing down the OLTP critical path
Banks need to hire data engineers to maintain the additional fields, which is offset by the reduced staffing needs in the reconciliation function
tip: Reports as projections means: same underlying entries, multiple views via Consolidation Keys. Want profitability by Account Officer? Already in the data. Want product-line P&L? Already there. Without that richness, a new report would mean changing posting logic or adding a parallel data store. Richer entries = report-by-config not report-by-code. The cost is bigger rows; the win is analytical agility.

q: A bank wants to compute interest on Saturday's value-dated transactions even though Saturday isn't a business day. Which date attribute pair correctly reflects this?
options: Booking = Saturday (the calendar day funds moved); Value = Saturday (interest starts that day) — both calendar-aligned with no business-day adjustment
Booking = Monday (next business day, so the books close cleanly); Value = Saturday (when interest starts accruing on the customer's behalf)
Booking = Saturday (raw calendar entry); Value = Monday (when interest mechanically begins because Saturday is not a business day per holiday rules)
Booking and Value must always equal each other in regulated banking, so both should be set to Saturday for this customer-initiated transaction
tip: Booking Date = when the entry hits the ledger (must be a business day for the books to close cleanly — so Monday). Value Date = the economic date the funds count for interest/balance (Saturday — what the customer 'feels'). The dates legitimately diverge because the business calendar doesn't match the calendar calendar. Picking the right date per use (interest, statement period, FX rate) is core accounting policy.

q: Consolidation Keys replace fixed GL accounts. The MOST important architectural advantage of this is…
options: The database footprint shrinks because keys take less storage than fully-materialised GL account records — significant cost savings at large transaction volume
The bank can adapt to new countries' GL chart-of-accounts regulations via configuration only — without changing posting logic or migrating historic entries
