Posting throughput increases because the engine no longer needs to look up the destination GL account; keys are computed directly from transaction attributes
Audit becomes simpler because consolidation keys are deterministic strings while fixed GL accounts often have ambiguous or overlapping numeric ranges
tip: Different countries have different GL numbering and grouping requirements. Fixed GL accounts mean 'add a new country = rewrite postings'. Consolidation Keys describe entries by DIMENSIONS (product, currency, sector); each country's chart-of-accounts becomes a CONFIGURATION that aggregates dimensions into its required structure. Same posting works for South Sudan, UEMOA, CEMAC. Configurability bought via abstraction.

q: Why does category-based P&L accounting (T24 / Globus categories 1-19999) outperform a flat chart-of-accounts approach for multi-product banks?
options: Categories reduce the total number of files the system must manage during EOD, lowering disk I/O during the time-sensitive close-of-business window
Categories impose structure (income vs expense, by source) that lets the system aggregate consistently across products WITHOUT each product reinventing P&L semantics
Categories are required by IFRS for any P&L reporting, so adoption is a compliance prerequisite rather than an architectural preference
Categories simplify staff training because new analysts only need to memorise a fixed set of buckets rather than an ever-growing GL chart of accounts
tip: Without category structure, each product reinvents 'where does my fee revenue post?' — leading to drift and inconsistent reports. Categories impose a shared taxonomy: every fee revenue post falls in a known range; every interest expense too. New products plug into the existing taxonomy. Reports stay coherent as the product catalogue grows. Structure = scalable consistency.

q: Andrei Sech flagged a concern that using Blockly/formula-builders for account entry generation 'lacks the flexibility of a virtual GL based on keys'. What's the deeper trade-off being highlighted?
options: Blockly's visual block paradigm is too constraining for the complex expressions banks need; pure code formulas in JavaScript would be more expressive
Per-event formulas only express WHERE each entry goes; Consolidation Keys express the entry's DIMENSIONS — keys support unforeseen future reports, formulas only support pre-planned ones
Blockly has a steep learning curve that puts the configuration burden onto trained engineers, defeating the goal of business-user configurability
Blockly's underlying execution engine has known performance issues at high transaction throughput, slowing posting in production-scale workloads
tip: A formula 'debit GL 1010, credit GL 2100' answers ONE report's question — the formula-author had to know what to post. Keys instead capture WHAT this entry IS (product=mortgage, currency=USD, sector=retail, type=fee-income); future reports SELECT entries by dimension combinations the formula-author never anticipated. Keys are forward-compatible; formulas are forward-fragile.

q: A 'pyramid' model is described: operations at the foundation, entries grouped above, then aggregated GL lines, then reports at top. What does each layer enable INDEPENDENTLY of the others?
options: Each layer is permanently coupled to the layers above and below — they were designed together and any change to one requires synchronised changes to all
Each layer can be replaced without breaking adjacent ones: change report definitions without touching entries; change posting rules without rewriting reports
Only the bottom (operations) layer can be replaced; reports, GL lines, and entries are vendor-shipped and effectively immutable from the bank's perspective
The pyramid is conceptual only — in implementation, all four layers compile to a single binary so swapping layers requires a full system rebuild
tip: The pyramid IS architectural decoupling. Operations don't know about reports; reports don't know about transactions. Each layer has a contract with its neighbours. The bank can change a report definition (top) without affecting the posting engine (bottom). The bank can refactor postings (middle) without rewriting reports IF the dimensions stay stable. This is what enables independent module evolution.

q: Why does a transaction-driven model also REDUCE the number of GL accounts that exist?
options: Transaction-driven systems use compressed account numbering schemes that physically take less space, reducing the storage footprint per account record
GL accounts are CREATED only when an entry first needs them (driven by key combinations encountered) — instead of pre-creating thousands of empty accounts upfront
Legacy systems duplicated GL accounts across companies; transaction-driven systems share one canonical chart, eliminating the duplication overhead
Banks adopting transaction-driven systems typically simplify their product catalogue at the same time, which incidentally reduces the GL account count
tip: Legacy approach: pre-define every possible GL account in advance (15,000+). Most stay empty. Transaction-driven approach: when an entry needs to land somewhere, the system materialises the GL key on demand. Result: only GL accounts that ACTUALLY hold balances exist. Queries are faster (less to scan); admin is lighter (no empty-account cleanup); reports cleaner (no 0-balance noise).

q: The legacy model has 'delayed visibility'; transaction-driven has 'real-time visibility'. From a treasury / risk perspective, what NEW capability does real-time enable that delayed can't?
options: Faster reports — the same analyst can generate the same end-of-day report in seconds rather than minutes, increasing analyst throughput across the bank
Intra-day liquidity decisions — treasury can see current cash position any second and act before close of business, instead of working from yesterday's stale snapshot
Reduced infrastructure costs since real-time visibility eliminates the need for separate reporting databases and the ETL pipelines that fed them
Improved customer-facing experience because tellers can quote up-to-the-minute balances during in-branch interactions, raising customer satisfaction scores
tip: With delayed visibility, treasury operates on yesterday's data — any decision today is partly blind. With real-time visibility, intra-day cash management becomes possible: spot a liquidity gap at 11am, fix it by 2pm. For a multi-currency bank with intra-day FX flows, this is the difference between proactive and reactive treasury — directly affecting funding cost and risk exposure.

q: Soft Accounting uses 'allocation rules' to produce entries from events. Compared with hard-coded posting, the deepest benefit is…
options: Less code overall in the codebase, which translates to easier code reviews, lower bug counts, and a smaller surface area for security vulnerabilities
When regulators change accounting treatment (e.g. new IFRS rule), the bank adjusts allocation rules in config — without touching the operational code path that handles customer transactions
Allocation rules can be hand-tuned per product to optimise reporting outcomes, giving the bank reporting flexibility competitors with hard accounting cannot match
Soft Accounting is significantly faster at runtime than hard-coded posting because configuration lookups are cached more efficiently than compiled posting routines
tip: Hard-coded posting means a regulatory change forces a code release: high risk, slow, expensive. With soft allocation rules, the regulator-driven update is a config change — quickly testable, easily auditable, reversible. The TRANSACTION code path stays untouched (customers' UX is unchanged). Decoupling the regulator's pace from the engineering pace is one of the highest-value benefits of soft accounting.

q: What does 'accounting is designed as a flexible multicomponent system' really mean from an engineering perspective?
options: Banking applications running on multicomponent systems generally outperform monolithic systems on standard transaction-throughput benchmarks by 20-30 percent
Posting, consolidation, reporting are SEPARATE services with defined contracts — each can be scaled, replaced, or upgraded without touching the others
Multicomponent design is a regulatory requirement in CEMAC and UEMOA banking compliance frameworks, forcing accounting separation into independent services
Multicomponent design lets each service run on a different database technology, optimising posting for OLTP and reporting for OLAP within one banking platform
tip: Multicomponent = independent services connected by contracts (events, key formats). Posting service writes entries. Consolidation service aggregates by keys. Reporting service queries aggregates. Each scales independently (heavy posting load doesn't slow reports). Each can be upgraded independently (new posting engine doesn't break reports). This is what enables modern operational characteristics — scaling, observability, partial deployments.

q: The system models a single transaction as having multiple distinct dates (booking, value, trade, processing). Why does each need to exist separately even though they often coincide?
options: Different teams within the bank historically owned each date field, and the model preserves those legacy ownership boundaries even though they no longer matter
Each date carries DIFFERENT legal/financial semantics — interest pricing (value), tax year (booking), regulatory reporting (trade), batch sequencing (processing) — collapsing them loses information
Different regulators inspect different date fields, so maintaining all four lets the bank present compliant evidence to whichever regulator audits a transaction
Some currencies require specific date conventions per ISO 4217 (e.g. JPY needs trade date, EUR needs value date), so the system must carry all four globally
tip: A trade date 31-Dec might value-date 02-Jan and book on 03-Jan. Tax reporting uses booking year; interest uses value date; trade-date drives MiFID reporting. Collapse all to 'date' = lose distinctions regulators need. Modeling them separately is more expensive but lets the same transaction satisfy multiple disparate downstream consumers. Critical for cross-border, multi-regulator banking.

q: Reports are described as a 'projection' over accounting data. What architectural property does this give the reports layer?
options: Reports become slower to generate because projections are computed at query time rather than pre-materialised into dedicated reporting tables for fast lookup
Reports add no operational load — adding a new report doesn't change postings; reports query already-captured data via key combinations
Reports gain ACID transaction semantics because they read from the same store the posting engine writes to, eliminating eventual-consistency reconciliation issues
Reports automatically inherit access-control restrictions from the underlying entries, so the security model is simpler than maintaining separate report-level permissions
tip: If reports were materialized differently (e.g. each report keeping its own pre-aggregated table), adding a new report would force the posting engine to write to one more place. Projection-based reports mean: posting writes to the canonical data; reports SELECT and aggregate as queries. Add a report → zero impact on the posting hot path. The cost is read-time aggregation; with consolidation already done, it's cheap.

q: A bank wants to report under both local GAAP and IFRS simultaneously. The transaction-driven model handles this how?
options: Run a second instance of the entire CBS in parallel — one configured for local GAAP, the other for IFRS — and reconcile the two outputs periodically
Same underlying entries; two separate consolidation key configurations roll them up differently per standard — no parallel postings
Use IFRS as the primary standard and translate to local GAAP only when reports are requested, accepting some translation imprecision as the trade-off
Schedule local GAAP postings during business hours and IFRS postings during EOD, ensuring the two never collide on the same transactional record
tip: Parallel postings (one set per standard) doubles storage, doubles processing, and risks divergence. Single canonical entries with multiple key configurations gives both views from the same source — guaranteed consistency, no divergence possible. Each GAAP / IFRS view is just a different roll-up of the same entries. This is the killer feature of dimensional accounting.

q: Foreign-original-equivalent amounts on every entry exist for what specific reconciliation purpose?
options: Customer-facing statements always need to show the original transaction currency alongside the converted amount, so the dual-currency capture supports clean statement rendering
Auditing FX positions — reconciling the bank's books in local currency against original-currency exposures requires both amounts on every cross-currency entry
Some regulators in the target regions require FX transactions to be journaled in both currencies for tax-evasion controls, making the dual amount a regulatory mandate
It enables faster FX rate revaluation because both amounts are pre-stored, eliminating the need to lookup historical rates when re-valuing positions intra-day
tip: Without original-currency amounts, you can only see 'this entry was worth X in LCY today' — you've lost the FX exposure. With both AMOUNT.LCY and AMOUNT.FCY on each entry, the bank can answer 'how much USD do we owe in total?' (sum AMOUNT.FCY for USD entries) and 'what's that worth in our local books today?' (sum AMOUNT.LCY for same entries) — both critical for FX risk and regulatory reporting.

q: What's the architectural significance of saying 'accounting is the BACKBONE of a CBS'?
options: Accounting consumes more CPU than any other CBS module so it sits at the centre of resource planning and capacity calculations for the entire platform
Every other module (loans, deposits, cards) eventually produces accounting entries — get the backbone wrong and every product builds on quicksand
Accounting is the first module Phase Zero is delivering, so its centrality in the delivery sequence makes it the metaphorical backbone of the timeline
Banking regulators audit the accounting module more frequently than other modules, making it the de facto compliance backbone of the system
tip: Accounting is the universal interface every product module talks to. A misdesigned accounting layer leaks into every product's design: workarounds, hacks, manual reconciliations, hidden journals. A clean accounting layer lets every product simply RAISE ENTRIES and trust the system to consolidate/report correctly. Getting it right first = leverage; getting it wrong first = tax on every later feature.

q: Why is 'operations form the foundation of the pyramid' (not 'entries form the foundation')?
options: Entries are written more frequently than operations are performed (a single transaction produces multiple entries), so the bottom of the pyramid naturally has the highest volume layer
Operations are what the bank does to serve customers (the real-world business events); entries are derived FROM operations — modelling entries as ground truth would let operations and books disagree
Entries can be aggregated and indexed for fast query while operations are typically logs that aren't queryable, so the queryable layer becomes the foundation
Operations require human input while entries are computed automatically, so placing operations as the foundation respects the human-in-the-loop design philosophy
tip: If entries were ground truth, you could have entries with no operation behind them (= fictional). Making operations ground truth means entries can only exist as derivatives of real business events — eliminating the 'phantom entries' fraud vector. The pyramid bottom = operations (the real world); everything above is derived. This is what 'transaction-driven' really means at its deepest level.

q: A bank disabled real-time posting to 'speed up the user-facing transaction'. What hidden cost did they incur?
options: Customer-facing latency increased because batched posting requires a second confirmation screen for the customer at the end of the wait window
Loss of intra-day GL accuracy — the GL is now batched, so any inter-step decision (limit check, balance lookup, fraud screen) operates on stale numbers; cumulative drift across modules
Storage volumes spiked because batched entries accumulate in temporary queues that consume far more space than real-time entries do steady-state
Audit logs became inconsistent because batched entries don't carry their original input timestamps, only the batch's commit time, blurring the audit trail
tip: Real-time posting isn't a luxury — many checks downstream RELY on current ledger state. Limit checks compare against current balance; fraud screens compare against today's transactions; AML compares against intra-day flows. Batching posting means all those checks operate on stale data — false negatives (frauds get through), false positives (limits incorrectly trip). The 'speed-up' shifts cost from UI latency to downstream correctness.

q: An audit requirement says 'every accounting entry must trace back to a customer-facing operation'. The transaction-driven model satisfies this how?
options: By accident — entries happen to carry the transaction code as one of many metadata fields, and auditors can reverse-engineer the operation if they know the codes well
By design — entries are derived from operations and carry the operation's identifier; auditing 'show me what operation produced GL line X' is a single lookup
Through periodic reconciliation jobs that match GL entries to operations using fuzzy timestamp matching, producing a probabilistic but not exact lineage
Through manual annotations added by operators during EOD review, which is why the team requires careful operator training to maintain audit traceability
tip: Each STMT.ENTRY / CATEG.ENTRY carries TRANSACTION.CODE and references back to the originating contract/event. Auditor query: 'show me the operation behind this GL entry' = follow the reference, see the FT/Teller/Activity. Legacy systems where entries were keyed in manually couldn't always reconstruct this — operators left notes 'wash entry for misposting on Tue' that auditors had to interpret.

q: A scenario: the system applies a late fee to 1,000 customers in a nightly batch. Under transaction-driven accounting, what's true?
options: An operator opens the GL module, types 1,000 line entries manually (one per customer), then posts them together as a single year-end batch journal
1,000 fee-Activities run; each automatically raises its own STMT.ENTRY + CATEG.ENTRY pair; no manual journal touches the GL
A single aggregate GL entry is posted (debit customer-receivable $5,000, credit fee-income $5,000), and per-customer detail is held in a sub-ledger only
The fees are queued and only the net P&L impact is posted nightly, with per-customer detail held in a derived analytical store rather than the GL
tip: Each fee is an Activity (well-defined operation). Each Activity raises its own entries. The 'batch' is just a scheduler running many Activities — each individually transaction-driven. The GL gets 1,000 + 1,000 entries; consolidation rolls them up. No human types anything. The audit trail shows each fee event tied to a customer. Same model scales from 1 fee to 1 million.

q: Four 'Architecture Significant Requirements' (ASRs) shape the CBS: Security, Availability, Scalability, Observability — plus Configurability. Why are these called 'architecture-significant' rather than just 'requirements'?
options: They are abbreviated as ASRs because the abbreviation conveniently fits in document footers and architecture decision records as a compact label
Each fundamentally CONSTRAINS how the system is built — bolting them on later requires rearchitecting; building without them forces them
ASR is a Temenos-specific terminology that the team adopts to stay aligned with the existing Temenos best-practice playbook the bank already references
The acronym distinguishes them from functional Requirements (FRs) in the project tracking tool, where different sprint planning rules apply to each label
tip: Functional requirements describe WHAT the system does (compute interest, post entries). ASRs describe HOW the system must behave (with 99.9% uptime, with zero-trust security). These can't be added later as features — they demand structural decisions: distributed vs monolith, mTLS vs perimeter security, batch vs streaming. Calling them 'architecture-significant' flags them for early decision.

q: Zero-trust security says 'verify every request, regardless of network location'. What's the most concrete architectural consequence?
options: All internal traffic is now logged in plain text for forensic investigation, increasing the storage volume needed for security audit retention
Every internal service-to-service call requires authentication (e.g. mTLS via Istio) — there's no 'trusted internal network' assumption
Each microservice must individually implement encryption-at-rest for its database, multiplying the per-service security configuration burden
VPN tunnelling becomes mandatory for every pod-to-pod communication, with rotating session keys managed by a centralised VPN gateway
tip: Pre-zero-trust: inside the corporate firewall = trusted (no auth between services). Zero-trust: every call (even between two pods on the same cluster) must prove identity. Architecturally this means: service mesh (Istio) with mTLS by default, every service has a verified identity (Keycloak), no 'internal' shortcuts. Costs latency overhead; gains: a compromised pod doesn't get a free pass.

q: Policy as Code (PaC) is highlighted with a 100% coverage goal for critical domains. PaC's advantage over written policies is…
options: PaC reduces the typing effort for policy authors because YAML and Rego are more concise than the prose-heavy regulatory frameworks they encode
Policies are executable — automatically enforced at request time (admission controllers, OPA), not just documented and hoped-for; deployment fails if policy fails
PaC integrates with the bank's existing Confluence policy documentation, reducing duplication between the spec language and the implementation language
PaC produces audit-friendly diff outputs that show exactly which policy clauses changed across releases, simplifying the regulator's review process
tip: Written policy: 'all PII must be encrypted' — relies on every dev to remember. PaC: an Open Policy Agent rule enforces 'reject any deployment without encryption-at-rest annotations' at admission time. Cannot drift. Cannot be forgotten. New regulation = new policy code = enforced from minute one. The 100% coverage goal means: every critical domain has executable enforcement, not just a wiki page.

q: Target regions are CEMAC, UEMOA, South Sudan, and Liberia. Why does identifying target regions BEFORE finalising the architecture matter?
options: Each region uses different SI units and currency precision, so the data model adapts at the table-definition level to each region's measurement system
Compliance regulations per region (data localisation, retention, encryption standards) drive concrete architectural choices — region-agnostic architecture often can't satisfy all rules
Different regions use different time zones, and the architecture needs to know in advance which zones to support in its scheduler and batch components
Regional differences in user-interface conventions (e.g. RTL languages) drive frontend architecture decisions that must be made before development begins
tip: South Sudan's 10-year retention drives 400TB storage planning. CEMAC may require data residency (servers physically in the region). Liberia may have specific encryption rules. Designing region-agnostic means picking the union of all rules — possibly more restrictive than any single region needs. Knowing target regions = right-sizing the architecture to actual requirements.

q: 99.9% availability = ~8.76h downtime/year. Why is this target heavily dependent on the BANK'S infrastructure, not just the CBS architecture?
options: The CBS team handles all aspects of availability end-to-end including the network, hardware, and power, so the SLA is fully under the CBS team's control
Availability is end-to-end: bank's network, power, hardware can fail in ways CBS architecture can't recover from — the composite SLA depends on every layer
Banks frequently introduce manual workflows that pause CBS during business reviews, which reduce availability in ways the architecture itself cannot prevent
Availability targets in banking regulations are denominated in bank-controlled minutes per year, so CBS calculates SLA only against bank-managed time windows
tip: A CBS with multi-region failover can survive datacentre loss — but if the bank's single network feed cuts, the CBS is unreachable. CBS uptime ≠ user-facing uptime. The composite SLA needs ALL layers to meet 99.9%. Hence 'custom tier plans' per bank: high-tier banks have redundant infrastructure; lower-tier have to accept lower composite uptime even with a great CBS.

q: 55-56 GB of logs per day is projected. What's the MOST important downstream consequence to plan for?
options: Centralised log storage is the only architectural cost; once provisioned, retention and search are handled by the storage layer with negligible additional planning
Retention policy + log management at scale — costs go beyond storage to indexing/searching/archiving; without policy, logs accumulate uselessly and breach retention regulations
55GB/day produces noticeable network egress charges if logs are mirrored cross-DC, making bandwidth planning the dominant downstream cost factor
The bank's compliance officer needs to manually review log samples each week, and the volume drives staffing requirements for the compliance team
tip: 55GB/day = 20TB/year just in logs. Indexing for search costs more than storage. Retention regulations may require keeping logs for years AND auditable deletion afterwards. Without a clear log lifecycle (hot → warm → cold → delete), the cost spirals and compliance breaks. ElasticSearch index management + Logstash filtering + retention policy must all be planned BEFORE volumes ramp up.

q: Scalability is described as 'a continuation of availability'. Why couple them rather than treating them separately?
options: They share the same monitoring stack (Prometheus, Grafana) so combining the two simplifies dashboard layouts and reduces the number of metrics to track
Under load spikes, capacity to scale IS what keeps the system available — running out of capacity = effective downtime even if no component crashed
Both are owned by the same team in the bank's operating model, so the requirements are paired to match the team's responsibility boundary in the org chart
Auto-scaling rules and availability monitoring use overlapping configuration in Kubernetes (HPA + readiness probes), which encourages combined treatment in design
tip: A non-scalable system at 110% load = effectively down for the over-110% portion of users — even though every component is 'up'. Scalability is the ability to HANDLE load growth without availability loss. They're two sides of the same coin: capacity headroom and recovery from failure. Treating them together forces an integrated capacity plan.

q: Observability collects logs, metrics, AND traces. Why all three rather than just 'good logging'?
options: Banking regulators in CEMAC explicitly enumerate the three signals (logs, metrics, traces) in their compliance framework, so all three are mandated for audit
Each answers different questions: logs (what happened?), metrics (how often / how slow?), traces (which path through services?). One alone leaves blind spots
Three is the industry-standard maximum that observability vendors price-bundle into their enterprise tiers, so including all three optimises licensing economics
Tracing is the most expensive of the three, so it's listed last in the priority order even though logs and metrics could in principle stand alone for most cases
tip: Logs tell you a transaction failed. Metrics tell you 5% of transactions fail at peak. Traces tell you the failure happens in the AML hook calling an external provider that timed out. Each tool answers questions the others can't. Modern systems need all three because production issues span multiple services and require correlation. Observability stack (Prometheus + Jaeger + Logstash) is structured around this triad.

q: The team supports Configurability with 'no-code' AND code-customization. The reason both exist is…
options: Pure no-code approaches expose too much complexity to non-technical users; pure code-only is too inflexible for line-of-business teams — pairing them targets each user group
Common adaptations should be config-only (catch 90% of bank-specific needs cheaply); deeply unique processes need code (the 10% no config language could express). Both = pragmatic balance
Bank IT departments have a strict policy against pure no-code approaches because they cannot be audited at the regulatory level, forcing a code escape hatch
The bank's existing developer team is more productive in code than configuration, so the choice reflects internal team capability rather than architectural philosophy
tip: Pure-no-code: config languages can't express every business process; banks with unique workflows would be blocked. Pure-code: every minor change is a release; banks waste IT capacity on trivial parameterisation. Hybrid: 90% of customisation is config (per-bank rates, fee structures, calendars); the other 10% gets code hooks (custom validations, exotic integrations). Pareto-optimal.

q: A bank's MOST realistic concurrent peak is between the 4th and 5th tier of the calculator. Why does this single number drive so much of the architecture?
options: Tier 4-5 happens to be the same as Temenos's default sizing recommendation for banks in the target regions, so it serves as a useful starting benchmark to validate against
Concurrent peak drives compute, network, DB connections, log throughput — many downstream NFRs derive from this one assumption, so a wrong estimate cascades into wrong sizing
Tier 4-5 is the highest tier the procurement budget can accommodate, so the architecture is anchored on that ceiling rather than measured demand from the bank
Tier 4-5 implies a specific Kafka partition count and PostgreSQL connection pool size; without this number, those configuration values cannot be locked in
tip: Concurrent peak isn't 'how many users total' — it's 'how many ACTIVE at once'. From peak you derive: max DB connections, request-per-second capacity, log generation rate, network throughput, autoscaling triggers. Under-estimate = production capacity crisis; over-estimate = massive over-spend. Why architects insist on getting this number right BEFORE finalising the stack.

q: 10-year data retention × 40 TB/year = ~400 TB. Why is cloud archive storage primarily 'a bank responsibility' rather than CBS team responsibility?
options: Cloud archive providers offer per-GB pricing that is dramatically cheaper than on-prem storage, so it's a no-brainer cost optimisation that the bank should embrace
Data sovereignty + compliance: who owns/encrypts/audits the archive is a regulatory question the BANK answers; CBS provides hooks but the storage choice is the bank's risk decision
Cloud archive providers in the target regions have outage histories that exceed banking SLAs, so the CBS team avoids endorsing one over the bank's own preference
Cloud archive APIs differ across providers (AWS Glacier vs Azure Cool vs GCP Nearline), and exposing CBS to specific provider APIs would reduce architectural portability
tip: Cloud archive raises sovereignty questions (which jurisdiction holds the data?), encryption-key custody, and audit access — all of which are bank-and-regulator decisions, not engineering decisions. CBS provides the export/archive API; whether the bank chooses local tape, regional cloud, or hyperscaler is their risk. Architecture exposes the seam; business chooses the substrate.

q: UI translation is described as 'simpler' than translating technical messages. Why is technical message translation hard?
options: Technical messages contain more words on average than UI strings, so the volume of translation work scales much higher and dominates the localisation budget
Technical messages (in compliance reports, audit trails) are read by regulators who may require LEGAL precision in the local language — informal translation risks legal mismatch
Technical messages embed variable values (e.g. account IDs) that must remain in their original format, complicating the translation tooling chain considerably
Technical messages are generated at runtime so each one needs a translation cache lookup, which adds latency to every transaction that produces such a message
tip: A UI button 'Submit' can be translated loosely. A compliance report stating 'transaction reversed due to insufficient funds at value date' carries legal weight — regulators may interpret the exact wording, and a sloppy translation could change legal meaning. Hence technical-message translation needs SOURCE OF TRUTH governance (which language is authoritative?) plus legally-reviewed translations.

q: Automatic failover capability is said to depend on 'the bank's operations team and knowledge'. Why isn't auto-failover purely a software capability?
options: Auto-failover scripts depend on commercial software (e.g. Patroni, Stolon) whose licence the bank must procure independently, making it a procurement dependency

When primary fails, the secondary needs to be HEALTHY, MONITORED, and the team must know how to verify failover succeeded + handle the eventual failback. Without ops maturity, auto-failover fails silently
Failover triggers are network-level events that the bank's monitoring tools detect, so the bank's tooling drives whether the trigger fires in the first place
Failover policy is encoded in the bank's Active Directory group policies for security reasons, putting the actual failover decision under bank IT governance
tip: Auto-failover software flips a switch. But: was the secondary actually replicating? Did all dependent systems failover too? When primary recovers, who fails back and how? Without ops team practice (chaos drills), auto-failover at 3am could fail silently and the bank wakes up to broken consistency. Software provides the mechanism; ops maturity provides the reliability.

q: 'Every requirement adds complexity, time, and resource needs.' What's the management lesson?
options: The lesson is that NFRs should be expressed in terms of measurable budget impact so stakeholders can prioritise by cost, which simplifies governance
Aggressive prioritisation is itself a critical architecture activity — saying NO to nice-to-haves before they touch the design is cheaper than removing them later
Each requirement requires its own architecture decision record, so the project should aim to keep the ADR catalogue small for ease of future review
Banks tend to over-specify NFRs because they pay by the requirement, so the lesson is to consolidate similar requirements into single billable line items
tip: Every yes adds cost; every no preserves capacity. Pre-design prioritisation is the cheapest place to say no — once a requirement is in the design, removing it can break downstream assumptions. The reminder that 'every requirement adds complexity' is a discipline against scope creep. Architects who can't say no end up with bloated, unfinishable systems.

q: Deployment strategy and 'configurability could be skipped in phase zero to meet delivery'. What's the engineering trade-off being made?
options: Hardcoding values is universally a bad practice and the team should refuse to ship phase zero rather than commit code that requires a later refactor cycle
Configurability now = framework cost + delay; hard-code now and add config later = faster phase 0 but harder retrofit. Pragmatic in Phase Zero only if the retrofit cost is bounded
Configurability and hardcoding produce identical runtime behaviour, so the choice is purely organisational — pick whichever the team is more familiar with
Configurability requires the bank to staff a dedicated configuration team, which they cannot do in phase zero, so hardcoding is the only viable option
tip: Phase Zero deadline pressure may justify deferring configurability. The trade-off: hard-coded values are quicker to ship but harder to change later (every change = release). Acceptable when (a) the values rarely change in Phase Zero, (b) the retrofit path is known and bounded. Dangerous when 'temporary hardcoding' calcifies into permanent inflexibility. Architects must enter such trade-offs eyes-open with a retrofit plan.

q: Vulnerability patching is part of NFRs. Why frame patching as architecture-significant?
options: Banking regulators publish CVE patch deadlines that align with the bank's quarterly compliance reviews, making patching a primarily-compliance activity
Patch cadence drives architecture: zero-downtime patching requires blue-green or canary; quick CVE response requires automated CI/CD — these structural choices made or not at design time
Patching tools (e.g. apt, yum) are well-established commodity software, so listing patching as an NFR is more about budget than architectural decision
Vulnerability patching is mostly a vendor responsibility for the underlying open-source components, so the bank's role is primarily monitoring and not patching
tip: If patching requires 4h downtime, you've broken availability. If patching takes 6 weeks to test, you're exposed to CVEs. Both depend on architectural choices: blue-green deploys, canary rollouts, automated testing — all decided at design time. Treating patching as an afterthought leads to monthly maintenance windows or month-long CVE exposure. Hence ASR status.

q: Why does Andrei Sialila emphasise 'narrowing the scope and prioritising' specifically when discussing NFRs?
options: Banks tend to demand all-or-nothing on NFRs, so framing the trade-off as 'narrowing' helps the architect manage the demand-side conversation diplomatically
NFRs have compounding cost — adding one 9 to availability multiplies engineering effort; getting every NFR perfect means delivering nothing. Narrow scope = ship a usable system
Architecture sessions are time-limited and Andrei wanted to fit the discussion into the remaining minutes without opening every possible NFR for debate
Each NFR maps to a separate Architecture Decision Record, and the team wants to minimise the ADR backlog to keep documentation manageable
tip: 99% → 99.9% might cost 2× engineering; 99.9% → 99.99% might cost 5×. Each 9 added isn't linear cost. Same for every NFR. Demanding 'best in class' across all NFRs ensures the project never ships. Narrowing = picking which NFRs are truly required (e.g. 99.9% for payments) vs which can tolerate less (99% for reporting). Honest prioritisation prevents perfectionism paralysis.

q: An NFR like 'support for Portuguese language' looks small but can be architecturally significant when…
options: Most Portuguese-speaking customers in the target regions also speak French or English, so the requirement is mostly about preference not strict necessity
When the system MUST display all entities (messages, statements, reports) in Portuguese — driving an i18n framework, multi-language data model, and translation pipeline through every layer
Portuguese requires specific character-encoding choices in the database, which has knock-on effects on storage planning and replication configuration
Portuguese is a relatively recent addition to banking-system character sets, so existing libraries may not handle Portuguese-specific banking idioms reliably
tip: If Portuguese is only UI, it's a translation file. If Portuguese is required across statements, reports, compliance documents, and audit trails, then EVERY content-producing layer needs i18n support — including the DDD-driven dynamic text, error messages from plugins, regulatory reports. That's a system-wide concern, not a frontend feature. Hence: 'small NFR' can be massive depending on depth.

q: Why is dependency on 'bank's operations team and knowledge' itself listed as an NFR consideration?
options: Banks are legally responsible for their own operational teams under most banking regulations, so the dependency is a compliance acknowledgement rather than a design factor
Some NFRs (availability, failover, observability) require human action — the architecture can't realise them solely through software, so 'who operates this' is part of the architectural scope
Operational teams set their own KPIs which can conflict with CBS team SLAs, so listing them as a dependency helps surface the misalignment for negotiation
Bank operations teams typically work fixed shifts that don't cover 24/7, so the architecture must compensate with more automation in off-hours coverage
tip: Availability requires humans to triage alerts. Failover requires humans to validate the failover. Observability is useless without humans who can READ the dashboards. Treating these as 'someone else's problem' guarantees they fail in production. The architecture must include the operations model: who's on-call, what playbooks, what training. Skipping this is architecture incomplete.

q: Why does Andrei Sech specifically distinguish 'NFR figures are a mix of good-practice figures AND TBDs requiring alignment'?
options: It positions Andre's team as having done thorough background research, which is useful for the team's positioning in subsequent project negotiations
Mixing good-practice defaults with explicitly-TBD items makes the document HONEST about what's assumed vs what needs decision — avoiding silently-baked-in assumptions
The distinction is required by the bank's vendor governance framework which mandates a specific evidence pattern for sources of NFR figures
Defaults and TBDs are tracked in different sections of the JIRA project, and the labelling helps keep the project tracker tidy across categories
tip: Marking defaults as 'industry good practice' and TBDs as 'needs alignment' communicates two different invitations: 'we did our research here' vs 'we need your input here'. Hides nothing; surfaces decisions explicitly. Compare with a sloppy doc that mixes defaults and assumptions in the same prose — the bank then can't tell what they need to push back on. Document honesty is itself an NFR-management discipline.

q: Andrei Sech compares AA to 'a car manufacturing factory: cars are designed from components like engine, wheels'. The deepest banking parallel this analogy makes is…
options: The analogy is mainly rhetorical — it makes the architecture talk easier for non-technical stakeholders to grasp during the introduction segment
Reusable, standardised components beat bespoke design — same engine in many car models; same Interest component in many products. Banks gain scale exactly the way auto makers do
It signals that the team has cross-industry experience in manufacturing methods, which builds credibility with bank stakeholders who value diverse backgrounds
The car factory analogy is the canonical Temenos training metaphor, so adopting it signals alignment with Temenos best practice for the audience
tip: The factory metaphor isn't aesthetic — it's structural. Auto factories don't redesign every car; they assemble from a shared parts library. Banks adopting AA similarly stop hand-crafting each product; they assemble from a Component library. The benefits transfer directly: faster time-to-market, lower defect rate, easier maintenance, predictable economics. The metaphor explains WHY AA exists, not just what it looks like.

q: Product conditions vs Arrangement conditions: a Product Manager updates a Product's interest rate. What happens to existing customer Arrangements?
options: Their rate auto-changes immediately for everyone, including contracts already in production — this is the whole point of having a central product definition
Depends on TRACKING mode — TRACKING contracts pick up the new rate; NON.TRACKING are frozen at original; CUSTOMER.TRACKING is hybrid where only negotiated attributes stay fixed
Their rate never changes once an Arrangement is created — Arrangements are entirely independent of Product updates and only reference Products at creation time
Their rate changes at the next maturity rollover but not before, so existing contracts continue at the old rate until their term expires and renews
tip: The PRODUCT defines the master conditions; the ARRANGEMENT carries its own conditions (initially inherited). The link between them is governed by ARR.LINK. TRACKING = follow product updates (mass rate change applies). NON.TRACKING = locked at creation (the customer's rate is contractually fixed). CUSTOMER.TRACKING = some attributes locked (negotiated rate) while others track. This per-Arrangement control is what separates a flexible bank from a rigid one.

q: AA is described as having 'design stage, proof stage, ready-for-sale stage'. Why is 'proof' a SEPARATE step rather than being part of design?
options: Proof was originally part of design in earlier Temenos versions but was split out for clearer team responsibility — Product designers vs Product validators
Proof is a system-driven QUALITY GATE — it compiles, resolves inheritance, checks mandatory attributes — separating it from design forces validation before sale regardless of designer confidence
Proof is significantly more compute-intensive than design, so separating it allows the bank to schedule proof runs in off-peak hours to avoid load impact
Proof was added later as a regulatory requirement; the framework originally had only Design → Publish but regulators forced the intermediate stage
tip: If design and proof were one step, 'finishing design' could mean 'I think it's done' — depending on the designer's diligence. Separating proof forces the SYSTEM (not the designer) to verify completeness. The product manager cannot bypass proof; it's a structural gate. This catches errors a human designer might miss (missing accounting attribute, conflicting inheritance) — protecting customers from misconfigured products.

q: 'Effective and expiry dates' on a product enable scheduled changes. A bank wants a fee schedule to drop from $25 to $20 starting next quarter. The cleanest approach is…
options: Wait for the start date, then have a senior operator log in early that morning and manually update the fee field on the product record
Stage a NEW product version with EFFECTIVE.DATE = next quarter start; the system swaps automatically — no weekend ops needed
Create a new product (Eco-Saver-Q4) as a child of the current product, then redirect all customer arrangements to point to the new product on the start date
Add a conditional rule to the fee component that checks the current date and applies the new fee if today is past the threshold; existing product stays the same
tip: Manually editing on the day = weekend ops + human error risk. Effective-dating decouples WHEN you stage the change from WHEN it activates. Architects can plan months ahead; bank ops can verify long before; on the effective date, the system simply uses the new version. Same pattern for any time-based change: rates, fees, T&Cs, regulatory adjustments. Built-in scheduling.

q: Business vs technical AA components: a 'Penalty Schedule' is which?
options: Technical, because Penalty Schedule needs to call internal scheduler APIs and trigger system events, which are technical concerns by nature
Business, because it encodes business behaviour (when penalties trigger, how much, on what events) that bankers reason about directly
Both equally — the schedule's data is business but its execution mechanism is technical, so it straddles the categorisation
Neither — Penalty Schedule is not a Component in AA but a separate engine that lives outside the Arrangement Architecture framework
tip: Business components encode business semantics: Interest, Charges, Schedules, Limits — bankers can describe their behaviour without thinking about code. Technical components are framework concerns: Screen Forms, Extension Hooks, API Versioning — these support business components but encode no banking logic. Penalty Schedule is squarely business: bankers configure it; technicians don't need to understand it.

q: Multiple balance types (Current, Total Commitment, Accrual) exist 'dynamically'. Why dynamically rather than always present?
options: Dynamic appearance is a Temenos legacy implementation detail that doesn't reflect a design principle — fixed schemas would be cleaner if storage cost permitted
Only contracts that need them get them — a simple savings account doesn't need 'Total Commitment'; a complex revolving loan does — dynamic = right-sized data per contract
Dynamic means the system can compute balances faster because it skips fields that aren't relevant to a given contract type
Dynamic balance creation is a security feature that prevents one product from inadvertently exposing balance types it shouldn't know about
tip: If every Arrangement carried every possible balance type, simple products would waste storage and complicate queries with empty fields. Dynamic appearance means: the system MATERIALISES a balance type when the contract has a reason to track it (e.g. Total Commitment appears when there's an undrawn facility). Right-sized data structures; clearer semantics.

q: Andrei Sech says financial activities 'raise accounting entries based on ALLOCATION RULES defined at the PRODUCT level'. The architectural insight is…
options: Centralising at the Product level is a regulatory requirement under most banking compliance frameworks, which mandate single-source-of-truth for accounting rules
Accounting rules belong to the PRODUCT (where they're configured once) — not to each Arrangement (where they'd be duplicated thousands of times); centralisation enables consistency and easy updates
Product-level rules are stored in a different database from Arrangement-level data, so the separation reflects physical storage rather than design intent
Allocation rules are too complex to define per-Arrangement; the system rejects rule definitions at the Arrangement level for stability reasons
tip: If allocation rules lived on each Arrangement, updating them (say, a regulator changes how interest is posted) would mean editing thousands of records. Centralised at the Product level means: edit once, all Arrangements (with TRACKING) pick up the change. Consistency by default. Same principle as 'business rules on Product, not on UI/EOD' — single source of truth.

q: 'Any change to an arrangement is executed via an Activity'. The strongest reason for this strict rule is…
options: Code clarity for the engineering team — Activities have explicit names that make code review easier than direct field updates would be
Direct edits would bypass: validation, accounting impact, audit trail, lifecycle rules — forcing changes through Activities makes EVERY change rule-checked and observable
Activities are the only mechanism that supports backdating, so direct edits are forbidden specifically to ensure backdating remains universally available
Activities are billable line items in the bank's transaction cost accounting, while direct edits are not — the structure is driven by cost accounting policy
tip: Picture a developer 'just updating the interest rate field' directly. No allocation rule fires → accounting drifts. No audit entry → regulator can't trace. No lifecycle check → maybe the contract shouldn't allow changes in current state. Forcing changes through Activities means every change is intentional, validated, audited, and consistent. Removing the shortcut REMOVES the entire class of subtle bugs.

q: Activities support 'deterministic backdating and replay'. What problem does this specifically solve?
options: It enables historic reports to be re-run with the same outputs they originally produced, which is a regulatory requirement for audit reproducibility

An operator discovers a missing transaction from 3 days ago — needs to insert it without breaking subsequent activities — replay rewinds, inserts, replays, producing the state as if the activity had been there originally
It supports performance benchmarking by replaying historic loads against the new system under test, comparing throughput and latency profiles
It allows the bank to test what-if scenarios on production data by replaying historic activities with modified parameters in an isolated environment
tip: Naive backdating just changes a date and hopes for the best — but interest accruals between then and now would be wrong; subsequent activities depended on the wrong state. Replay rewinds all activities since the backdate, applies the new one, then re-runs the rewound ones — producing the exact state that WOULD have existed. Same answer as if no error had occurred. Critical for operational correctness without manual catch-up.

q: The Activity Orchestrator ensures 'identical behaviour across all channels'. Why is this hard to achieve without a central orchestrator?
options: Each channel's UX team prefers their own activity implementation because it gives them more control over the user experience without core team approval
Without a central path, each channel (mobile, branch, API) tends to implement its own version of the activity → behaviour drifts → identical operations produce different outcomes per channel
Channels run on different hardware so each compiles its own version of the activity logic, and without orchestration these versions diverge over time as they're maintained separately
Centralisation is a Temenos best practice that the team adopts mainly to align with Temenos terminology even though it's not strictly necessary for correctness
tip: Pre-orchestrator world: mobile app posts a transfer one way; branch teller posts it another way; corporate API a third. Subtle differences emerge — different fees applied, different validations, different audit detail. Orchestrator centralises the activity logic; every channel just triggers the same Activity. Same outcome, regardless of entry point. Eliminates 'channel-dependent behaviour' bugs.

q: End-of-Day processing is described as a 'staged, dependency-driven orchestration layer'. The 'staged' part means…
options: Staging refers to running EOD in a staging environment first before production — the dependency graph protects the staging environment from data corruption
EOD jobs run in DEPENDENCY ORDER — interest accruals before capitalisation before reports — so no job operates on stale data from a not-yet-run predecessor
Staging means the engine pauses between each job for operator approval, ensuring human-in-the-loop oversight for every step of the EOD cycle
Staging is a database concept — each stage commits a sub-transaction so partial failures only roll back to the last stage rather than the whole batch
tip: If EOD jobs ran in random order, reports would generate before accruals finished — producing wrong numbers. Staged = explicit dependency graph: 'capitalisation needs accruals'; 'reports need both'. Each stage waits for predecessors. The 5-Phase COB (A-S-R-D-O) is this principle made concrete. Lets the bank know reports are ALWAYS accurate at the time they're generated.

q: AA 'centralises business rules into property classes'. The complexity-management win is…
options: Property classes are stored centrally because the database's referential integrity constraints require it; the centralisation is driven by DB design not business architecture
Bank logic isn't scattered across products — adding a new product reuses property classes; updating a property class improves every product that uses it. Linear scaling instead of quadratic
Property classes reduce the bank's licensing cost with the CBS vendor because they count as shared assets rather than per-product modules
Property classes are required by IFRS for product configurations to be auditable, so centralisation is a compliance prerequisite
tip: Without property classes, every product re-encodes the interest logic. 10 products = 10 copies of interest logic = 10 places to fix bugs. With property classes, interest is ONE thing; 10 products use it. Adding product #11 = configure components, not write logic. Adding a new behaviour to interest = one change, benefits 11 products. This is what makes the catalogue scale beyond a handful of products.

q: AA's 'activity history' is praised as easier to audit than traditional change logs. The deeper reason is…
options: Activity history uses a more compact storage format that performs better in audit-query workloads compared to row-level change tables
Activity history is BUSINESS-SEMANTIC: 'CHANGE-INTEREST 5%→6% on 2026-03-15 by user X' — readable by auditors. Traditional change logs are FIELD-LEVEL: 'rate field changed' — readable by developers
Activity history is encrypted at rest using stronger key sizes than field-level change logs, meeting more stringent audit security requirements
Activity history is automatically replicated to the audit-only database while field-level change logs stay on the operational database, separating concerns
tip: Field-level change logs make auditors hunt: 'this field changed — but what business action caused it?' Activity-level history says directly: this was a 'CHANGE-INTEREST' activity by this user on this date. Auditors and regulators speak business — and the audit trail meets them in their language. Less translation work; less ambiguity; faster audits.

q: AA-tracked contracts let 'a single product update cascade to all existing contracts'. What's the operational scale benefit?
options: It reduces the bank's IT spend because central-bank rate changes no longer require external consultancy work to deploy across the product catalogue
When central bank changes base rate, the bank updates ONE product → thousands of contracts adjust automatically. Manual approach = thousands of contract edits, days of work, error risk
Cascade updates run faster than direct contract edits because the database can use bulk-update operations that streamline the I/O patterns
Cascade updates allow the bank to negotiate volume discounts with the central bank because it can demonstrate uniform rate application across its book
tip: Without cascade, a base-rate change requires touching every contract. For a retail bank with 200,000 mortgages, that's 200,000 edits. Cascade = update the product condition; tracker propagates. Done in minutes; zero human error. The bank can react to market/regulatory changes at the speed of the change itself, not at the speed of back-office bandwidth.

q: Trade-off: AA introduces 'performance and scalability constraints due to activity overhead'. Why accept this cost?
options: Activities are a Temenos best practice and adopting them aligns with industry-standard reference architectures used in CEMAC banking deployments
Activity overhead buys consistency, auditability, deterministic backdating, atomic transactions, governance — qualities that ad-hoc direct edits would sacrifice
Activities are the only mechanism that supports multi-currency Arrangements properly, so the overhead is the price of multicurrency support
Activity overhead is reduced by automatic query-plan caching, so the trade-off is mostly theoretical and rarely surfaces in production deployments
tip: Each Activity wrapper adds milliseconds of overhead. Multiplied across thousands per day, that adds up — but compared to running an inconsistent ledger, it's nothing. The trade-off is: small predictable performance cost vs. eliminating an entire CLASS of correctness bugs. Engineering teams have universally chosen the former. AA's overhead is the price of a reliable system.

q: Trade-off: AA has 'integration complexity requiring bulk processing'. Why does AA require bulk-mode integration?
options: Bulk processing is required because Activities incur licensing fees per call, and processing high-volume integrations one-by-one would be financially prohibitive
External systems often deliver many transactions at once (a file from clearing); AA's per-activity overhead becomes prohibitive at high volume unless bulk processing batches activities efficiently
Bulk processing is mandated by SWIFT and ISO 20022 messaging standards, which both prefer batched submission to single-message delivery
Bulk processing is the only way the system can fit into the bank's existing nightly batch windows without breaking traditional ops cadences
tip: AA optimises for correctness per activity. When 50,000 incoming SEPA payments arrive, processing them one-activity-at-a-time saturates the orchestrator. Bulk processing batches similar activities, sharing setup/teardown. The architecture must explicitly support bulk — without it, high-volume integrations stall. This is the operational reality of accepting AA: you get the wins but must engineer bulk paths.

q: Trade-off: 'data spread across multiple records' increases operational complexity. The concrete pain?
options: Investigation tools (Composite Screens, drill-downs) are not yet mature in early AA implementations, so investigators rely on raw database access initially

An Arrangement's full state is in ARRANGEMENT + balance records + activity history + condition records — a single bug investigation may need to join across all these. Simpler 'one row per loan' was easier to spelunk
Records are physically stored across multiple disks for performance, so investigation queries must coordinate across storage subsystems each time
AA encrypts each record separately, so investigation requires decryption keys for every record type — adding friction to debugging workflows
tip: Legacy 'one big loan row' was a debugging shortcut: open one record, see everything. AA spreads across Arrangement, Conditions, Activities, Balances — for very good reasons (each has its own lifecycle, audit, update semantics). But for the engineer chasing 'why is this loan in this state?', it means joining multiple records. The system needs good tooling (Composite Screens, Activity History views) to compensate.

q: Negotiation rules let a relationship manager grant a slight rate discount within pre-approved limits. Why bake this into AA rather than 'just letting them edit the rate'?
options: Hardcoding negotiation rules means relationship managers can't accidentally enter an invalid rate, which improves data quality for the bank's reporting
Limits enforce risk policy SYSTEMICALLY (e.g. max 0.5% below standard) — preventing rogue negotiation that exposes the bank. Customer gets flexibility; bank retains control
Negotiation rules are required by IFRS for reporting purposes — without them, the bank cannot distinguish negotiated rates from standard rates in regulatory reports
Negotiation rules are stored separately from rate definitions so each can be audited independently, which simplifies the SOX-style separation-of-duties review
tip: Free-form rate editing = relationship manager could give a 5% discount to a friend → bank loses money. Negotiation rules: 'this rate is negotiable, MIN/MAX defined' → relationship manager can offer up to the floor, not below. Customer happy; bank's risk policy enforced automatically. The structural control replaces approval workflows.

q: The reverse-and-replay capability handles backdating 'automatically'. What manual process does this replace?
options: It replaces the manual process of asking the customer to re-submit the transaction with corrected dates, which was error-prone and reflected poorly on service
Operator computing the delta between 'what happened' and 'what should have happened', then manually posting corrective entries to bridge — error-prone and slow
It replaces the bank's previous use of external accounting consultants who would manually reconcile backdated transactions, removing a costly outsourcing dependency
It replaces the legacy approach of running a separate batch overnight to compute corrections, eliminating the gap between detection and correction
tip: Pre-replay: backdating meant calculating each affected accrual/event by hand, posting reversals, posting the new transaction, recomputing balances — hours of work per case, frequent errors. Replay does this mechanically: it knows every activity since the backdate, can mathematically rewind/redo each. Operator effort drops to seconds; correctness becomes mechanical.

q: Oleksandr Stashenko noted that 'arrangements and activities have not been found built so far — may need to be built from scratch'. The strategic implication is…
options: AA's value is concentrated in product design and there's no need to build Arrangements/Activities — once products are right, customer instances are trivially derivable
AA's value is in the FULL chain (Product → Arrangement → Activity → posting). Having Products but no Arrangements/Activities = the architecture isn't realised; significant build work remains
AA's value lies in its terminology and the team should focus on the missing components last, since the existing Products already capture the main intellectual work
AA's value can be approximated by skipping Arrangements and going directly from Products to posting via custom adapters, saving the build effort for that layer
tip: Products without Arrangements = templates with no instances. Arrangements without Activities = state without controlled changes. The architectural value only materialises when the full chain is built and connected. Recognising this gap shapes Phase Zero priorities: build the missing chain links before scaling outward to specific products.

q: Why is the 'reusable building blocks' framing more valuable for a bank's COMPETITIVE POSITION than 'fast product launches' alone?
options: Faster launches are the primary business value — competitive position is mostly about speed, and reusable blocks are an implementation detail to enable that
Reusable blocks mean every NEW product improves the COMMON layer; over time the bank's capability platform compounds — unlike each product being its own silo where improvements don't transfer
Reusable blocks reduce IT spend because the same blocks are used across products, which spreads the development cost across more revenue-generating products
Reusable blocks let the bank acquire other banks more easily since their product catalogues can be assimilated via the standard block library faster
tip: Fast launches help once. Reusable blocks compound: building product #5 makes building product #6 cheaper, because the components are now battle-tested and richer. Competitors who chose siloed products see linear development cost; banks with AA see decreasing marginal cost per product. Over years, that gap is enormous. The architectural choice is a compounding strategic asset.

q: AA component activity-based processing solves 'distributed posting responsibilities risking inconsistent financial behavior'. The mechanism?
options: All financial state changes go through the Accounting service which is the single authoritative posting service the entire bank relies on for consistency
All financial state changes route through Activities → single posting pipeline → posting can't disagree with itself because there's only one source
All financial state changes are recorded in a central event store that's continuously reconciled by an automated job, eliminating drift retroactively
All financial state changes use synchronous distributed transactions across modules, ensuring atomicity at the cost of some latency overhead
tip: Pre-AA: posting logic scattered across modules (FT module posts its way; teller module posts its way; loan module posts a third way). Discrepancies emerge as the codebase diverges. AA: all posting flows through the activity → allocation rule → entry pipeline. One canonical path. If it has a bug, everything has it (which is detectable); if it works, everything works.

q: How does AA's strict 'arrangement architecture-based for contract modules' help future regulatory adaptation?
options: AA's product hierarchy is purely an internal organisational concept — regulators see only the final published products and don't care about the hierarchy
Regulators evolving rules (capital treatment, accounting, disclosure) typically target product CATEGORIES; AA's product hierarchy lets banks apply regulatory updates at the right level uniformly
AA's product hierarchy lets the bank pre-anticipate regulatory categories by mapping them in advance, eliminating the need for regulatory consulting on category mapping
AA's product hierarchy mirrors Basel III risk weight categories exactly, so regulatory adaptation is automatic for capital-related rule changes
tip: A regulator says 'all mortgages over 20 years now require X disclosure'. Without product hierarchy, you'd hunt for affected products. AA: update the relevant Product Class or Product Type once; all child products inherit. Regulatory change ↔ architecture's inheritance points line up — making adaptations predictable, not exploratory.

q: The activity orchestrator unifies 'validation, execution, posting, auditing'. The deepest correctness benefit is…
options: These four steps run in well-defined separate transactions, each independently transactional, so partial completion of some is acceptable for higher overall throughput
These four steps are guaranteed to run TOGETHER in the same atomic transaction — partial failure rolls all back; partial success is impossible
These four steps are logged into the same database table for auditing, which makes post-hoc reconciliation possible even if they run separately
These four steps are eventually consistent via a background reconciliation job that aligns any temporary mismatches between the steps within a short window
tip: Imagine: validation passes, execution succeeds, posting succeeds, auditing fails. Without orchestration, you have a posted entry with no audit trail — terrible for compliance. With orchestrated atomicity, all four either succeed together or none do. The system is never in a half-done state. This atomicity is what makes the audit trail TRUSTWORTHY: if an entry exists, its audit record exists too.

q: AA's effective-dated versioning is described as letting business analysts 'schedule future-dated changes'. The control-flow advantage over manual scheduling is…
options: Effective dating is mainly a documentation convenience — it lets architects record when changes 'should' apply, but the actual activation requires manual operator action
The change is staged TODAY (reviewable, approvable, testable) but activates on its effective date — the bank gates the change before activation, not at activation time — eliminating last-minute panic
Effective dating eliminates the need for change-management approval workflows, since the future-date IS the approval, accelerating governance
Effective dating ensures the database always has a future snapshot ready, so reads can use either current or future state without recomputation
tip: Manual: 'change the rate on April 1' = an action happening on April 1, when something might go wrong, no one's there to fix it. Effective-dated: 'change is reviewed and committed in March; activates April 1'. By April 1, the change has been approved, validated, signed off — activation is just a system tick. Same effect; far lower operational risk.

q: Why was Strapi (a headless CMS) chosen for managing CBS web-app configuration, when CBS has its own DDD?
options: DDD and Strapi serve overlapping needs and the team picked Strapi mainly because it's the more visually appealing of the two for stakeholder demos
DDD governs CORE data structures (Tables, Fields, validation); Strapi governs FRONT-END concerns (visualisation, dynamic UI elements, copy/messaging) — these are orthogonal concerns
DDD is too heavy for frontend use and would slow down rendering, so Strapi takes on the frontend role to keep page-load times within SLA
DDD is mainly used in the database layer while Strapi is used in the application layer, and the architecture wants to keep these strictly separated
tip: DDD shapes the data layer — what fields exist, what validation applies, how the database is structured. Strapi shapes the presentation layer — what users SEE, how content is arranged, what marketing copy displays. Both are 'dynamic configuration' but at different layers. Trying to bend DDD to handle presentation would muddy its purpose; using a CMS for that role keeps each tool in its lane.

q: Keycloak is reused (rather than building a custom IAM). The clearest reason is…
options: Building IAM is technically straightforward — the team chose Keycloak mainly because it's already running for Payment Hub and operational reuse is convenient
IAM (OAuth, OIDC, SAML, SCIM, MFA, federation) is a deep specialty with constant security updates — reusing a mature engine eliminates a huge surface area the team would otherwise need to maintain
Keycloak comes with a strong vendor support contract that the bank already pays for separately, so reusing it leverages an existing licensing investment
Keycloak integrates uniquely with the bank's Active Directory implementation in ways that custom IAM wouldn't support, making it the only viable option
tip: Building IAM looks straightforward — until you handle token rotation, MFA, social logins, federated identities, SAML edge cases, attacks (token replay, CSRF). It's a forever-evolving security battleground. Reusing Keycloak means: get a battle-tested engine, benefit from upstream patches, focus team effort on banking logic. The architecturally healthy choice is 'don't roll your own auth'.

q: PostgreSQL is the transactional DB. The architecture leaves OLAP database 'under discussion'. The decision should depend on…
options: Cost — OLAP databases require additional licensing and the architecture is conservatively deferring that spend until phase one when budget is clearer

Reporting load profile — if reporting is light AND can tolerate eventual consistency, PostgreSQL replicas suffice; if heavy analytical queries need column-store performance, an OLAP store is justified
Regulatory compliance — OLAP databases need separate compliance certifications which the bank hasn't completed, so the decision waits on compliance team review
Vendor relationship — the team is evaluating whether to consolidate vendors for OLTP and OLAP or use separate specialists, which affects the final tooling choice
tip: Over-engineering risk: adding an OLAP store doubles operational complexity (replication, schema sync, query routing). Under-engineering risk: heavy analytics on PostgreSQL slows the OLTP path. The 'right' answer depends on MEASURED load, not assumption. Postpone the decision until real workload data exists — the architecture's open question is intentionally so.

q: Why was Kafka chosen as the primary event platform, with RabbitMQ 'depending on future requirements'?
options: Kafka is significantly cheaper than RabbitMQ at scale and the architecture optimises for cost rather than functional capability differences between the two systems

Kafka excels at high-throughput persistent event logs (audit, webhook fan-out); RabbitMQ is better for request-reply patterns. Most CBS needs match Kafka; reserve RabbitMQ for use cases that justify the operational addition
Kafka has stronger integration with the bank's existing Active Directory than RabbitMQ does, which simplifies the security configuration significantly
Kafka and RabbitMQ are interchangeable in this architecture; the choice is mainly stylistic and the team picked Kafka because it's more modern
tip: Kafka and RabbitMQ are NOT interchangeable — Kafka is a distributed log (events replay, persistent); RabbitMQ is a smart router (queues, routing, RPC). CBS's primary needs (event sourcing, audit replays, webhook fan-out) match Kafka. Adding RabbitMQ adds operational burden — only justified if a specific use case clearly needs queue semantics. Default to one; add the other only on demand.

q: ElasticSearch is chosen 'to maintain a simple team skill set' (existing Payment Hub use). The MOST important non-obvious lesson is…
options: ElasticSearch outperforms competitor products like Splunk on every benchmark relevant to banking workloads, making it the objectively superior choice
Operational excellence requires deep skill — using a tool the team already operates well is better than picking the theoretically best tool the team would learn from scratch
ElasticSearch is the only logging system that supports the encryption-at-rest standards required by South Sudan's banking regulations as of 2026
ElasticSearch has been donated free of charge to the bank as part of a vendor partnership, so the choice reflects budget reality rather than technical preference
tip: Comparing Splunk, Datadog, ElasticSearch — the 'best' depends on heaps of dimensions. But if the team already runs ElasticSearch successfully in Payment Hub, they ALREADY have: monitoring, backup, alerting, on-call experience. Picking the same tech for CBS = leveraging that operational maturity. Architecture choices include team-context, not just tool-feature comparisons.

q: The architecture uses C4 notation. Why C4 over UML for explaining the system to stakeholders?
options: C4 is the only architecture notation the bank's reviewers are trained in, so the choice is driven by audience familiarity rather than notation features
C4 has a defined ZOOM HIERARCHY (Context → Container → Component → Code) that lets the same diagram set communicate at different abstraction levels for different audiences
C4 outputs are stored as version-controlled text files which integrate better with git workflows than UML's binary diagram formats do
C4 produces smaller image files than UML diagrams, which reduces the storage burden on documentation systems like Confluence
tip: UML overwhelms with notation. C4 limits to four levels of abstraction, each appropriate for a different conversation: Context for execs, Container for architects, Component for developers, Code for individual contributors. Same architecture, different views per audience. Predictable; teachable; clean. Avoids the common architecture-diagram disease of 'too much detail for some, too little for others'.

q: The Application Registry 'allows banks to implement custom applications within the CBS framework'. What's the strategic value vs. forcing vendor-only applications?
options: It gives the CBS vendor more billable hours since custom applications often need vendor consulting to integrate correctly with the registry framework

Banks always have unique workflows (regional, regulatory, business-specific) that a generic CBS can't anticipate — letting them inject custom applications lets the bank own its differentiation while CBS provides the platform
Vendor-only applications would tie the bank to a single vendor's release schedule, and the bank wants to negotiate parallel vendor relationships for diversity
Custom applications can be developed faster than vendor applications because the bank's internal team has direct access to the underlying source code
tip: If only vendor apps were allowed, every bank would have to wait for vendor releases for any unique need — slow, expensive, sometimes impossible. Custom apps via the Registry = bank engineering can build their differentiated capability on top of the platform. CBS becomes a PLATFORM, not a closed product. Banks adopt because they retain control over their unique edge.
