q: The 'Application Orchestrator within the registry' handles API composition, bulk processing, dynamic query engine. Why combine these three?
options: These three operations happen to share the same database connection pool, so grouping them in one component is an infrastructure-resource optimisation
All three are CROSS-CUTTING concerns over the registered applications — handling them centrally avoids each app reinventing them; provides consistent behaviour across the platform
These three operations are required by the Temenos OFS specification to be implemented together, so the grouping mirrors the OFS standard requirements
These three operations form the bottleneck of the system, so colocating them helps the operations team monitor and tune performance in one component
tip: Each application could implement its own bulk processing, its own query engine, its own API composition. The result: 20 implementations, all subtly different. Centralising in the Orchestrator: one battle-tested implementation, consistent behaviour, easier optimisation. Applications focus on their unique value; the platform handles the universal concerns.

q: The External Event Publisher exposes events via webhooks to external consumers. From an integration architecture perspective, what does this enable that a polling-based API doesn't?
options: Webhooks reduce the bank's total integration count because external partners can subscribe to many event types through a single connection
Push-based reactivity — partners react to events in near real-time without polling — reduces partner integration latency and reduces CBS's API load
Webhooks are mandated by the FATF (Financial Action Task Force) framework for real-time AML alert distribution to partner systems
Webhooks integrate natively with the bank's Active Directory authentication system, simplifying credential management for external partners
tip: Polling-based: external systems hit the CBS every N seconds, asking 'anything new?' — wastes both sides' resources, introduces latency. Webhook-based: CBS pushes events as they happen. Partner systems react immediately; CBS doesn't field hundreds of polls per second. Architecture supports both patterns, but webhooks scale better for high-event-frequency banking integrations.

q: Financial Crime Management (FCM) acts as a 'gateway' for external providers — screening, sanction checks, AML, KYC. Why gateway pattern rather than direct provider calls per service?
options: The gateway pattern is mandated by FATCA reporting requirements which require all external compliance calls to flow through a single auditable component
Centralising in a gateway: (a) easier to swap providers, (b) consistent retry/timeout policy, (c) single point for compliance auditing, (d) hides provider variability from the rest of the system
The gateway pattern is significantly faster than direct provider calls because the gateway maintains persistent connections that avoid TLS handshake overhead
The gateway pattern is a Temenos best practice that all CBS-style architectures adopt because Temenos's customers expect it for migration ease
tip: Without a gateway: every service calling FCM has its own retry logic, its own provider config. Provider migration = touching many services. Audits = hunting across services. Gateway pattern: callers see ONE API; behind the gateway, providers can be swapped, retried, A/B tested. Encapsulates provider details cleanly; FCM team owns the gateway, callers don't care.

q: 24/7 operations are 'supported by switching clocks'. What's the conceptual mechanism?
options: Server clocks are synchronised across all data centres so 'today' is a global concept — the COB advances the synchronised global clock at the boundary

Business date is decoupled from server clock — when COB runs, it advances the business date; transactions during COB are accepted but value-dated to the NEXT business day, so the bank stays operational while books close
The system uses different clocks in different data centres so it can serve one day in DC1 and the next day in DC2 simultaneously during COB
Cobalt-tracked operators manually advance the clock based on regional time zones, with each region running on its own synchronised clock setting
tip: Naive approach: shut the bank during COB. Clock-switching approach: COB processes 'yesterday' (the day being closed) while the system simultaneously accepts 'today' transactions value-dated forward. Clock switching = the system effectively serves both days at once during the transition window. Customers see no downtime; books close cleanly. Engineering trick that hugely improves customer experience.

q: Object storage uses SeaweedFS (S3-compatible). What architectural property does S3-compatibility give the system?
options: Bank IT prefers S3 because they already have S3 expertise from cloud projects, even though SeaweedFS is the underlying technology choice
S3 is an industry-standard interface — the bank can later swap SeaweedFS for AWS S3, MinIO, or another provider WITHOUT changing application code that uses the storage interface
S3-compatibility means the storage can be accessed by the bank's existing analytics tools, which expect the S3 protocol for data-lake integrations
S3-compatibility is a regulatory requirement under most cloud-storage compliance frameworks the bank is subject to in CEMAC and UEMOA
tip: Picking a proprietary blob storage means: switching providers = rewriting every storage call. S3-compatible = a standard interface; SeaweedFS today, MinIO tomorrow, AWS S3 the day after = same code. This is interface-based portability. Especially valuable for banks where data sovereignty or vendor-mix may force changes over time.

q: The architecture distinguishes 'CBS-managed infrastructure' from 'bank-managed infrastructure' (DNS, firewalls, VPNs, etc.). The boundary line communicates…
options: It's a contractual matter so the bank can charge the vendor for any infrastructure costs that fall on the bank's side of the boundary
Where CBS team's responsibility ends and the bank's begins — important for SLA, incident response, and security audit conversations. Ambiguity here causes blame games and incident delays
It documents which side handles licensing and procurement decisions, which is a financial governance concern rather than an operational architectural one
It marks the security boundary between trusted CBS infrastructure and untrusted bank-managed infrastructure, with mTLS at the boundary line
tip: Pre-defined boundary: incidents have clear ownership ('that's the bank's DNS layer' vs 'that's CBS routing'). Ambiguous boundary: incidents devolve into who's responsible. SLAs depend on this clarity. Documenting the boundary IS architecture work — it shapes operations as much as the code.

q: Why does the architecture integrate via Keycloak with the bank's 'internal identity provider, typically Active Directory'?
options: AD federation is required by SOX-style compliance frameworks the bank is subject to, making it a compliance prerequisite rather than an architectural preference

Bank employees already have AD accounts — federating via Keycloak means CBS doesn't maintain a duplicate user store, and employee provisioning/deprovisioning continues to flow through bank HR processes
AD federation provides significantly faster authentication than building a custom user store, which is critical at the bank's expected user concurrency
AD federation reduces licensing cost since CBS pays per user and federated users count toward AD's quota rather than CBS's quota
tip: Duplicate user stores mean: when an employee leaves, the bank disables AD but forgets CBS = security gap. Federation means: AD is source of truth; deactivation in AD instantly propagates to CBS. Employee lifecycle stays in bank HR/IT control; CBS is downstream. Keycloak handles the protocol translation. Standard, secure, low-friction.

q: Financial reporting is described as 'primarily through asynchronous events to ensure scalability and avoid system blocking'. Why async rather than sync queries?
options: Sync reports use too much memory because they materialise the full result set in memory before returning, while async streams results progressively
Heavy reports could lock or slow the OLTP path; async means reports consume events at their own pace into their own store — keeping the transactional core fast
Async is required by SWIFT for financial reporting submissions because sync paths cannot guarantee delivery to the central reporting authorities
Async reports have higher throughput at the cost of accuracy, and banking reporting prioritises throughput over absolute accuracy at the per-record level
tip: Sync reports against OLTP DB = report query slows the transactional system. Heavy reports could lock tables, exhaust connections, starve customer-facing transactions. Async pattern: events flow from OLTP to a reporting store; reports query the reporting store — never touching OLTP. Decoupling protects the customer-facing path's responsiveness.

q: The COB engine 'handles rollbacks and mechanisms' for 24/7 ops. The non-trivial part of COB rollback in 24/7 context is…
options: COB rollback is only used during testing phases — in production, COB never rolls back so the design challenge is mostly hypothetical
Once COB has advanced the business date and accepted 'next day' transactions, rolling back COB risks invalidating those future-dated transactions — needs careful staging to preserve them
Rollback uses a checkpoint mechanism that snapshots the entire system state every minute, which makes rollback trivial but uses significant storage
Rollback is handled by a dedicated team of operators who manually unwind the changes, with no automated rollback support in COB itself
tip: Naive rollback: undo everything, restart COB. But during 24/7 ops, some 'next day' transactions have already been accepted. Rolling COB back without preserving them = data loss. The engine must rollback its own work while protecting forward-committed transactions. This is a non-trivial concurrency problem the architecture explicitly accounts for.

q: Why was bare-metal VM development environment chosen, rather than cheaper cloud VMs?
options: Bare-metal VMs are slightly cheaper than cloud equivalents when amortised over the project lifetime, so cost is the primary driver of this choice
Development infrastructure must MIRROR production closely — production is bare metal, so dev should be too — cloud VMs would mask production-specific issues
Bare-metal VMs offer better debugging capabilities because the team has direct hardware access for low-level diagnostics that cloud doesn't permit
Bare-metal VMs are required by the bank's security policy because cloud VMs are considered untrusted infrastructure under most banking compliance frameworks
tip: Cloud-dev/bare-metal-prod divergence = bugs found in production that worked perfectly in dev. The point of dev environment is to catch issues BEFORE production. Mirroring production minimises 'works in dev, fails in prod' surprises. Bare-metal dev costs more but de-risks production. Engineering economics often favour 'expensive dev that catches bugs early'.

q: Configurability is listed as the fifth ASR alongside Security/Availability/Scalability/Observability. What does treating it as architecture-significant prevent?
options: Configurability without architectural support tends to be slower to implement, so calling it an ASR helps prioritise the engineering effort early in phase zero
Hard-coded customer-specific or region-specific values that, when changed, force a release — architectural configurability means changes are config-only, no code release for routine adaptation
Configurability is a Temenos-marketing concept and elevating it to ASR signals that the architecture is genuinely Temenos-aligned in its decision priorities
Configurability scores high on customer NPS surveys, so making it an ASR ensures the architecture optimises for customer satisfaction metrics
tip: Without configurability as ASR: a country-specific holiday rule gets coded; a region-specific report format gets coded; etc. Every adaptation = release. Banks slow down. With configurability as ASR: changes are configuration loads, instantly testable, instantly reversible. The architecture buys ITSELF the right to evolve without releases.

q: Andrei Sialila reminded that 'every requirement adds complexity'. From an architecture review standpoint, this should drive what behaviour?
options: Architects should accept all stakeholder requirements and let the implementation team push back later, since architecture's role is to capture intent not negotiate scope
RUTHLESS prioritisation — for each requirement, explicitly ask: what does NOT delivering this cost? Often the answer is 'less than building it', and the requirement should be cut
Architects should focus on technical purity and let project management handle prioritisation conversations with stakeholders, keeping concerns separate
Architects should defer prioritisation to the final design review when more information is available about likely effort and impact for each requirement
tip: Stakeholders are biased to add features; nobody's bonus depends on cutting them. Architecture reviews are where 'no' is structurally easier — the review weighs cost. Without that discipline, the system bloats with features no one uses but everyone maintains. Saying no early is cheaper than removing late; ruthless prioritisation is itself an architecture practice.

q: An external consumer wants to read account balances. Should this go through (a) a direct DB read, (b) an API gateway, (c) a webhook subscription, or (d) the External Event Publisher?
options: Direct DB read — fastest path to current balance data with minimal middleware overhead, since balances are time-sensitive and latency matters
API gateway — synchronous reads of CURRENT state are pull-based and should use the gateway's auth/rate-limiting/observability
Webhook subscription for every balance change — partners need real-time updates and webhooks deliver them immediately without polling
External Event Publisher — balance events are continuously published, so subscribing gives the consumer the latest state derivable from the event stream
tip: Reading current state = synchronous pull = API gateway. Notifications about CHANGE = async push = External Event Publisher. Different consumer patterns demand different mechanisms. The architecture explicitly separates them so external consumers pick the right shape: 'show me current balance' (gateway), 'tell me when it changes' (events). Forcing the wrong pattern (webhook for current reads, gateway for change notifications) gives bad UX and load characteristics.

q: An engineer suggests merging the BFF (Backend-For-Frontend) responsibilities into the core CBS API layer to reduce 'one extra service'. The architect rejects this. The strongest principled reason is…
options: The BFF runs slower than the core API, so combining them would improve perceived latency for the web app's end users in branch contexts
Separation of concerns — BFF serves ONE consumer (the web app) and aggregates data for it; the CBS API serves MANY consumers and must remain consumer-agnostic. Merging couples them
Banking regulators in CEMAC mandate API separation under the new core-banking compliance framework released in 2025, making it a regulatory rather than design decision
The BFF and core API use incompatible programming languages (Next.js vs Java) so merging them is technically infeasible regardless of the architectural philosophy
tip: If the CBS API absorbed BFF logic, it would start shaping responses for the web app — at which point a mobile app or partner API integrating with CBS would suffer from web-app-specific quirks. Keeping BFF separate means: the core API stays clean and reusable; the BFF can aggregate, transform, and shape data freely for ONE consumer; future consumers (mobile, partners) get fresh BFFs without disturbing each other. Classic separation-of-concerns win.

q: In the Application Registry, what's the precise distinction between an 'Action' and an 'Activity'?
options: Actions and Activities are interchangeable terms inherited from Temenos terminology — the framework treats them identically inside the orchestrator's execution engine
An Action is a SINGLE piece of reusable function (e.g. 'validate amount'); an Activity is a GROUP of actions composed into a business operation (e.g. 'DISBURSE-LOAN')
Activities are larger units of code measured in lines, while Actions are smaller and typically don't exceed a few dozen lines of business logic
Activities are named with verbs and Actions with nouns — the distinction is naming convention rather than a structural difference in the framework
tip: Action = smallest reusable unit ('check balance', 'apply fee', 'validate signature'). Activity = orchestrated set of Actions forming a meaningful business operation ('LENDING-DISBURSE-COMMITMENT' might compose: validate, draw on Term Amount, post to Account, raise accounting). This composition lets the same Action be reused across many Activities without duplication. Activities are user/system-triggered; Actions are building blocks.

q: The Application Orchestrator within the registry is described as handling 'API composition, bulk processing, AND dynamic query engine'. Why pair the query engine WITH composition + bulk?
options: These three operations share the same database connection pool, so co-locating them in the orchestrator is an infrastructure-level optimisation for connections

All three serve cross-application read patterns: compose data from many apps in one response, process many records in bulk, query across apps dynamically — sharing a single optimised path benefits all three
The Temenos OFS specification requires these three to be implemented together in the same component, so the architecture follows the OFS structure exactly
These three operations form the bottleneck of the platform, so colocating them gives the operations team a single component to monitor and tune for performance
tip: If each app implemented its own composition / bulk / query, you'd get 20 inconsistent implementations. The Application Orchestrator runs ONE optimised read engine — applications register their data shapes; the engine composes responses, runs bulk operations, executes cross-app queries (OFS-style). One mature implementation; consistent semantics; easier to optimise and audit. Less code, more reliability.

q: Strapi serves dual roles: dynamic UI configuration AND caching layer. Why combine these in one component rather than separate them?
options: Strapi's licensing model bundles a CMS and cache together, so using only one half of the bundle would waste the licensing investment the bank has already made
UI configuration changes rarely; serving it through a CMS that's already cache-aware avoids adding a separate cache for static-ish frontend assets — Strapi naturally caches what it serves
Strapi has a unique caching algorithm optimised for banking workloads that competitors like WordPress don't offer, making the combined role essential
The bank requires every cache layer to use a CMS-backed configuration interface for compliance reasons, so caching outside a CMS would violate policy
tip: UI configuration (layouts, copy, validation rules) is changed by admins, then read by thousands of users — perfect for caching. Strapi inherently caches its content delivery, so the same component handles both 'configure' and 'serve cached'. Adding a separate Redis/Memcached layer for the same purpose would mean two systems to manage with overlapping concerns. Architectural minimalism: don't add a layer when an existing one suffices.

q: Why does Keycloak need to handle BOTH user pool management AND service-to-service token validation?
options: OAuth and OIDC are different protocols and Keycloak is the only IAM that supports both, which the bank needs for human and service authentication respectively

Both are identity questions — humans authenticating to APIs and services authenticating to each other use the same protocols (OAuth/OIDC) — reusing one engine means consistent token policy, key rotation, and revocation
Keycloak performs faster at human authentication than service-to-service so using it for both ensures balanced load characteristics across the IAM infrastructure
The bank's licensing agreement with the IAM vendor allows only one identity provider, so Keycloak handles both as a constraint rather than a design choice
tip: Separating human-auth and service-auth into different engines means: two key rotation policies; two revocation flows; subtle policy drift. One engine = one source of truth for identity. When a token issue arises (compromised key, revocation), you act in one place. Keycloak's flexibility — supporting humans (login flows) and services (client credentials) — is why it's chosen for both.

q: The Activity Orchestrator receives activities from COB, the Application Registry, AND the API. Why expose three different entry points?
options: The orchestrator is a stateless component so it doesn't matter where activities originate — having multiple entry points doesn't add architectural complexity

Activities can be triggered by: scheduled batch (COB), custom bank applications (registry), or external/user request (API) — all three are legitimate origins and converge through the same orchestrator for consistency
Three is the maximum number of entry points the orchestrator's API gateway can support efficiently, so the count reflects an infrastructure limit
These three entry points correspond to the three tiers of the product hierarchy (Class, Type, Product), aligning the activity entry with the product origin level
tip: The same Activity (e.g. APPLY-LATE-FEE) might be triggered nightly by COB, on-demand by an API call, or by a custom bank application detecting a missed payment. All three triggers should run the IDENTICAL Activity logic. By routing all three into the same orchestrator, the system guarantees this consistency — without each trigger needing its own implementation.

q: An asynchronous integration architecture is deliberately NOT shown in detail on the high-level diagram. The architect's stated reasoning is…
options: Async patterns are not yet finalised and showing speculative async flows would commit the team to decisions they haven't actually made yet, hurting credibility
Async sequence/orchestration is a separate detailed topic — high-level diagrams must stay readable; over-stuffing them with sequence detail defeats their purpose
Async patterns are confidential to the vendor's intellectual property and showing them in a customer-facing deck would breach internal disclosure policy
Async patterns require their own dedicated diagram tool (e.g. PlantUML) that doesn't integrate with C4 notation used elsewhere in the architecture deck
tip: Diagrams have a hierarchy of detail (C4: Context → Container → Component → Code). Putting sequence-level async detail on a Container-level diagram pollutes it for stakeholders who only need the high-level. Async details live in sequence diagrams (separately maintained, separately reviewed). Knowing which level each detail belongs to is part of architecture craftsmanship.

q: The architect mentions 'sequence diagrams are high-level and will be changed during specification'. What's the principled posture being communicated?
options: It signals to junior team members that experienced architects are not infallible, which is good mentoring practice in early-career development settings
Early sequence diagrams are HYPOTHESES — concrete implementation reveals details that shift them; premature commitment to detailed sequences risks anchoring on wrong assumptions
The team explicitly wants to charge change-orders for sequence refinements during implementation, and signalling 'will change' establishes that billing precedent
Sequence diagrams in implementation are auto-generated from code, so refinement happens automatically — the comment reflects tooling rather than design philosophy
tip: Detailed up-front sequence design feels rigorous but often anchors teams on assumptions that don't survive contact with implementation realities (latency, error modes, race conditions). High-level sequences capture INTENT — concrete sequences get refined when constraints emerge. This is part of mature iterative design: commit early to direction, defer detail commitment.

q: Collateral management is explicitly 'out of scope at the moment but we still need to aim for it'. The strategic intent is…
options: Collateral is fundamentally incompatible with the AA model and will require a separate system that's deliberately kept outside the CBS scope long-term
Phase Zero scope discipline excludes collateral, but the architecture must NOT preclude it — design now so adding collateral later is additive, not architecture-breaking
Collateral is regulated separately under CEMAC banking rules and falls under a different compliance regime that warrants a separate implementation program
Collateral has the highest licensing fees of any AA module from the vendor, so its inclusion would push the project budget beyond the bank's approved limit
tip: 'Out of scope but plan for' is a discipline that prevents scope creep WITHOUT closing future doors. Architects pick component boundaries, data models, and interfaces compatible with future collateral. The Phase Zero codebase doesn't ship collateral; the structure invites it cleanly later. Opposite to 'we'll figure it out later' which often forces painful retrofitting.

q: The Provision Model service is specifically 'used in COB for asset classification and provision calculation'. Why couple it tightly to COB rather than being on-demand?
options: Provision Model uses external risk-rating providers (e.g. Moody's) that are only accessible during off-business hours due to commercial contract restrictions
Provisioning is a daily/periodic regulatory exercise — running it inside COB (when balances are settled) ensures accurate inputs; running it ad-hoc during the day uses stale state
Provisioning calculations consume significant CPU that would impact online transaction throughput if run during business hours, so COB is the only feasible window
Provisioning results are required by IFRS as part of the EOD report bundle, so technically the engine could run anytime but must publish during COB
tip: Provisioning needs CONSISTENT inputs: each day's final balances, classifications, and exposures. During business hours those are moving targets. Running provisioning inside the COB pipeline (after balances settle, before reports run) means: same inputs every day, reproducible results, regulatory accuracy. On-demand provisioning during the day would produce 'snapshots' that don't match official EOD figures.

q: What does the 'Configuration module' specifically encompass per the transcript?
options: Application code (the executable parts of each microservice) plus their compiled binaries that get deployed to the Kubernetes nodes
System tables, system dates, calendars, parameterisation — the cross-cutting reference data many applications and the activity orchestration consume
Customer data (Party records and their associated identifiers) shared across applications for unified customer-view rendering on the BFF
Configuration UIs (Strapi-backed pages) that admins use to tune the system, distinct from the actual configuration data they edit there
tip: Configuration module = the single source of truth for reference / parametric data: DATES, HOLIDAY, CURRENCY, COUNTRY, system parameters. Every application that needs to know 'what date is it?' or 'is this a holiday?' or 'what's the rounding rule for KRW?' reads from this module. Centralising prevents drift (no app keeps a stale local copy) and makes regulatory updates atomic.

q: The External Event Publisher and Delivery Service are both 'outbound' but kept separate. The key difference is…
options: The External Event Publisher uses Kafka while the Delivery Service uses RabbitMQ, so they're kept separate to align with their respective message-bus origins

External Event Publisher is webhook-based (subscribers PULL their event types); Delivery Service is direct integration using templates (printing, SMS, email, Swift) — different consumers, different patterns
External Event Publisher handles internal events while Delivery Service handles external transactions, marking an internal/external responsibility boundary
The two were originally a single component but were split during refactoring to make each team's responsibility clearer in the org-chart structure
tip: Webhooks suit consumers who define their own subscription (an external app wanting to know when accounts open). Direct delivery suits well-known integrations (printers, SMS gateways, Swift networks) where templates and protocol-specific framing are needed. Putting them in one component would conflate event semantics with delivery semantics. Separate modules keep each concern focused.

q: FCM (Financial Crime Management) acts as a 'gateway' to external providers. Beyond just routing requests, what other benefits does the gateway pattern provide here?
options: Banking regulators in the target regions mandate the gateway pattern under their AML reporting frameworks, making it a compliance prerequisite
Consistent retry/timeout policy across providers, provider-swap capability, single audit point for compliance, and hiding provider variability from the rest of the system
The gateway pattern adds significant latency that's only acceptable for low-volume operations like AML screening, which is why it's used specifically for FCM
Gateway-style isolation is the only way to access FCM providers that require static IP whitelisting on the bank's egress traffic configuration
tip: Gateway > raw client because it provides architectural insulation. If callers used providers directly: every caller implements retry/timeout; provider changes ripple everywhere; compliance auditing scatters. Gateway centralises these cross-cutting concerns. The cost is one extra hop; the gain is operational and architectural sanity. Same pattern as the Configuration module — centralise concerns vs scatter them.

q: An architect would describe Strapi's choice over WordPress for headless CMS as…
options: Headless CMS systems are significantly cheaper to license than full CMS like WordPress, making Strapi the budget-friendly choice for the project

WordPress is traditional (CMS + frontend coupled); Strapi is HEADLESS (CMS just provides content/config via API). CBS frontend is React/Next — needs a backend-only CMS to feed it, not a tightly-coupled WordPress-style stack
WordPress doesn't support the required PostgreSQL backend, while Strapi does — so storage compatibility drives the choice between the two
WordPress lacks the multi-language support needed for French/English/Portuguese banking content, while Strapi handles all three natively out of the box
tip: Traditional CMS (WordPress) renders pages itself — the CMS IS the frontend. CBS frontend is already a React/Next app; we just need a configurable content/config source. Headless CMS exposes content via API — the React app fetches and renders. Strapi fits this 'API-only CMS' role. Using WordPress would mean fighting against its rendering model to get just the data layer.

q: The architect chose to NOT show ESB / message bus in detail because…
options: The message bus is part of the bank's existing infrastructure, so showing it would imply CBS-team ownership of something the bank already operates
It's still being decided (Kafka vs Kafka+RabbitMQ) and async integration is a separate diagram concern — showing tentative choices at high level would confuse stakeholders
Message bus details are confidential to the vendor's competitive positioning, so they're omitted from customer-facing decks for IP protection reasons
The message bus is conceptually obvious to banking architects and including it would add diagram clutter without conveying new information to the target audience
tip: Showing a tentative choice as if decided gives stakeholders false confidence. Architectural integrity: surface what's DECIDED on diagrams; flag what's IN DISCUSSION explicitly (yellow blocks in the deck). Stakeholders then know where to ask questions and where decisions are firm. 'Honest about state' is itself a design quality.

q: HashiCorp Vault is specifically chosen for 'internal certificate management for mutual TLS between services'. Why is mTLS between internal services treated as a security compliance requirement?
options: mTLS is significantly faster than alternative service-to-service security mechanisms like JWT tokens, so it's chosen for performance reasons primarily

Zero-trust security: in-cluster traffic must NOT be implicitly trusted — every service-to-service call must prove identity via certificate; without mTLS, a compromised pod gains free reach inside the cluster
Bank IT departments in the target regions only allow mTLS-secured communication on their internal networks, making it a network-policy requirement
mTLS is the only encryption method that meets the FIPS 140-2 standard required by the bank's encryption compliance officer for internal traffic
tip: Pre-zero-trust: cluster firewall = security perimeter. Get inside the cluster, you're trusted. Modern (and bank-regulator) view: assume the cluster IS the threat surface. mTLS means each service has a cryptographic identity; calls authenticate at the connection level. Vault rotates certificates so a compromised cert has short useful life. This converts a static perimeter into per-connection authentication.

q: Container Registry and CI/CD pipeline placement are marked as 'yellow' (under discussion). The actual decision depends on…
options: The decision will be made by the CBS team's lead architect once the design phase completes — it's a deferred technical choice rather than an external dependency
Bank's policy on whether external (CBS team) tools can deploy into their infrastructure — if bank restricts external access, registries/pipelines must live bank-side; if not, CBS-team-side is simpler
Container registry choice depends on whether the bank uses Docker or Podman as its container runtime, and the bank hasn't yet committed to one of the two
The decision waits on a procurement-side licensing comparison between Azure Container Registry, Harbor, and JFrog Artifactory to pick the most cost-effective vendor
tip: Highly-regulated banks may forbid third-party CI/CD touching production; they want everything within their managed boundary. Less-regulated environments are happy for the vendor team to push releases directly. The decision changes the entire deployment workflow. Hence: cannot finalise architecture until bank policy is known. This is the realistic shape of B2B banking architecture.

q: Two Kubernetes clusters, one per data centre. Why not one logical cluster spanning both?
options: Two clusters provide DOUBLE the throughput of one stretched cluster, so the choice is primarily a performance scaling decision rather than an architectural one

Spanning a single cluster across geographically separate data centres introduces latency, split-brain risk, and complicates failover — one-cluster-per-DC keeps each independently operable; replication happens at the data layer, not the orchestration layer
Kubernetes itself does not support cross-DC stretched clusters in production-supported configurations, so the architecture has no choice but to use two clusters
Two clusters allow the bank to use different Kubernetes distributions in each DC, which is required to satisfy the bank's multi-vendor strategy for resilience
tip: Stretched clusters across DCs face: high latency between control-plane components, hard split-brain scenarios on network partition, complex maintenance. Per-DC clusters: each DC's cluster is independently healthy; if DC1 fails, DC2's cluster keeps running. Replication / failover happens at databases (PostgreSQL replication) and event streams (Kafka mirroring). Cleaner failure modes; standard pattern for multi-DC banking.

q: ElasticSearch is chosen for logging AND can also host Jaeger for tracing. Why is this 'reusing one tool for two purposes' beneficial?
options: Combining logs and traces lets the team negotiate a better licensing deal with Elastic for combined volume rather than two separate licensing tiers

Same backend store, same indexing/scaling skills, same operational tooling — the team doesn't need expertise in two separate stacks (e.g. Splunk + Tempo) — plus search across logs+traces becomes trivial
ElasticSearch is the only logging-and-tracing engine that supports the encryption-at-rest standards required by the bank's compliance officer for both data types
Combining logs and traces is the Temenos best practice, and following it ensures the architecture remains aligned with the Temenos reference design for observability
tip: If logs are in Elastic but traces are in Tempo, correlating a trace ID with its surrounding logs requires cross-system search. Storing both in Elastic means: one query language, one tuning model, one capacity plan, one on-call playbook. The trade-off is Elastic must scale to both workloads — usually fine if sized right. Team skill economics often justify this consolidation.

q: Longhorn is chosen for block (blob-on-VM) storage specifically because of…
options: Longhorn is significantly cheaper than competing block-storage solutions when measured per-GB and at the scale the bank expects to deploy
Kubernetes context — Longhorn is Kubernetes-native; integrates with PVCs (Persistent Volume Claims) naturally; no external SAN required for stateful pods
Longhorn provides higher IOPS than competitors like Rook-Ceph at the same hardware footprint, making it the performance leader for this workload
Longhorn integrates uniquely with the bank's existing storage management software, which is required for the bank's tape-archive lifecycle policy
tip: Stateful pods in Kubernetes need persistent volumes that survive pod restarts and node failures. Longhorn provides distributed, replicated block storage backed by node-local disks — Kubernetes-native (no external storage vendor). Replication factor protects against node loss. The 'no external SAN required' is huge for bank deployments where bringing in extra storage hardware is a procurement project.

q: Filebeat is paired with Logstash for log shipping. What does each do?
options: Filebeat handles transport security and authentication while Logstash handles routing — splitting the security responsibility from routing keeps each layer focused
Filebeat = lightweight agent on each VM/pod that ships log lines; Logstash = central pipeline that parses, transforms, enriches, and routes them into ElasticSearch indices
Filebeat is used for application logs while Logstash is used for system logs — the split reflects the source of the log rather than processing responsibility
Filebeat operates on the legacy Payment Hub side of the infrastructure while Logstash operates on the new CBS side, so the split reflects organisational boundaries
tip: Filebeat is intentionally tiny — its job is to harvest log files and forward them, nothing else. Logstash sits centrally and does the heavy lifting: parsing log formats, enriching with metadata, dropping noise, routing to the right Elastic index. Splitting agent (Filebeat) from processor (Logstash) keeps each VM's footprint minimal while centralising complex parsing logic.

q: Jaeger vs Grafana Tempo for tracing — the architecture decision will be logged in an ADR. Why is a formal decision log valuable here?
options: ADRs are required by the bank's vendor governance framework before any architecturally significant choice can be approved by their procurement office
Future teams will ask 'why did we pick X?' months later — without ADRs, the reasoning is lost; ADRs capture decision + context + alternatives + consequences so the answer survives team turnover
ADRs provide audit-friendly diff outputs that show exactly which architectural choices changed across releases, simplifying the regulator's review process
ADRs serve as the legal record of decision authority — establishing who chose what and when, which is required for the bank's internal IT governance audit
tip: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) document not just WHAT was chosen but WHY — and what alternatives were considered. Without ADRs, six months later someone asks 'why Jaeger?' and the original architect has moved on; nobody knows. With ADRs, the rationale survives. They're cheap to write and pay back enormously when (not if) the question recurs.

q: The development VMs are deliberately bare-metal rather than cloud (e.g. Azure VMs). Why mirror production this closely in dev?
options: Bare metal VMs are required by South Sudan's banking regulations under the local-infrastructure-residency requirement which excludes cloud VMs entirely
Cloud VMs have different latency, networking, and IO profiles than bare metal — bugs that appear only on bare metal would never surface in cloud dev, then fail in production
Bare metal is the only way to run Longhorn's distributed block storage, which the production environment relies on for stateful pods
Bare metal lets the dev team install custom kernel modules required for low-level debugging that's not possible on cloud-managed VMs from any provider
tip: Classic 'works in dev, fails in prod' bugs often come from environment divergence. Cloud-vs-baremetal differences include: disk IO patterns, network virtualisation latency, CPU isolation, hardware-level reliability characteristics. Banking workloads are sensitive to these. Bare-metal dev costs more but catches issues at dev time — far cheaper than hunting them in production.

q: Bank-managed infrastructure includes an 'ICAP server for scanning' and 'quarantine storage for file uploads'. What does this protect against?
options: ICAP scanning is required by the bank's antivirus licensing agreement which mandates that all incoming file traffic pass through the ICAP-compliant scanner

Malicious file uploads — every uploaded document is intercepted by ICAP (Internet Content Adaptation Protocol) for AV/malware scanning; suspicious files go to quarantine instead of reaching CBS storage
ICAP scanning is the only protocol that supports the encryption-at-rest standards the bank requires for uploaded documents under their compliance policy
Quarantine storage exists to handle files that fail validation against the document-management framework's allowed-format list rather than security threats
tip: Customers upload IDs, statements, scans. Without scanning, a poisoned PDF could embed malware that triggers when bank staff open it — or worse, propagates. ICAP is the standard protocol for in-band content scanning. Files pass through ICAP → if clean, into CBS storage; if not, quarantine for forensics. This is bank-managed because content scanning policy/engines are typically the bank's IT-security responsibility.

q: Keycloak is configured to federate with the bank's Active Directory. The cleanest reason to federate (rather than syncing users) is…
options: Sync-based federation is forbidden by GDPR-equivalent regulations in the target regions, making delegation-based federation the only legally permitted option

AD remains the single source of truth — when an employee is deactivated in AD (e.g. termination), the CBS login fails immediately. Sync-based models have a lag window where deactivated employees can still log in
Federation reduces the bank's licensing cost by avoiding duplicate user counts in both AD and CBS, which is the dominant cost factor in the IAM architecture
Federation is required by the bank's IT department for political reasons — they refuse to allow CBS to own a parallel identity store independent of their AD
tip: Sync = periodic copy from AD into CBS user store. Window between deactivation and next sync = security exposure. Federation = CBS Keycloak DELEGATES authentication to AD live; every login checks AD's current state. Deactivation is immediate. For an institution where employee termination must instantly cut access, federation is the only safe pattern.

q: Bank-managed VMs are required from the bank's side. Why does the CBS team accept this dependency rather than provisioning their own?
options: VM management is technically simple but the bank's IT team enjoys having control over the infrastructure, so the bank-owned approach is a political concession to bank IT
Banks insist on owning physical/virtual infrastructure inside their datacentre (regulatory, sovereignty) — CBS team provisioning servers inside the bank's DC is usually not permitted
The CBS team lacks the on-site presence in target regions (CEMAC, UEMOA, South Sudan) to physically install and manage servers, so the bank does it by necessity
Bank-owned VMs are cheaper per-VM than CBS-managed VMs when measured on the bank's procurement framework, so the cost-optimisation favours bank ownership
tip: Banking regulators typically require infrastructure to be owned/operated by the regulated entity — the bank. Vendors aren't allowed to bring their own hardware into the bank's DC. So CBS deploys ON the bank's VMs. This isn't a technical choice — it's a regulatory boundary. Architecture must accept it cleanly: 'bank gives us VMs; we deploy ON them; we don't run our own'.

q: Object storage uses SeaweedFS for 'data lake'-like capability. The reason it can act as a data lake is…
options: SeaweedFS is the only S3-compatible storage approved by South Sudan's banking regulators for handling customer-facing financial document storage
SeaweedFS exposes S3-compatible API + scales horizontally + replicates across DCs — making it functionally equivalent to AWS S3 or Azure Blob for analytical/blob data needs
SeaweedFS was originally built by a banking consortium specifically for data-lake-style banking workloads, making it purpose-fit for this use case
SeaweedFS uses a unique columnar storage format that's optimised for analytical workloads, distinguishing it from competitors which use row-oriented storage
tip: Data lakes typically need: S3-compatible API (so any tool integrates), horizontal scaling (petabytes of data), replication (durability), eventual consistency tolerance. SeaweedFS provides all of these. The bank-on-premise version of a cloud data lake. Same usage patterns: unstructured/semi-structured data, ML/analytics processing, archive. Cloud-equivalence without leaving the bank's data centre.

q: Why is RabbitMQ described as 'might be added if patterns dictate' rather than committed up-front?
options: RabbitMQ's licensing is more expensive than Kafka and the project would breach budget if both were adopted simultaneously without staged rollout

Avoiding over-engineering — Kafka covers most needs (events, audit, fan-out); adding RabbitMQ doubles operational surface — commit only when a specific need (e.g. request-reply, priority queues) clearly demands it
RabbitMQ requires its own dedicated infrastructure team that the bank cannot provide in phase zero, so deferral is a staffing-pragmatic decision
RabbitMQ's existing presence in Payment Hub creates a dependency the CBS team wants to avoid — they prefer a clean Kafka-only architecture for new components
tip: Two message systems = two sets of clients, monitoring, scaling configs, ops playbooks. Worth it ONLY if Kafka genuinely can't cover a need. Most patterns (events, replay, fan-out) Kafka handles well. RabbitMQ wins at: priority queues, request-reply, dead-letter exchanges. Defer the addition until a real use case justifies the operational cost. Defaults matter: defaulting to LESS is usually wiser.

q: PostgreSQL replication for financial reporting uses 'eventual consistency'. Why is eventual consistency acceptable for reporting but unacceptable for transactions?
options: Eventual consistency is the only consistency model PostgreSQL replicas support, so the architecture has no choice but to accept it for all workloads

Reports are READ-mostly snapshots showing recent history (slight lag is OK); transactions are WRITES that MUST see canonical state to avoid double-spends — different consistency tolerances per workload
Reports run during off-business hours when no transactions are happening, so consistency is irrelevant for the reporting workload by design
PostgreSQL's eventual consistency mode is significantly faster for both reads and writes, making it the universally preferred consistency mode if the application can tolerate it
tip: A wire transfer reading a slightly stale balance could approve a double-spend. Eventual consistency = death for transactions. A report showing yesterday's totals as 'a few seconds late' is invisible to users. Mixing both on the same DB = compromise both. Splitting them (OLTP strong consistency; reporting eventual) optimises each workload. This is why architectures split read replicas from primaries.

q: The architect references 'OFS-type integrations' as one of the Application Orchestrator's use cases. OFS in Temenos terminology means…
options: Object File System — Temenos's distributed object storage protocol used by the Application Orchestrator's bulk-processing layer for handling large blob datasets
Open Financial Service — Temenos's integration API standard; the Application Orchestrator's dynamic query engine can serve OFS-like multi-application requests in CBS
Offline File Sync — Temenos's mechanism for handling disconnected branch operations, replicated in CBS through the Application Orchestrator's bulk-processing layer
Original Functional Spec — Temenos's terminology for the foundational application requirements document, referenced when designing custom applications
tip: Temenos OFS (Open Financial Service) is their canonical integration channel — XML-based requests that span multiple applications, with transformations, validations, etc. The Application Orchestrator must support similar patterns in CBS for external systems and integrations to compose requests across applications. Knowing this pedigree explains why the orchestrator specifically lists 'dynamic query engine + bulk processing + API composition' as its triad.

q: The 24/7 operation mechanism is described as 'switching the clocks'. The architectural prerequisite making this possible is…
options: Server clocks are NTP-synchronised across all data centres so 'today' is a global concept, and COB advances the synchronised global clock at the day boundary

Decoupling business date from server clock — transactions tag with the business date they belong to, COB advances the business date, and the system can simultaneously process 'today closing' and 'tomorrow opening'
Each data centre runs on its own clock so DC1 can serve 'today' while DC2 serves 'tomorrow' simultaneously, with cross-DC requests crossing the date boundary as needed
Operators manually advance the clock based on regional time-zone offsets, with each region running on a different clock-time setting calibrated to local hours
tip: If business date == server clock, COB requires a downtime window. Decoupling them lets the system accept incoming transactions tagged 'next business day' while COB processes 'current business day'. Functionally, the system serves two business days briefly. Customer experience: no downtime. The architectural cost: every transaction-touching component must respect the business date as a first-class concept.

q: Eric asks how a CBS that handles 24/7 can still produce a consistent EOD report. The technical answer is…
options: EOD reports are generated continuously throughout the day with each transaction adding to running totals, so the 'EOD' framing is conceptual rather than time-bounded

EOD reports use a 'cut-off' moment — transactions tagged with that day's business date are included; later transactions belong to the next business day and don't pollute the report — cut-off is logical, not physical-time-based
EOD reports are run only during a strict downtime window when no transactions are accepted, ensuring the report set is consistent without needing a cut-off concept
EOD reports include all transactions up to the wall-clock midnight in the bank's primary time zone, with cross-zone transactions being post-adjusted manually
tip: Without a logical cut-off, 'EOD' becomes ambiguous: does 23:59:30 count? What about 23:59:59 in another timezone? Logical cut-off (defined by business date, not wall clock) gives every transaction an unambiguous day. EOD report queries for 'all transactions where business_date = today' — clean criterion, deterministic result, regardless of when the transaction was physically posted.

q: If COB hits an error mid-run during 24/7 ops, how does the architecture handle 'next day' transactions already accepted?
options: All pending and forward-committed transactions are dropped during rollback so the system reverts to a clean known-good state before retrying COB from scratch
COB's rollback mechanism must preserve forward-committed transactions — they're already authorised under the new business date and must survive the rollback of the OLD business date's close
The architecture refuses to accept any next-day transactions until COB completes successfully, so the rollback scenario doesn't actually apply to forward-dated transactions
Forward-dated transactions are stored in a separate database from the closing day's transactions, so rollback of one database doesn't affect the other under any circumstances
tip: Naive rollback (undo everything since COB started) would invalidate accepted next-day transactions — losing customer work. The architecture must distinguish: 'closing day' work (rollbackable) vs 'next day' transactions (already valid, must persist). COB's rollback scope is its OWN work, not customer-facing transactions that have already crossed into the new business date. Non-trivial concurrency engineering.

q: Two data centres with eventual sync. If DC1 fails mid-transaction, the customer's transaction in DC2 might…
options: All pending writes are immediately replicated synchronously to DC2 before being acknowledged to the customer, so DC2 always has identical state to DC1 instantly
See a slightly stale view of state from DC1 — eventual sync means the latest DC1 state may not have replicated yet — bank tolerates this for read consistency; writes serialise to a designated primary
The transaction is fully blocked at the bank's gateway until DC1 recovers, since no other DC can serve writes for the customer's account during a primary failure
DC2 takes over with potentially stale state but the system runs a reconciliation job afterwards to detect and reverse any duplicated writes within the lag window
tip: Eventual sync = there's a replication lag window. If DC1 had just written and lag was 2s when it failed, DC2 doesn't yet have those 2s of writes. To prevent double-spend, writes typically pin to a primary DC; reads can be served eventually-consistently from secondary. DR failover then promotes DC2 to primary, accepts that any in-flight DC1 writes within the lag window are lost (which the bank manages with retry+idempotency).

q: Why does the architect specifically split 'object storage management' (SeaweedFS+Postgres) from 'blob storage management' (Longhorn)?
options: Object and block storage actually use the same underlying SeaweedFS cluster — Longhorn is just a frontend interface that exposes block semantics over SeaweedFS

Different use cases: object storage = S3-style buckets for documents/data-lake (high volume, scale-out); blob storage on Longhorn = persistent volumes for stateful pods (databases, message brokers needing block IO)
Object storage uses the bank's existing storage hardware while block storage requires CBS-team-managed hardware, marking an infrastructure-ownership boundary
SeaweedFS handles development workloads while Longhorn handles production workloads — the split reflects the dev-vs-prod boundary in the architecture
tip: Object storage: customer documents, archives, ML data — accessed via S3 API, eventually consistent, scaled by volume. Block/persistent volumes: a PostgreSQL primary's data files — accessed via filesystem, strict consistency, scaled by IOPS. They're fundamentally different storage classes. Using one for the other (S3 for a DB file or block storage for blob archive) would be a serious anti-pattern.

q: Why is financial reporting designed as primarily ASYNCHRONOUS rather than running synchronously when requested?
options: Async reports use a different data format (Parquet) than sync reports (CSV), which makes them suitable for the analytical query patterns reporting needs

Reports may scan huge data volumes; running them sync blocks the report requester AND loads the OLTP system (or its replica) — async means data flows into the reporting store continuously, reports query that store independently of OLTP
Sync reports require dedicated reporting connections from the OLTP DB which would exceed PostgreSQL's connection limits at the bank's transaction volume
Reports are mostly generated during off-peak hours, so async is the natural fit for the temporal pattern in which they're consumed by the reporting team
tip: A sync report against 50M transactions = minutes of waiting AND heavy OLTP load. Async: events flow from OLTP into the reporting store (via Kafka). Reports query the reporting store. OLTP stays fast; report generation can scale separately; users don't wait. The async coupling is via event streams — natural fit for Kafka's persistent log.

q: Andre says 'gathering information will be asynchronous, mostly from the accounting service'. The 'mostly' caveat hints at…
options: Hedge against vendor lock-in — keeping a non-accounting data source ensures the bank can swap accounting services without breaking reporting capability
Some reports need data from non-accounting services (Customer, Limits, Activity History) — the financial reporting layer composes across services via async events from each
Some reports are generated by Payment Hub directly without involving CBS at all, and 'mostly' reflects the partial coverage CBS reporting provides for those flows
The accounting service handles only synchronous postings while other services handle async — 'mostly' refers to the async/sync split rather than data source diversity
tip: Pure-accounting reports (balance sheet, P&L) come from STMT.ENTRY / CATEG.ENTRY. But regulatory reports often need: customer attributes (Sector, Country) from Customer service, limit info from Limits, activity history. The reporting layer subscribes to events from each — composing a complete reporting view. 'Mostly accounting' acknowledges this composition without burying the lead.

q: Onboarding a new country = onboarding new report types. Why is this not a 'one report engine' issue but a 'per-country compliance' issue?
options: The reporting engine is region-locked at build time so each region requires a different build of the reporting binary tailored to that region's compliance rules

Each country's regulator demands specific report formats, fields, frequencies — the reporting engine is configurable enough to produce them, but each new country requires its own report definitions and possibly compliance attestation
New countries require new reporting databases because each country's reports cannot legally cross national borders even within the same banking group
The reporting engine uses different output formats per country, so each country brings a new template format that the engine needs upfront to render correctly
tip: A regulator-mandated report has VERY specific requirements: exact field set, exact format, exact filing cadence. The engine doesn't ship knowing every country's rules; it ships flexible enough to be configured for any. Onboarding South Sudan = configuring its specific reports + getting them signed off by compliance. Engine = reusable; per-country rules = bespoke work. Honest scoping.

q: Why is financial reporting hosted across multiple DCs (not single-DC like, say, a teller terminal)?
options: Reporting requires significantly more storage than other components, and distributing it across DCs is the only way to fit within each DC's storage budget
Reporting is regulatory-critical — missing a regulatory deadline due to single-DC outage = compliance breach; multi-DC reporting ensures reports can be generated even if one DC fails
Reporting is computationally expensive and spreading it across DCs takes advantage of compute capacity in each, balancing the load efficiently
Reports must be generated in the DC closest to the regulator's office to meet network-latency SLAs on the regulator's API endpoints for report submission
tip: Regulators have deadlines (e.g. submit X report by 9am on the 15th of the month). Missing them carries fines or sanction. Hosting reporting in a single DC = single point of failure for compliance. Multi-DC reporting: even if a DC fails on the 14th, reports still generate on the 15th from the surviving DC. The data is eventually consistent (acceptable for reporting); availability is non-optional.

q: Eric asks about handling DATA LAKE-style reporting needs. The architecture's answer is essentially…
options: Build a dedicated Apache Hadoop cluster alongside the CBS infrastructure to act as the data lake, since CBS itself is not designed for analytical workloads
SeaweedFS object storage + accounting service events + reporting service together fulfil data-lake-style needs without a separate explicit 'data lake' product
Purchase a vendor data lake product (e.g. Snowflake) and replicate CBS data into it via nightly ETL, treating the data lake as a separate external system
Use the bank's existing analytics database directly, which is already integrated with CBS via the bank's data warehouse team's pipeline infrastructure
tip: A 'data lake' isn't a product — it's a pattern: store all data, structured and unstructured, queryable in flexible ways. SeaweedFS handles the blob side (documents, exports). Accounting service emits structured events into Kafka, landing in the reporting store. Together they satisfy 'data lake' needs. Banks don't always need a separate explicit DL stack.

q: Eric ties financial reporting to South Sudan specifically. Why is per-region report scoping more than just 'pick currency and language'?
options: Currency and language are the primary regional concerns and configuring them is a couple of hours of work per region, making scoping straightforward
Each region has unique compliance requirements: data retention, formats, mandatory fields, reporting frequency, regulator-specific filing channels — far beyond UI localisation
Regional compliance is primarily a documentation exercise where the bank's compliance officer attests that the system meets local rules without architectural changes
Regional differences amount to time zone configuration in the DATES table and a few currency-table entries — the rest of the architecture is region-agnostic
tip: UI localisation (French menus) is trivial. Compliance per region is profound: South Sudan's 10-year retention; CEMAC's specific report format; UEMOA's particular templates. Each demands specific FIELDS, FREQUENCIES, FORMATS, sometimes specific filing endpoints. Reporting engine must be flexible enough to produce them all — and configuration must be region-specific. 'Pick a language' would be wildly under-scoped.

q: Why does the architecture introduce an 'Application Service' inside the registry, alongside the orchestrator?
options: The Application Service handles UI rendering while the Application Orchestrator handles business logic, splitting the presentation and logic layers cleanly
Orchestrator EXECUTES; Application Service is the CATALOG of registered applications, knowing how to communicate with each — separating registry-of-apps from execute-engine is clean separation
The two components are deployed to different data centres for redundancy reasons, marking a physical-deployment boundary rather than a logical one
The Application Service handles the bank-built custom applications while the Orchestrator handles the CBS-team-built applications, reflecting build-source ownership
tip: Conceptually: 'WHAT apps exist + how to call them' (Application Service) vs 'EXECUTE this composition across apps' (Application Orchestrator). Same as how kubelet (executes) is separate from API server (knows what should run). Singular responsibility per component; testability; cleaner ops. Lumping them = one large component with tangled responsibilities.

q: Why is Action listed as a separately registered concept alongside Activity, Arrangement, etc., when Actions are 'just' building blocks?
options: Actions need to be tracked separately for licensing purposes — the CBS vendor charges per Action invocation rather than per Activity, requiring action-level registration
Actions are REUSABLE across many contexts — registering them as first-class lets multiple Activities, Activities in different products, and even bank-custom code all reuse them without duplication
Actions are slower-running than Activities and benefit from a dedicated execution pool to avoid blocking Activity dispatch, so they're registered separately
Actions are subject to different security policies than Activities because they're more granular, requiring fine-grained access control at the action level
tip: If Actions lived inside Activities, reusing 'validate balance' across 10 activities = 10 copies or weird sub-imports. Registering Actions in their own namespace lets ANY Activity, custom application, or even a Plugin invoke them by name. Same Action; many callers; no duplication. The catalogue style scales as the platform matures.

q: The transfer service 'encapsulates every money movement to be secure'. Why centralise money movement here rather than per-product?
options: Money movement is governed by SOX-style separation-of-duties rules, which forbid embedding money-movement logic inside the modules that initiate transfers

Money movement is the highest-risk operation in the bank — centralising it means one battle-tested code path, one audit point, consistent fraud checks, consistent regulatory hooks — all products move money the same canonical way
Money movement is the slowest operation in the system and centralising it allows the team to apply maximum optimisation efforts to a single component
Money movement uses cryptographic primitives that are export-controlled, and centralising them simplifies the bank's export-control compliance documentation
tip: If loans, deposits, cards, FT all moved money their own way: fraud screens scattered; AML hooks inconsistent; audit trails uneven; bugs in one product don't get fixed in others. Centralising in Transfer service: one canonical mover; everyone uses it; one place to add a screen, one place to fix a bug. Same principle as the FCM gateway — high-risk concerns belong in shared, audited surfaces.

q: Andre says the high-level architecture diagram 'may change during deep dive'. The healthy interpretation of this is…
options: The architect lacks confidence in the chosen high-level design and is hedging by explicitly building in the option to change later if it doesn't work
High-level design is a HYPOTHESIS to be refined by detailed analysis — committing to never changing it would mean detailed analysis can't surface needed refinements — honest expectation-setting
The bank's procurement framework requires architects to commit to flexibility on high-level design as a buyer-protection term in the contract
Banking architectures are required by regulators to be flexible to accommodate future rule changes, so the architect signals compliance with that expectation
tip: Pretending the high-level design is final blocks legitimate refinement. Architects who commit too early either: refuse needed changes (rigid), or change quietly (eroding trust). Saying upfront 'this WILL refine' sets a healthy expectation. Stakeholders understand that decisions evolve; refinements are tracked (ADRs); the system improves. Mature posture.

q: The transcript reveals 'Architecture Decision Log' (ADL) will be maintained. Why is the existence of an ADL itself an architectural quality?
options: ADLs are required by the bank's IT-governance framework as a buyer-protection mechanism in case the project needs to be audited mid-stream
An ADL externalises decision rationale — making the architecture's reasoning available to future maintainers, auditors, and regulators — without ADL, the architecture is opaque
ADLs are produced as a billable deliverable in the project contract, so maintaining one is contractually required regardless of its actual utility
ADLs are searchable via the bank's Confluence search index, which makes them useful for engineers looking for examples of similar decisions in prior projects
tip: Architecture that can't explain itself is fragile: future changes risk violating unstated assumptions. ADLs make the implicit explicit. Regulators love them ('show me how you decided X'). New team members ramp faster ('I see now why we use Strapi'). Defending against challenges is easier ('here's the ADR that captures the trade-off'). An ADL is itself a deliverable, not just paperwork.

q: The architect references 'OFS-type Temenos-like integrations' to explain the Application Orchestrator's dynamic query needs. The takeaway about being Temenos-inspired is…
options: The CBS implementation will literally copy Temenos's OFS code into the new system, since rewriting it from scratch isn't justified given Temenos's working implementation
Taking Temenos's PROVEN integration shapes as targets — not as code to copy, but as patterns the CBS Application Orchestrator must support to satisfy real-world bank integration needs
OFS is the only integration pattern Temenos customers know, so CBS adopts it primarily as a marketing alignment to ease the Temenos-to-CBS migration story
OFS is a documented industry standard that all core-banking systems must support, so the CBS team builds it as a baseline requirement rather than a derived choice
tip: Banks integrating with CBS will demand patterns they already understand: OFS-style multi-app queries, bulk operations, composition. CBS supporting these patterns reduces integration friction. The CBS implementation is greenfield (not Temenos code); the INTERFACE SHAPE benefits from being Temenos-similar so partners can reuse their playbooks. Pragmatic inheritance of patterns without inheriting legacy.

q: Why does the architect explicitly mention that the BFF won't grow into a 'data joiner' for the core CBS API (avoiding the legacy mistake)?
options: Legacy BFFs that grew into data joiners had memory leaks and performance issues that the team wants to explicitly avoid in CBS by mentioning the concern upfront

Legacy BFFs that absorb data-joining become performance bottlenecks (queries across multiple DBs, merging in memory) — keeping BFF as a thin frontend-view-optimiser means joining stays in the data layer where it's optimised
Legacy BFFs were the source of most security incidents in Payment Hub, and the architect wants to flag this as a known anti-pattern to avoid in CBS
Legacy BFFs were licensed per data-source connection, making the cost-per-BFF grow as new data sources were added — CBS architecture avoids this licensing pattern
tip: Legacy BFFs evolved into data-stitching layers because it was easy: 'just merge these two services' becomes 'just merge ten'. Each merge in BFF memory is slow, brittle, and unable to leverage DB-level optimisation. The architectural posture: BFF formats / optimises VIEW; joins happen IN the data layer (via Application Orchestrator's query engine). Hard-won lesson from many legacy systems.

q: Why does the architecture FORBID asynchronous integration between core services? Wait — does it?
options: Yes — all inter-service communication must be synchronous to maintain ACID transaction guarantees across the platform, which is a hard architectural constraint
No — async IS used between services (via Kafka); the architect just deferred showing async detail at high level because it's a separate diagram layer — the system is intentionally async-friendly
Mostly — async is allowed but discouraged in favour of synchronous patterns for clarity, and most production paths use synchronous calls between services
No — async is forbidden because of the bank's audit requirement for immediate trace correlation; all paths must be synchronous for clean audit chains
tip: Misreading the transcript: 'no asynchronous integrations yet described here because it's already a pretty big diagram' = 'we deferred showing them on THIS diagram'. NOT 'we don't have async'. Modern microservice architectures rely heavily on async for resilience and scale. CBS is no exception. The high-level diagram leaves async to sequence diagrams for clarity.

q: Andrew references the production target as 'virtual machines, NOT EKS/Kubernetes-Engine PaaS'. The reason for not using a managed K8s service is…
options: Managed Kubernetes services like EKS would add too much vendor lock-in to a specific cloud provider, which the architecture explicitly avoids for portability reasons

Banks deploy on-prem (or in their own DC); managed Kubernetes services (EKS/AKS/GKE) require cloud infrastructure the bank may not have, or sovereignty issues — self-managed K8s on bank VMs is the lowest-friction path
Managed Kubernetes services charge per-node fees that exceed the cost of self-managed at the bank's expected scale, making the cost-optimisation favour self-managed
Managed Kubernetes services don't yet support the specific Kubernetes version (1.30+) the architecture requires for some of the newer container features
tip: Managed K8s services tie you to a cloud provider; banks often can't use them due to sovereignty, regulatory, or pure 'we own our DC' posture. Self-hosting K8s (RKE2, Rancher) on the bank's bare-metal VMs is portable, sovereignty-friendly, and works in any environment. Trade-off: bank/CBS team operates the cluster rather than offloading to AWS/GCP. Acceptable for the multi-region, multi-bank deployment model.
